> I am still trying to get my head around why other top end athletes haven't had success with this approach. Seems there is plenty of athletes that have given Keto adapt performance a long term experience. And it isn't embraced even where the money flows if you know what I mean. Athletes will do almost anything, risking harmful health consequences and death for victory and fame but you don't hear of any going the PPP route.
>
> I see now Ben Greenfield is giving it a true try rather than just a lower carb approach which is good to see. He is a pretty smart guy and athlete that walks his talk and does his research and education.
>
> Thanks for your thoughts..... I am still pushing forward with my approach to the PPP path and "Quilt" for heath and performance.

Yes, when all my dots are connected and harmonized ....

others don't ever achieve this or understand how.... and a lot of it can be down to practicalities ... plus we're still in unchartered territory

a previous post I wrote explains this a bit more......

The findings so far are flawed for 2 reasons:

Reason 1: time. Adaptations take time. The studies to date have not lasted much more than 8-12 weeks. We need 24-36month studies to really see the changes.

Reason 2a: Athletes are not fully Fasted State Adapted

Reason 2b: Athletes are not Cold Adapted

Okay, let me try to explain this.

If you look at the affects of prolonged exercise and then marry this with depleted training you will see the following

It stimulates AMPk

\- increases fat oxidation

\- increases mitochondrial biogensis

It stimulates Sirt-1, PGC-1 and PPAR

\- increase fat oxidation

It upregulates CPT-1, CAT-1, Citrate Synthase or other fat oxidative enzymes

\- increase fat oxidation

It increases BAT formation and UCP-3

\- increase fat oxidation

\- increase REE

It increases IL-6 with a postive feedback loop

\- more fat oxidation

\- reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8594005](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8594005)

It upregulates protein synthesis

\- improved recovery and training adaptations

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/p0n17115736tt254/](http://www.springerlink.com/content/p0n17115736tt254/)

Size and Use of IMTG improves

\- improves fuel efficiency and RER

What all this does collectivity is improve practically every energy production pathway in the cell. Beta oxidation goes up, Ketosis upregulates, Cori-Cycle improves, Oxoacetate ramps up, Ketogenic Amino Acids get used more efficiently , Acetly-CoA Dehydrogenase is stimulated.

Tie this in then with improved thyroid function, increased insulin sensitivity, natural calorie restriction, upregulation of endogenous antioxidants and the big one - Epigenetics... and what do you get ?? a lean mean fat burning machine !!

And this all happens as a result of exercise, particularly prolonged endurance, just pubmed any of the above and you will see.

What I;m doing is accelerating all that by the Fasted State Training I am doing. This hasn't been studied. They have studied people doing 30mins or 1 hour fasted. And they've done this over a few weeks, a couple of times. I'm doing 3hr runs completely fasted with nothing during and 5hr bike rides fasted with minimal intake during. I'm running ultramarathons fasted , 50/100mile events, 8-24hrs in duration, fasted (although I do consume fats/protein and carbs during these races). Still, I'm doing stuff that no one has studied yet. And I understand the science (most of it, not all !). And I'm living it and I know others like me.

My sport is perfect for fat adaptation though. Its sub max. You cannot run that far at a high intensity. The big question is whether you can perform at max effort in a fully keto adapted state and compete at a world class/olympic level ? Thats where the grey area is for me at the moment. I think sprinters, middle distance runners, elite triathletes etc, where max efforts are required, would struggle with relying on ketones and fatty acids. Would Fasted State Training allow them to ?? I think so, I am close to it myself. Do we have any studies on this ? no. Like I said, no-one has looked at it yet. Due to the time required and the changes needed to the training regime of an elite athlete, we will never see a study like this done. I work with some world class athetes and I have been slowly implementing my strategies and they appear to be working. Thats all I can say for now.

Finally, this brings us back to CT. If you read all of Jacks blogs (a tough task I know!!), you will see that practically every adaptation he talks about related to the cold occurs via fasted state endurance training.

As the majority of these responses are linked to leptin, thats where I have an issue with it in terms of athletes. Most athletes have very low body fat levels. I've tested hundreds of them and rarely do they go above 10%. Leptin production simple does not happen (or at least it is extremely minimal) is when the individual has low body fat. So cold can't trigger leptin for athletes if they don't have any !! Okay, so cold helps GH and steriod receptors so thats where it might be beneficial. However, all of the other adaptations via leptin cannot happen.

I've probably left out a lot more than I know but its hard to put everything I've looked at down here in a few words.

Time is the key.

Fasted State Training is the master upregulator of fat adaptation.

Cold Therapy is like FST in terms of fat adaptation and has other benefits but potentially not for athletes.

Grey area's are max effort ATP production via fat oxidation. Also, lack of insulin on a permanent basis is not good for athletes either. I won't go into it here but there are serious practicality issues if you are a full time athlete that wants to keto adapt.

However, FST and CT still have to be optimised for athletic development. I don't have all the answers thats why I am sharing this info. Saying that it doesn't work when we are really only at the tip of the ice-berg is foolish

### Barry — 2013-06-13T14:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88023>

Race Update:

Malandrino del Trail, 70km, 5000m elevation gain, Tuscany.

Race start: Midnight, 12am

Day of Race: 3 meals, keto/HFLC, last meal 7pm

Result: Finished 12th in 10.5hrs

Not my best race for a few reasons. Primarily I would say its nothing related to keto/fitness etc, simply due to course and length. A race taking 20hrs is where I do well. It might sound strange to most, but a 10hr race is short for me... and I lose ground to the slightly "faster" guys.

Secondly, starting a race at this time if endogenous clock/timing is all in sync normally, is not the obviously conducive to optimal performance. Basically, I suffered for the first 5hrs of the race, the circadian signalling was messed up and it affected me big time. It wasn't until the first sign of daylight did I start to feel "normal". So it was a tough battle. However, another journey completed and more knowledge gained :

"Struggling and suffering are the essence of a life worth living. If you're not pushing yourself beyond the comfort zone, if you're not demanding more from yourself - expanding and learning as you go - you're choosing a numb existence. You're denying yourself an extraordinary trip."

.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-13T19:22:21+0300

Thanks for clearing up the confusion.

### Barry — 2013-06-14T01:05:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88054>

nothing is ever black and white...... don't think that going HFLC and taking a few grammes of d-Ribose will suddenly make you superhuman

first go back to Biochemistry 101.... does the PPP actually exist much in the muscle cell ? Does supplemental d-ribose actually work ?? Does the ATP production of the PPP actually meet anything near the requirements of your sport ? again, everything has to be put in context...

and always question everything.... the winner of my race was at the pasta party before the race.....

there are still a lot of grey areas here.... and unless you're in the trenches... you're not going to be able to see whats actually happening..

### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-20T11:05:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88482>

Hi there people!

I've been following this thread for several weeks and today decided to write something down hehe.

I'm very interested in everything that you've been discussing and I'm willing to see how could it be to be able to unleash the PPP pathway.

I'm an endurance athlete also. I compete in events from the 5K to the Marathon (just did two marathons in 4 years of running) but my 90% typical event is the 10K. I've got a PB of 35:24 on the 10K but just once, and a 2:47 on the Marathon. Not edge-cutting but considering that 10 years ago I was a fat guy who couldn't have run 5 minutes at any avg speed at all... it's a great achievement for me.

All other results in the 10K are between the 36'-37' but last year I struggled a lot and came down to 38' high as I became anaemic and developed what I think it's adrenal fatigue due to the combo of hard training (6-7 days per week) with a low-fat ovo-lacto-vegetarian diet... I say \*I think\* because no conventional doc I have seen until now seems to make the connection but from my labs and everything I experimented I do really think this is my issue.

I always tried to supplement and deal with recovery the best I could and incorpored easy runs at recovery pace, etc but even like this I couldn't be helped I got worn out. I've been reading many of JK stuff (still lot to read) and many many other sites/places and now I understand what I've been doing wrong. Right now it has been almost 6 months into the epi-paleo template with leptin reset included and some CT, grounding and sunbathing plus recommended supps and I feel like things have improved a lot for me but I doubt I'm still fully recovered.

So right now I've cut back my running training absolutely since last race I did two weeks ago and I'm just focusing in my strenght training (I also like bodybuilding) 3-4 times per week and not until failure, trying to maintain muscle but not pushing too far, I'm all into the recovery thing.

But, even If I recover well and keep with the epi-paleo template and all possible stuff to mitigate the burn-out I'm eager to think right now that this is a good life-style... most people suggest that the carb intake should fill the needs of an endurance athlete, but I know from what Jack posted and everything I read that If I go the carb route I'll back-fire ROS generation and stem cell depletion that will reduce my life-span dratically and put me into a constant high cortisol and pregnenolone steal status.

That's why I even have considered quitting endurance running. But... if I could just unleash the PPP pathway and mitigate the bad sides of the high ROS generation from carb sustained endurance... I'd be able to stick with running which has bring also a lot of positive things into my life (a really nice circle of people, I rule a forum about endurance running right now, etc...)

I've also been reading Ben Greenfield's stuff and heard the podcast with Jack... my labs look pretty much like him's maybe just a little bit worse, so you can have an idea where I'm right now.

I've an entry into the forums If anyone wants to give a quick look about me...
[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?7216-Albert-s-Story-and-Journey-to-optimal](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?7216-Albert-s-Story-and-Journey-to-optimal)!

Right now I'm not running but I plan to start again maybe in a month or so when I consider I've had plenty of rest and I'd like to introduce some of the concepts Barry has been talking about like fasted state training, recovery without fast-carbs... etc. I'm also considering to up my keto-adaptation. I've been moderate carb for a long time right now (I guess a year now) but not fully keto adapted, I didn't have as much high fat and almost upped my protein and averaged 100-150 grams of carbs per day... good for avg person but not enought to reap the benefits of ketosis. Now I'm reading Volek and Phinney's book and planning to do some of the things outlined there in combination with JK protocols, what I learn from here, BG... etc etc

What I think is that I'm on the worst side of all possible because my endurance events are very demanding of the glycolithic pathways as I'm running very near or slightly above the lactate threshold. I think that's why I might have seen good results this year in the Marathon (2:47:58 and the year before 2:56 on higher carb) but not so much in the 10K...

Well, lets start experimenting with all that stuff!

### Barry — 2013-06-20T17:55:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88497>

I really don't think people should be getting too caught up in "chasing" this PP pathway. There seems to be a trend with modern health and fitness of chasing something you cannot have.

Apart from that, there needs to be some serious research done on athletes, of different types and genders and context, before we can really ascertain whether this pathway actually exists and functions.

Finally, and probably more importantly, there is no real point in putting the spoiler on a toyota corolla. You are better off working towards getting the BMW or Mercedes first, then maybe consider the spoiler.

### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-21T00:10:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88533>

Well, I don't really know if there's a PPP or whatever, but what I know is that I cannot keep like I've been doing untill now because it has screwed me up so bad. And not only me, I see people surrounding me that is following the same patterns and ending all like the same.

The thing could be sumarized like this: they discover running, train 3 to 4 days a week, do fine, get hyped by the PB fever, then reach a stall, decide to engage in more demanding plans, they beat PB again... stall, new plan.. every time less recovery and push more harder... all this with a low fat, high carb diet based on "healthy" grains and some protein from any available source... and then with time they start to feel awfull, tired... but since we have put already so much effort and will in this we keep training and pushing through all kind off odds and some days feel better some days worse until one day you feel always bad... "fortunately" some people get injured first from running and are forced to have a break, some like me which don't get injuries from running can be training for a long time almost 6-7 days a week with high mileages with interval training, recovery runs, temps and long runs on weekend... and there you have it, insane stress to the body that ends badly.

MeghanK I'm sorry I don't have an answer to your question, I don't even know if it will be safe for myself... but the thing is that something has to change. I guess we'll only know by trying... It's really discouraging because it seems like that where we are right now we only have two options: embrace the idea that keto-training might aid in recovery and allow for both health and competing (in the line of Volek & Phinney's work) or accept that there cannot be performance in a low carb diet and then that means that we should probably go even more high in carbs because if we have ended like this maybe we suffered from too much glucose deficiency, typical CW-traditional thinking that too much effort and too few substrate to repair lead to overtraining.

From JK's work and others I understand that I might support high-performance with carbs (and this has been studied and backed up by science) but that comes with a price that shows in a reduced lifespan due to the cascade of ageing effects it puts to the body. It's something that somehow I thought by myself many times that there should be something wrong with people doing 'healthy' lifestyle training and enjoying sport while eating 'well' but then everyone seems to be getting injured or blown up in the long run... so I had an epiphany connecting dots with when I was a child and I didn't do any sport and eat only crap, then when doing bodybuidling and eating clean... and then I saw something: I realized carbs are like the fast-forward button on the tv commander, they speed up things. You wanna muscle mass? eat carbs. You wanna put weight? eat carbs also. You wanna life less? Feed any cancer? eat carbs.

All the thing that keto diet and calorie restriction improve lifespan, etc... I can somehow understand, and with JK's blogs I think now it makes a lot more of sense. So right now I'm pretty sold into the low carb thing but the question is: can we get performance at sports that are performed outside the aerobic effort zone? And can we do it safely? Maybe what we are trying to do... we're just not supposed to?

BTW I was going to suggest you listen to one of BG podcasts where a girl asks him your exact same case but I've seen someone already told you in another thread in this forum

### Dextery — 2013-06-21T02:27:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88539>

I remember back a couple years ago on paleohacks, Dr Kruse indicated that endurance athletes were very prone to tearning down their bodies leading to early death. He was poo pooed by the establishment there as being a whack job.

I began looking around and found a lot of elite athletes who met their demise much earlier than they should have.

One was Jim Fixx, author and lecturer, died of a massive heart attack. Age 52
The autopsy showed:
Mr. Fixx's left circumflex coronary artery was almost totally blocked; only trickles of blood could flow through the pinholes that were left of the inside of that artery. About 80 percent of the blood flow in the right coronary artery was blocked. The chief nourishment to Mr. Fixx's heart came from blood flowing through the third artery, the left anterior descending, which was less severely affected. Nevertheless, half that artery was blocked in places.

There was additional arteriosclerotic damage to a portion of Mr. Fixx's aorta and the arteries in his legs, but no blockage. The disease spared the arteries that fed his brain.
[http://www.nytimes.com/1984/07/24/s...d-james-fixx-the-enigma-of-heart-disease.html](http://www.nytimes.com/1984/07/24/science/the-doctor-s-world-james-fixx-the-enigma-of-heart-disease.html)

The 2nd person I read about was Norway's Greta Waitz, winner of the LA marathon, died of cancer at age of 57.
[http://www.foxnews.com/sports/2011/04/19/marathon-legend-grete-waitz-dies-battle-cancer/](http://www.foxnews.com/sports/2011/04/19/marathon-legend-grete-waitz-dies-battle-cancer/)

Back during the careers of these athletes, no one ever talked anything but carb loading. The word of the day was fat is bad.

Elite athletes in cycling have had their hearts scanned with some kind of imaging that shows tremendous scarring of the heart muscle on the inside...leaving it less pliable to move with the attached arteries.

Art DeVany has been beating this drum for years.

How can elite athletes that push their bodies to the limit not buy into seeking the PPP?

### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-21T08:37:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88560>

**Dextery** great stuff. One think I wonder is if these athletes were 'legit' and didn't abuse of any kind of 'aid' for performance.. that could mess them up a lot, in the bodybuilding scene this happens pretty often also... before pointing out that just endurance competition brought them down it'd be nice to put everything over the table, you know how many variables can dramatically affect these things... not saying that you might have a point in that, indeed I think it's the most likely, just trying to understand 100% of the context...

The curious thing is that if you google something like 'telomere lenght and endurance exercise" and click at the google link that only shows you research papers... almost 99% state that endurance sports positively impact telomere length and that's why most people think that endurance sports are healthy! That puzzled me a lot, there seems to be no evidence that stem cells get wasted but instead they say it helps generating more! Well, you could say: the people on the studies might be recreational athletes that exercised in a moderate fashion so they really ripped the benefits of moderate, hormetic exercise... well, I'm not sure about that, most people, even the aficionado ones we tend to overtrain more easily that undertrain... so it really puzzled me. But there's also research showing that too much oxidative stress is bad... so I guess there must be something they don't grasp in the telomere thing and JK stuff really seems to have the right point at my eyes right now.

**MeghanK**... I can really understand you. I'm just a middle-of-the-pack recreational runner but I also somehow made out of this a living lifestyle and have now plenty of friends that are into this, I can somehow understand how you feel although I also undestand that your situation is even worse than mine because you have took it to another stage and been near to qualify for the Olympics... that makes your situation a lot more frustrating because you got higher and because of this have even higher aspirations... so I can really understand what you say about sacrificing lifespan to live your dreams... I also agree with **caroline** that as you grow older you start seeing things differently... but achieving both things would be awesome, that's why we need to sort something out, we're some folks here right now seeking for this, I think we can work toghether to see how can we sort it out, even if this means there's no PPP that can sustain us... for me it would also be worth knowing... whataver the outcome..

**caroline** I find it great that you gravitate to young people... that shows you're a young soul and still have illusion to pursue goals and dreams! as we get older most we start forgetting about our dreams and the joy of living and just live life as the society expect us as adults... work, bring up the children, work more, sacrifice lifestyle and joy for commodity, work more... and die. We live half dead already and die without maybe having reached a plentiful life... just to follow the mainstream... well I guess some might find happiness in that, it depends a lot, actually...

### Albert83BCN — 2013-06-21T16:41:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88583>

BTW, I got to a Lyle McDonald article on this issue today, just wanted to share:
[http://www.bodyrecomposition.com/training/cyclical-ketogenic-diets-and-endurance-performance-qa.html](http://www.bodyrecomposition.com/training/cyclical-ketogenic-diets-and-endurance-performance-qa.html)

His line of though goes well with the worries that I have been formulating in my head and my worst fears (I know this applies to you also Meg) that low carb and anaerobic racing might be a no-no both for performance and health (well for health might even be even worse with high carb indeed...). He doesn't have a clear answer to our question yet (althought he points out that training fasted can bring some cool adaptations, but as for racing things seem to change and that correlates pretty much with everything that the current sports science has to say)... and I think this guy has pretty good stuff... so you see how difficult an issue this is. I bet Jack would say that things can be very different for the fully keto-adapted, cold adapted ... (PPP?) but as for right now I think we can't have a definitive clue, and more important the few ones that seem to be reaping it's benefits are ultra-endurance athletes... which makes sense because fat fuelling here plays a massive role... It would be fun to prove that you can run your PB on a 5K only on fat... I would find accomplishing this challenge a lot more astonishing... I've yet to decide if I'm willing to try to approach that route.... scary.

### Barry — 2013-06-21T18:21:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88589>

snooker players can die from heart attacks in their 50's..... is snooker then bad for you ? actually, people die from crossing the road, should we all stop crossing the road ?

Honestly, the bashing of endurance exercise is a complete dogma that largely stems from meat heads and weights/sprints devotees that think that the best and only way to exercise is to bench press and do a few metcons.

But rather then just getting into a slagging match, here's some facts. Arguably the most demanding endurance sport is professional cycling. Here you're dealing with individuals that "endurance" train 20-30hrs/wk, barely lift anything, eat copious amounts of carbs, and know nothing about CT and EMF's. Surely thats massive oxidative stress and telomere shortening, right ? So how are the majority of them living healthy and long lives, pretty much as good if not better than the average ?

This is a study done just to prove that very point. 834 pro cyclists compared to the general population, and guess what, they had a longer life expectancy !??

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21618162](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21618162)

not only do we need to question everything, but we need to ask the right questions....... and always look before you jump

### RickD — 2013-06-21T19:41:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88597>

>
> Jack and Caroline.... I know this but I can't help but feel invincible all the time... it's a SERIOUS problem... I can read the research up and down then I pick and choose what I want to apply to me... i.e Epi-Paleo Diet YES..... Reduced Exercise NO.....

Meghan it's not a problem. That feeling of invicibility is what makes you a top notch endurance athlete. I say that as someone with a half dozen Ironman races, 25 marathons and numerous marathon XC races and bicycle stage races under his belt. I lived the dream for nearly 20yrs, but arrived at 50 with the hormone panel of a 80 yr old. Fast forward a few years and I turned those numbers around so I'm not sure the damage is irreversible at least I hope so.

So by all means continuing hammering just realize there are consequences in doing it.

You also ask how to break yourself of your current way of thinking well if you're like me it will happen on its own. In a few years when the personal bests are harder and harder to achieve you'll realize it's time to move on with your life. I still train harder than most, but no longer feel I have anything to prove to myself. Been there, done that collected the T-shirt

### Dextery — 2013-06-21T20:43:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88604>

**Bloomberg News [http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2013-...uld-think-twice-before-running-triathlons.htm](http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2013-06-20/men-over-40-should-think-twice-before-running-triathlons.htm)l**

**For men competing in triathlons past the age of 40, the grueling slog to the finish line could be their last.
**

As the average age of competitors in endurance sports rises, a spate of deaths during races or intense workouts highlights the risks of excessive strain on the heart through vigorous exercise in middle age. Among the recent casualties: Michael McClintock, senior managing director of Macquarie Group Ltd. (MGQ) and a triathlete, who died at age 55 of cardiac arrest earlier this month after training.

The 40-to-60-year age bracket, often referred to as middle aged men in Lycra, or Mamils, now holds 32 percent of the membership in USA Triathlon, the sportâ€™s official governing body in the U.S. More fitness conscious than previous generations, their numbers in competitive races are swelling, along with their risk of cardiac arrest. Triathlons, the most robust of endurance races requiring swimming, biking and running, are also believed to be the most risky.

â€œPeople need to understand that theyâ€™re not necessarily gaining more health by doing more exercise,â€ said David Prior, a cardiologist and associate professor of medicine at the University of Melbourne. â€œThe attributes to push through the barriers and push through the pain are common in competitive sport, but thatâ€™s also dangerous when it comes to ignoring warning signs.â€

While benefits of exercise are well-known, researchers now suspect that there may be a point at which exertion becomes dangerous, especially for middle aged men who, because of gender and changes that accompany aging, are more susceptible to cardiac arrest caused by vigorous exercise.

**CardiacArrest**
Cardiac arrest, which occurs when the heart suddenly stops beating, can be caused by almost any heart condition, including abnormal heart rhythm, thickening heart muscle and arteries -- changes that can occur silently as healthy people age. The risk of sudden cardiac arrest, which can be brought on with physical stress, increases with age, and men are two to three times more likely to suffer from it than women, according to the National Institutes of Health.

McClintock, the Macquarie Bank executive who died on June 2 at his home in Larchmont, New York, was an avid skier, biker and golfer. The previous September, he completed the Jarden Westchester Triathlon, his first Olympic-length event, taking less than 4 hours to swim 0.9-miles (1.4 kilometers), cycle 25 miles and run 6.2 miles.

While McClintockâ€™s death canâ€™t be directly linked to the race, USA Triathlon has noted an increase in race-related fatalities, with the highest number occurring in the 40-to-49-year age group.

**TriathlonRisk**
The death rate for triathlons is about twice that of marathons because of increased intensity of the competition and the initial swimming leg of the events, according to a 2012 study published last year in the journal Mayo Clinic Proceedings.

â€œThe swim seems to be a particularly dangerous time,â€ said Andre La Gerche, a cardiologist at Melbourneâ€™s St Vincentâ€™s Hospital and marathoner. â€œParadoxically, in the marathon, itâ€™s the opposite: itâ€™s the last mile of the event where the vast majority of fatalities occur.â€ Researchers speculate that sprinting to the finish produces a rush of adrenalin that may trigger an abnormal rhythm in runners with susceptible hearts.

**â€˜Fighting to Breatheâ€™**
The swim leg of the triathlon, often held in open water, can be â€œextraordinarily stressful,â€ said La Gerche, who has also competed in more than 100 triathlons. â€œYou have people climbing all over you. Sometimes youâ€™re fighting to breathe, and thatâ€™s not something the body is used to.â€

Open-water racing triggers a clash of two mechanisms of the involuntary nervous system, according to researchers at Englandâ€™s University of Portsmouth. A â€œfight or flightâ€ response activated by physical exertion, cold water temperature or anxiety tries to speed up the heart rate and causes hyperventilation, just as the body tries to slow the heart rate to conserve oxygen in response to facial wetting, water entering the mouth, nose and throat, and extended breath-holding, the scientists said.

â€œNormally the two responses donâ€™t happen at the same time, but when they do, the heart can go into abnormal rhythms, which can cause sudden cardiac death,â€ Mike Tipton, who runs the universityâ€™s Extreme Environments Laboratory, wrote in a commentary for the British Journal of Sports Medicine in February.

**Train to Sprint**
Runners should maintain their pace or slowdown in the last mile and not sprint unless they have trained for it, the International Marathon Medical Directorâ€™s Association, a doctorâ€™s group, said in 2010 in a list of recommendations in response to race-related sudden deaths.

Running appears to lower the risk for mortality when an athlete doesnâ€™t exceed more than 20 miles a week, log more than five to seven miles per hour, or run more than two to five times a week, researchers at the Ochsner Health System of New Orleans and the University of South Carolina found in a study last year.

More than 2 million people participate in long-distance running races in the U.S. each year -- a number that has doubled since 2000. Even though the risk of death from marathon-running is small, increased participation has resulted in a higher incidence of sudden death at the events, according to a study published in the New England Journal of Medicine in January 2012. Out of 11 million long-distance runners, 59 people suffered cardiac arrest, 51 of them men.

Causes of cardiac events in athletes vary depending on age. For those under 35, cardiovascular conditions are usually inherited. Heart incidents in older athletes can be due to coronary artery disease that they donâ€™t know they have, Melbourne cardiologist Prior said.

**VesselDisease**
By middle age, most people have developed some underlying early stage vessel disease, such as hardening or plaque buildup in their coronary arteries, said Kade Davison, who teaches clinical exercise science at the University of South Australia in Adelaide.

â€œIf anyone is going to have a cardiac event theyâ€™re far more likely to have one during exercise,â€ said Davison. A person is seven times more likely to have a heart incident while exercising than at rest, he said, citing a 1984 New England Journal of Medicine study.

A personâ€™s electrolyte balance changes while doing long distance endurance events like marathons or long cycle rides, Davison said. People might also suffer potassium or sodium depletion, or become dehydrated, which also contributes to extra stress on the heart.

**HeartTears**
Intense exercise for periods longer than one to two hours can cause over-stretching and tiny tears of the heartâ€™s tissue, said James Oâ€™Keefe, a sports cardiologist and head of preventative cardiology at the Mid America Heart Institute in Kansas City, Missouri. This type of repeated injury over years can cause irregular heart rhythms, increased inflammation, scarring and stiffening of the arteries, he said.

Athletic over-achievers tend to think that â€œmore is better,â€ though when it comes to health, â€œmoderation is almost always best,â€ said Mid America Heart Instituteâ€™s Oâ€™Keefe.

As a precaution, getting a computerized tomography, or CT, scan of the heart to look for calcified plaque is a good way for endurance athletes to check if their workouts are putting their heart at risk, Oâ€™Keefe said. Yet there is no agreement on what the best strategy is for testing.

**â€˜No Good Testâ€™**
â€œThe throwaway line is to consult your doctor to make sure youâ€™re fit to race,â€ said Melbourne cardiologist La Gerche. â€œThe only good tests depend on people having symptoms and telling their doctors. In healthy asymptomatic people, there is no good test to see if someone is at risk of sudden death.â€

Those who experience a bit of chest pain or become breathless should see a doctor to check it out, especially if the discomfort occurs during training, said University of South Australiaâ€™s Davison. Sudden events that occur in people who have had no previous sign of heart disease usually indicate a build-up of plaque.

When plaque ruptures, it can cause a clot in an artery, which often doesnâ€™t show up in typical stress tests of ECG monitoring of the heartâ€™s electrical activity. Clots arenâ€™t often detected until the heart becomes stressed enough to cause a rupture, causing sudden onset of chest pain, Davison said.

Screening typically would have more value in people who are new to exercise and donâ€™t know whether they have developed a disease. People who regularly exercise without any symptoms arenâ€™t likely to show any signs in a stress test, Davison said.

**Competition Changes**
World Triathlon Corp., the owner of the sportâ€™s Ironman-branded events, made changes to the swim portion of select races after an increase in competitor deaths in recent years, the company announced last month.

Events in Coeur dâ€™Alene, Idaho; Lake Placid, New York; and Mont-Tremblant, Quebec, no longer feature a mass swim start, eliminating a long-standing Ironman tradition. Athletes at those races will either enter the water in a continuous stream through an access point, with their time starting when they cross a timing mat, or in staggered waves based on their age group.
The changes came two months after Ross Ehlinger, a 46-year-old man from Austin, Texas, died during the swim portion of the Escape from Alcatraz Triathlon and nine months after Andy Naylor, a 43-year-old member of the Hong Kong Police Force, died near the conclusion of the 2.4-mile swim portion of the New York City Ironman. In 2011, two competitors died during the swim portion of the Olympic-distance New York City Triathlon.

### Dextery — 2013-06-21T20:44:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88605>

**MetaMan Iron**
Other organizers are also taking precautions. On the last day of August, triathletes will gather on the beach of Bintan, Indonesia and participate in the MetaMan Iron Distance race. Two speedboats will be on hand to help racers in the event of a medical emergency, said organizer spokeswoman Hollie Avil.

O’Keefe advises his patients, especially those over 45, to run no more than 20 miles a week, spread out over three to four days.

“That’s not to say you can’t get problems when you’re under 45,” said O’Keefe. “But you’re much more susceptible when you’re over 45 because it just takes longer for your body to recover and when you hammer it day in and day out, it just takes a toll on your body.”

### caroline — 2013-06-22T04:13:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88641>

Meghan - I have been thinking a lot about this since the previous posts....... I don't know anything about runner's high or what ever your chosen poison/passion is ........... but I realise that I did the same thing - only in my field of endeavor. I would work 14 hours days routinely and think nothing of pulling all niters. I loved what I did - it was my passion and so - I guess ....my high. I only see now and understand the damage I did to myself and only because of Dr. K. He resonated because he has the science and cares enough to share it .... and is brutally honest. He has been laying all this out for us the last couple of years .....I think anyone would be a fool to ignore it.

Not only did I love what I did so much - I was really good at it .... pretty hard to turn your back on that combination.....but if I had read Jack's stuff then - I am pretty sure I would have stopped and listened and figured out a better way ... and that is what you guys are trying to do here .. I applaud you for that.

The easy part is if you just drop dead at 40 or 50 or 60. The hard part is if you become very ill and incapacitated and live for years with loved ones caring for you. I know of what I speak.........This happened to my DH. He was a very strong man physically and mentally. All of a sudden - one day - he was attached to a dialysis machine, had heart failure and then vascular dementia. This is not how you want to live if you have a choice .........and everyone that cares enough to be on this forum and searching for truths and asking questions of Jack ad nauseum - as he would say .........we are at least thinking and listening and searching and reading and stretching ourselves to do better.........

The thing is - when you get to 60+......you look back and your life has gone by in an instant.........I think that is what you don't get when you are younger. When you are young time stretches in front of you seemingly for ever - and maybe that is a good thing....

At least now you can see the handwriting on the wall ..... there is this guy with a flashlight.....

It is amazing to me that you can do what you love .....just get your labs done and adjust your lifestyle accordingly...you are in the driver's seat! It might not be the perfect answer .... but life is far from perfect.....As we have been saying for so long - this a journey of a lifetime .......we only get one shot to get this right......it is all in the trying.

Meghan, never met you and don't know you but out of all the nutritional, adaptation, training and performance advice I could give you..... the most important thing I sense you need to focus on is your state of mind. What you think you can do or can't do, is usually right. Stress is the central command centre. I explained this in a previous post I wrote about the Orchestra/Conductor....

What I'm trying to convey here in general is that we can do anything we think we can do. Plus I'm trying to show that there are athletes that live long healthy lives that don't eat any fish and gorge on carbs. I'm not saying its better or even good. I'm simply saying it can and has been done. Thus, we don't yet know exactly how everything works. So synthesize your own happiness, create your own fish bowl and enjoy the journey.

### Barry — 2013-06-25T23:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88920>

Lets get back on track here:

Training Update:

230km bike ride, 8.5hrs, with big mountain climbs

Breakfast: fasted, just black fresh cofee

first feed: after 2.5hrs

I train with pro cyclists. This isn't easy riding. When you put your body to the test, it has to adapt. I now have identified a pattern during these ultra distance challenges. The first 3hrs are good, no hunger or fatigue. 4-6hrs is where the fatigue and hurt manifest. Appetite goes up, mainly for sugars, so I address that. 6-8hrs is a transition period, almost a rebirth where the physiological mechanisms are almost forced to comply and function. After this, its cruise mode, steady state, almost feel as good as I did in the first 3hrs. Finished today in that state, no major hunger or tiredness.

This is when you really know how metabolic efficiency works

"Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined"

I encourage you to start devouring the information on this site, I have just begun doing the same and it's life changing.

I mentioned Jack's blog to him and he is aware of it. He also said that after 18 months in ketosis he noticed a remarkable shift in his anaerobic performance supporting the idea that it takes a long time to adapt even if you do a lot of things right (and maybe still miss out on some too).

### marcus — 2013-06-26T10:56:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88966>

Just want to chime in here: this is a very interesting thread. There is so much information... thx!

Some words about me: I won't tell you my backgroud here but just one experience of mine. I have had several starts with a Zero-Carb diet but it didn't last more than three days. Then I craved carbs! But finally I am on zero carb (except some days where i start with a handful of blueberries) for two weeks now without struggling.

What has helped me? CT every morning (sadly it is summer here and water is quite "hot" - at least far away from recommended 10Â°C), walking barefoot in my garden as much as possible (e.g. mow the lawn) and living the circadian rhythm.

Besides I am doing long road cycling trips (approx. 2-3 hours) in a fasted state. No hunger before, while and after these trips. Fascinating!

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-26T14:24:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-88969>

Yeah, it's really incredible what your body can do if you push it a little and allow it time to adapt. As Barry says, 'when the stimulus resonates, the adaptation occurs.' Something along those lines, lol. Give it more time and you will find it becomes better/easier. I did a keto-diet way back for like 8-10 weeks and stopped. Then went back to a cyclical ketogenic nature and then stopped again. Now I've been doing a cyclical keto diet as per the Bulletproof Diet and it was even easier this time around and there are even times I forget to consume a good amount of sodium in ketosis and I'm completely fine. I eat a BAB and go 7-10hrs to my next meal at 6 or 7pm without any hesitation. Any thought of food is easily combated by some tea. Not that I'm looking to lose weight or suppress my appetite, I'm just trying to get in a good period between my two large meals. I guess I've started "fasted training". I'm not necessarily doing it after 16-24hrs but during the period between my meals, so it's more like 6 hours of no food and then I do my BBS style workout or sprints and I feel fine, not even light headed after the workout like I used to be in keto.

Keep up the CT, I've found it helps a lot. It also helps with aches and pains. I've only been doing cold showers but I will soon ramp it up to really deep CT to help combat the EMFs I'm surrounded by and the fact that I can't eat the most optimal foods 100% of the time.

### erixsparhawk — 2013-07-01T00:29:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-89253>

Well I'm not as fast as Barry but here is my latest race report. I'm always torn on whether or not to take supplements and ergonomic aids in races and training. I firmly believe it is necessary to train the PPP pathway without using them to truly improve. On the other hand if you don't use them you will lose all your top end speed during the years it takes your liver to learn to pump out the huge amounts of ketone bodies required for running fast fasted. Taking the vespa was like being shot out of a cannon.

"This race was a big step in my Achilles recovery. It was one of the longest runs of 2013 and I didn't have any pain  . It seems to be a sign that I can really up the training intensity to where it should be and regain my lost fitness.

Brief Summary.
19 hour fast
Race
Took vespa concentrate half way through
Didn't really drink anything

Here is the important pre-race and race info:

Friday Morning - 45m Spin, 1h leg workout, 1 hour walk in the afternoon - 3 regular keto meals
Saturday Morning - 1h Swim, 30m core workout, 1 hour walk in the afternoon - keto breakfast and lunch finished at 1:45 PM.
Sunday Morning -
5:45 AM - 1/2 tsp course sea salt,
6:45 AM - grapefruit seed extract supplement
7:00 AM - double shot espresso American0 8 oz fluid.
8:30 AM - one 12min mile warm-up on hills
8:50 AM - 1.7 mM/mL blood ketone level
9:08 AM - Race Start
9:48 AM - 1 Tablespoon water
9:58 AM - 1 vespa concentrate
10:42 AM - finish
11:00 AM - < .1 mM/mL blood ketone level
2:00 PM - got hungry, ate big keto meal. beef, avocado, eggs, and bone broth.

Results: I was very flat and a bit sore for the first mile right from the start. 1.5 miles in felt normal. 1 min after taking vespa gained 5th gear and felt 100% fresh. I should have taken the vespa 30 min earlier or taken some Amino Acids before starting the race. Maybe a longer warm up but I was worried the Achilles wouldn't hold up for so many miles. Finished the race feeling strong enough to do it again and with a major runners high. No pain before, during, or after the run.

I hope the link works.

[http://connect.garmin.com/activity/335638792](http://connect.garmin.com/activity/335638792) "

### Barry — 2013-07-31T16:58:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-91267>

Race Update:

Distance: 60Km
Ascent: 4000m

Finishing Time: 8.36hrs
Position: 14th

Day Before Race: 3 meals, LCHF

Race Morning: 1 x black coffee

These types races of "short" for me and the courses involve big mountain climbing and descending. Basically, I'm still learning how to race these types of races. They are not my forte. >100km races is where I can compete at the very top end. So I am not putting the blame on my diet/fasting for not winning these races. I was just over 30mins off 3rd place, which can be made up by changing the specificity of my training. At the moment, I am training specifically for the 15-20hr type races.

Still felt quite comfortable during this 8.5hr run, no signs of "bonking" , and minimal fuelling during, more brain fuelling (as I've discussed before) than muscle fuelling. The race also had varied intensities, not just steady state fixed pace. The body is capable of more, the less we give it. As mentioned several times before, all the dots need to be connected. This in itself varies on an individual basis, sometimes they are, sometimes they're not. But in terms of energy production for ultra endurance events, we have ample stores and methods of producing energy without having to rely on the bucket loads of carbs.

And for the record, I've told Ben Greenfield all this, so if he connects the dots and goes on feel... he should get similar results

### Danco3636 — 2013-08-10T02:41:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-91982>

OK my update.....
Been CT-ing, daily.... eliminating and protecting as much as I can from EMF.... eating straight epi-paleo - ketogenic diet with no refeeds..... very low carbs with IF.... basically following the Dr. Jack Kruse PPP protocol for performance and health the best I know how. (no carbs in the summer for me right now).

Finding sleep is great.... I wake up with energy through the roof.
I am leaner than ever before.
Strength is at my upper end again.
Basically all good things happening health wise....
I can't sit still and look forward to getting cold as often as I can.

One great observation is that I am becoming a _"supple leopard"_ but not the Kelly Starrett way. I am becoming aches and pain free.... no more back and IT issues.... The great thing is that I am becoming flexible, pliable and bendable through movement (MovNat and Ido Portal way).... I no longer need a foam roller, lacrosse ball or rubber bands to work muscles out. I do work on mobility but through movement. I find I just don't have the knots, and tight muscles like I did before.... My muscles seem pliable and take on my youthful movement....

Just got done moving back to my old home which is in more of a rural area away from the city neighboring wifi, EMF and tons of electrical lines and towers.... I also have a pool (I have a lap pool  )that will get cold during the winter..... So I will be stepping it up even more... plus some dirt trails and outside grass to ground and run/ sprint on! Lots of room to play on.....

I am 49 and growing younger!

### Barry — 2013-08-14T17:24:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-92499>

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=92442)
>
> Interesting article....
> [http://www.inigomujika.com/en/2013/08/interviews-with-the-elite-louise-m-burke-i-am-always-suspicious-when-new-dietary-crazes-hit/3026](http://www.inigomujika.com/en/2013/08/interviews-with-the-elite-louise-m-burke-i-am-always-suspicious-when-new-dietary-crazes-hit/3026)

interesting but missing the point..

The point is that the academics use CW and look at things in isolation. They have to in a way... its the conventional way to carry out studies

However, my big issue with these type of studies is that they are not using adapted athletes and they are not incorporating all the dots. Jack has made the point before I think in CT-6, a warm adapted athlete works differently to a cold adapted one. I have also made a similar point, a fasted state/fat adapted athlete works differently to a standard HC/non fat adapted athlete. EMF 4 also covers this point extensively.... basically, put the blinkers on and eat and train in a certain way, then that's all you will ever be capable of doing.

So you can do all the studies in the world, but if you are not using the right subjects, then you will not see the real results.

Secondly, she is still stuck in the world of energy output/input and calories etc. They haven't looked at endogenous clocks, timing, energy coherence and electron flow..... they probably never will... but if you've been reading Jacks blogs, then you should realize by now how crucial all these things are

Finally, can't remember where I read this, but if you keep believing in the evidence-based science, then you will only know now what the rest did a few years ago

★彡 This mornings "Wake-up" ☆ー 6:00 am
First thing a large cold glass of San Pellegrino water. Then a cup of green tea お茶.
Off to a cold shower and a cold pool swim for about 20-25 minutes.
Once done while still dripping wet off for a walk with the dog with the sunrise. (I love seeing the sunrise).
Explored my surroundings, MovNat: climbed trees, crawled, handstands, handsprings, sprints, balanced on cable, stretching movement, went barefoot etc. basically had fun.
Got home jumped back into a cold bathtub, did some ring work and warmed up in the sun with a warm cup of black coffee and my wife.

Break-fast will be sometime this afternoon.

〜 Now off to some job walk throughs in Napa Ca.

How did your morning kick off?
Do you live your ideal morning?

### Danco3636 — 2013-10-21T02:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-98454>

Posting to keep this thread alive.....
Also keeping my eye on it to see how Barry's progress and updates are coming along....
Been at the PPP adaption for almost a year and 1/2 now with pretty lower carb before that. (cycled some cabs).
Now it has been 100% nutritional keto/ Epi-Paleo and working at connecting the dots.

Did a triathlon for the fun of it last weekend at the end of a 20 hour fast and fast for a few hows afterwards (no intake during).... Then killed some sashimi.... It was a sprint distance one.... went pretty hard and felt great..... Jumped in and did it for a fun check.... Haven't been formally training other than just random swims, runs and some biking along with mostly strength training and MovNat skills.... Most of the training is in the form of sprints or high intensity intervals.... Short and minimalist aproach.

Today:
★〜 Feeling like a million bucks on a Sunday?

★ First a mid morning fasted workout of MovNat, sprints, trail run and cold plunges in the local river.
★ After a 20 minute cold dip session in the pool and sun reading Jacks book.
★ Then around 1:00pm ate bacon, shrimp and oysters.
★ Finished it off with a 10-15 min nap on the grass in the sun.

Woke up from that state with lots of cool dreams.

### Danco3636 — 2013-10-30T23:38:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-99453>

Another short update.....

Jumped into a Merrell Down & Dirty Mud Run 5k race. I won my age division (45-49, I'm 49).
Done in a fasted state with coffee for breakfast. Ran the race in a ketosis state no carbs needed for this bad boy!
Before this "race" it was just black coffee. I did add a dab of butter (Kerry Gold) for some flavor. About 50 calories or 1/2 a table spoon or so. I then didn't eat for 3 or 4 hours afterwards until my wife and I stopped off for Mexican food on the way home. Just water after the race. Had pulled pork and guacamole on a little greens with salsa to spice it up.

Oh and my run training for this event is 1 run a week on Sundays..... Mostly intervals, slow jog, sprints and exploring area's for MovNat fun. After the run is followed with a cold plunge in my now cold pool or cold streams along the way. I just run intuitively. Most of my training now is strength, power, mobility and movement oriented otherwise. I do 1 cold water swim a week and another day of bike intervals and fun. I try and keep training to a minimalist approach and use daily activity for movement skills..... I see a tree I climb it.... I see a rock I jump on it.... I see a curb I am balancing.... I see an open field I am crawling and doing cartwheels and handsprings..... I love to play.  Instead of work-out it is Play-in for me.....

I am discovering why I am making this keto 100% diet work while others need carbs or carb refeeds. - Seems the "cold". The more I do it the sweeter it gets. It seems to tie it together. I am still pursuing optimal and my performance potential, both in health and performance using the PPP and keeping it fun!

Anyway that is what is going on with me... I would love to hear what others are up to.....

### Hemming — 2013-11-13T10:04:02+0200

My current routine is biking to the ocean around lunch time then I swim for however long I feel comfortable (the water is 8-9C currently). I bike back to work and do different body weight exercises in a HIIT format.
Additionally, I commute by bike 10k every day. Its not really fasted state training as I eat two times each day so the longest fasts I do at the moment is 8-9h. I still need to recover more before I push it further. I have tried running fasted not that long ago and it went surprisingly well.

### Josh — 2013-12-09T20:08:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-102987>

I am posting this here because they advocate eating a lot while working with their direct current modality/device, but I feel better now with no food. Consider this video:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SiR1cKixrBA](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SiR1cKixrBA)

I wonder about the synergy with their technology, CT, Keto-adaption, etc.. If we stimulate the muscles 250 times faster than normal, do we alter time and can the body respond or does it just age faster. There is a secondary signal to the nervous system that may alter something along the lines of what Becker found in his D/C work where one could alter brain waves and nervous system response at the brain level. Still trying to figure it out myself. I have one of the units and there is a lot to learn. I have no idea what she ate on her run.

### Danco3636 — 2013-12-09T21:06:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-102993>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=102987)
>
> I am posting this here because they advocate eating a lot while working with their direct current modality/device, but I feel better now with no food. Consider this video:
>
> [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SiR1cKixrBA](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SiR1cKixrBA)
>
> I wonder about the synergy with their technology, CT, Keto-adaption, etc.. If we stimulate the muscles 250 times faster than normal, do we alter time and can the body respond or does it just age faster. There is a secondary signal to the nervous system that may alter something along the lines of what Becker found in his D/C work where one could alter brain waves and nervous system response at the brain level. Still trying to figure it out myself. I have one of the units and there is a lot to learn. I have no idea what she ate on her run.

So far I have been finding out that with CT, Keto, Grounding - combined with body movement and mobility work my flexibility, strength, muscle function has improved dramatically. That and with minor aches, pains and seemingly related injury's are but a thing of the past. I had always thought that these aches and pains where just a part of the training.
I have discovered that ice is great for injury's but not in the context of putting ice on the injured spot.... that just isn't nearly as effective as whole body CT.

I hyper extended my elbow in some martial arts training about a week ago.... It tightened up pretty bad and was sore. I started putting ice on my elbow which did very little other than take the pain away upon application.... but once I introduced a bit more Deep CT.... up to the point of shivering and just being cold for most of the day (wasn't fun) most of the pain and limited function almost entirely went away in about two days... Right now it is 90% healed.... I say that because I feel a little twinge in it still.
Seems rapid healing and recovery happens when the environment supports it and is right.

### Josh — 2013-12-12T20:25:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-103233>

The point about icing an individual spot versus whole body is interesting. I hurt my back on Sunday so I began using whole body in a 33 degree tub outside and the ARP and made good progress. Yesterday I spoke with the ARP people and they said that they do not work with people who use cold because it causes concentric contraction of muscles and the device stimulates eccentric contraction. One of their stated goals is to replace R. I. C. E with their protocols which they say are far more efficacious. I am still learning and will go back and forth until I get to clarity. You can see Dr. Kruse's answers in the Cave where I asked for help on this as well. I feel the answers are in the EE10 material and perhaps what is coming regarding these principles in the mitochondria, etc.. It is a lot of information to scale out into one's physiology. I quote Dr. Kruse: "Details Matter in Semiconduction". I remember my internal martial arts teachers saying that the two greatest challenges are to get people to stop using what they already know and to stop at their resonant capacity, not their physical/external/strength capacity. I now see it as training to function all of the time at resonant/entropy optimal as opposed to pushing the system to function at sub-optimal physiology which over time breaks it down. FOr most people including me, this has involved a lot of un-learning.

### Albert83BCN — 2013-12-23T16:33:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-104117>

IMHO it depends a lot on what you are after but I doubt you can have everything without paying a price.

HIIT and weight training are great if you want to build muscle and shred fat but they are intense, specially in the build muscle thing, you're basically destroying yourself so you can rebuild a stronger version of you... and in a rush for becoming huge and ripped you do this day after day so no wonder it activates mTOR, generate ROS... you're basically speeding up ageing by using your regenerating capabilities constantly, as a consequence you might be living faster. I doubt doing in it on the morning or not will make a significant difference in that regard, might be just a slightly one. Long endurance running? I see it very similarly, here you cannot build massive muscle but instead you develop endurance and economy... awesome! but abuse it and might end in a similar scenario.

So exercise in general might be great until a certain degree while serves as a way to stay fit and offset your other daily stressors. And in that context maybe it's up to everyone if HIIT, strenght, mass, endurance, crossfit, slow cardio or whataver with only beenfits and no harm until you stay in the green/orange zone. But when you have to ramp up training not to be a healthy person but to excel at perforance because you self imposed it or whatever then it is when you surpass your ability to regenerate without having to suck your life energy and things start to get bad. Not much different from eating huge carbs + trans fats all day long and dealing with constant daily stress, different bodies and lifestyles you'll get for sure but in the end it might perfectly be that the mechanisms for ageing are not that different. Quantum anabolic/catabolic time-travelling? I think I'm starting to develop cool new concepts like Jack hehe.

If you seek for longevity then probably living in a metabolically reduced state, on the cold under a waterfall, quiet and meditating all day long with a ketogenic under caloric diet might be the best.... now, what kind of life do you do like or can afford the most? Want to live until 100 that way or do you prefer living on fire, training hard, getting huge and looking for hot girls on the disco so you die young but leave a good-loking cadaver? This forum is mainly to reverse health issues and longevity so a certain bias to the first case is expected.

Well, finding the perfect balance in between would be awesome for most of us, I guess what's what we are after here in this thread in the first place. Aren't we?

This forum is mainly to reverse health issues and longevity so a certain bias to the first case is expected.
>
> Well, finding the perfect balance in between would be awesome for most of us, I guess what's what we are after here in this thread in the first place. Aren't we?

### Josh — 2014-02-04T02:34:12+0200

Thanks for the update, just hit a new place with ketosis and ct and hunger has dropped off with just a lot more water and some more cold and sunlight. Training is easier with no soreness. In 1994 in Hong kong, I met a 70 year old tai chi master who weighed 110lbs and was 5'3" . He uprooted me and threw me 20 feet across his apartment. He stood in his apartment in a dress shirt and slacks with dress shoes on a parquet floor. He stood on one leg, raised the other to waist level in a graceful spiral like a ballet dancer and said all of my good students do this 15 minutes each day, it is all you really need, If you want I will teach you more, but you do not need it. I decided to chase my now wife to Majorca. I often wonder if I had stayed...At the time love seemed a greater mystery and risk...still learning from my wife. I always get high and light when fasting.
