### JoeBranca — 2014-02-18T07:27:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-110959>

I love this crowd ; )

It is very interesting applying this pathway to strength gains like Dan says and also BJJ training which is its own unique beast. It didn't seem too long ago when I had the hard wired need to down bananas and sweet potatoes before and after a class, though I'd still get gassed pretty quickly. Now I've come a long way and it's all about water, water, water, and intelligent breathing / energy conservation. And then yeah I'll get aroud to eating afterwards but that's becoming almost as much an after thought as when I last ate before training. My stamina still has a way to go (more to do with consistency in training than anything else), but the potential is apparent. I'll be making note of my progress -- i.e. the interior sense of how well activity and energy utilization are yoked, and hope to post updates later this year.

### Josh — 2014-02-22T00:31:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-111600>

Hi Barry,

I really appreciate your recent podcast. I had some thoughts.

1.  To recommend coffee to everyone without assessing whether they may be TH1 dominant which would make coffee pro-inflammatory seems imprudent. It seems that determining whether someone is TH1 or TH2 dominant would prescribe whether Green Tea or Coffee would be a better choice. In addition adequate molybdenum is required to handle the sulfur in the coffee and the methyl xanthines from both.
2.  I have found that high performance athletes often ignore inflammatory issues that are sub-clinical because they are strong enough to not notice them. This does not mean that they do not affect performance. As Dr. Kruse has highlighted, the immune system is the top dog in the energy game with the brain.
3.  As far as the "Central Governor" idea goes, wouldn't the common pathway of the effect be a stress response that diverts resources from physical performance to run the energy hog of a brain we have? Classically, this always comes down to variants on 2 approaches: A. absolute focus and concentration which can be perceived as a "stress" for the body, B. some form of "emptying" the mind which allows people to perform in a state of "calm" which triggers less of a stress response.
4.  Has anyone researched the advanced internal martial artists who are able to move and act without muscular tension? Are any top performance athletes able to do this?
5.  Pasta, its preparation, how it was made, from what wheat, and the accompanying sauce, etc. vary a lot from place to place. Even the water it is boiled in and for how long. Do the riders who live on it, carry their own and have it prepared in special ways everywhere or do they take whatever is available locally?
6.  Do you notice that over a season or a few years, the need for transition elements such as molybdenum, manganese, and others increase? Or do people become more efficient in their utilization? If you have noticed this, how is it assessed.

Hope these are not annoying questions. They are just what came up after listening. I greatly appreciate your sharing here and in the larger performance community.

### Danco3636 — 2014-02-22T03:33:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-111643>

Good and interesting questions..... ^^^

### Barry — 2014-02-23T16:38:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-111807>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=111600)
>
> Hi Barry,
>
> I really appreciate your recent podcast. I had some thoughts.
>
>
> 1.  To recommend coffee to everyone without assessing whether they may be TH1 dominant which would make coffee pro-inflammatory seems imprudent. It seems that determining whether someone is TH1 or TH2 dominant would prescribe whether Green Tea or Coffee would be a better choice. In addition adequate molybdenum is required to handle the sulfur in the coffee and the methyl xanthines from both.
> 2.  I have found that high performance athletes often ignore inflammatory issues that are sub-clinical because they are strong enough to not notice them. This does not mean that they do not affect performance. As Dr. Kruse has highlighted, the immune system is the top dog in the energy game with the brain.
> 3.  As far as the "Central Governor" idea goes, wouldn't the common pathway of the effect be a stress response that diverts resources from physical performance to run the energy hog of a brain we have? Classically, this always comes down to variants on 2 approaches: A. absolute focus and concentration which can be perceived as a "stress" for the body, B. some form of "emptying" the mind which allows people to perform in a state of "calm" which triggers less of a stress response.
> 4.  Has anyone researched the advanced internal martial artists who are able to move and act without muscular tension? Are any top performance athletes able to do this?
> 5.  Pasta, its preparation, how it was made, from what wheat, and the accompanying sauce, etc. vary a lot from place to place. Even the water it is boiled in and for how long. Do the riders who live on it, carry their own and have it prepared in special ways everywhere or do they take whatever is available locally?
> 6.  Do you notice that over a season or a few years, the need for transition elements such as molybdenum, manganese, and others increase? Or do people become more efficient in their utilization? If you have noticed this, how is it assessed.
>
> Hope these are not annoying questions. They are just what came up after listening. I greatly appreciate your sharing here and in the larger performance community.

Josh,

nothing annoying about your questions, however I would like to make the point that going into any specific details during a 1hr podcast is not that easy to do. I have stated already that the podcast only scratched the surface and the my posts here on this thread go a lot deeper.

1\. An example of an specific detail that I'm not going to cover during a podcast. Did you know that many people don't have alpha-galactosidase which breaks down raffinose ? This is the sugar present in broccoli... so no everyone should eat broccoli. The same goes for practically every food and drink.

2\. Totally agree. The only true way to ascertain this is the learn how to listen to your own body and go on feel. If you are in tune with yourself and can intuitively sense when something is not right, then you can be your own master. That's what I've noticed personally and also with top athletes I work with. They know immediately as soon as something is out of sync.

3\. This is deep rabbit hole. In my opinion, its the RDS of athletic performance, that's why I mentioned it. There are several factors. Prof Tim Noakes has written several articles on this. Here's one: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21326375](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21326375). The main point is this~: performance does not drop because of diminishing physiological reasons but more due to how to the brain wants to conserve a homeostasis. In others words, you don't bonk because you have ran out of energy/ATP. You bonk because your brain says so.

4\. Not to my knowledge

5\. They take whatever is available. My point was that nutrition is not what determines performance. My other point is that athletes simply as a result of their exercise regime, have many of the adaptations that a "normal" person is trying to achieve, from UCPs to BAT, from SIRT-1 to PPAR, from PPP to Redox Potentials..... athletes actually have these almost by default.

6\. No precise way of assessing this. I consider these things the tree's, and I think its better to look at the woods.

Like I said, I was only scratching the surface. This stuff goes a lot deeper, I've already discussed some of it here in previous blogs. I think the missing links are related to quantum mechanics. Then I think a lot is related to spirituality. Finally, in terms of performance in fatigue dependant sports that is, you really need to be in the field to truly make sense of it. Today, there is simply too much advise coming from the trenches.

### Josh — 2014-02-23T18:04:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-111822>

Thanks for the in depth reply.

### Josh — 2014-02-23T20:21:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-111849>

> My blog is about small things. People joke about details, and paleo makes fun of them. It turns out the smallest things in life matter most. In the quantum realm, the smallest things have the biggest meaning for humans and all biology. Our real modern problem is we still remain unaware of just how much our tinkering is hurting us today. Spaceship Earth is how we evolved to travel through a small swarth of space for the last 4.5 billion years. From our “little ellipse of space” we have a few primordial elements that life organizes around. We think tinkering with this “three legged stool” is something we evolved to do. In reality, the umbilical cord of this planet’s tether to our species is a lot shorter than we all think.
>
> Dr. Kruse

1.  (1) Alpha-galactosidase is produced on the brush border of the small intestine, so it may be a victim of leaky gut and inflammation. The genetic issues may be more epigenetic related to conditions of existence. Consider for me, before higher dose Molybdenum supplementation no beans or high sulfur stuff worked, after no problem. I cannot find the formula for alpha-galactosidase, and I bet there is a sulfotransferase with molybdenum in at least one if not multiple steps of its creation and degradation. I wonder which transition metals are in its complex? What working in agriculture and health/nutrition has taught me combines with Dr. Kruse's insights in to EMF and their disproportionate effects on transition mantels is that I CAN NOT TAKE FOR GRANTED THAT ANYONE HAS ENOUGH. I am wondering whether it is actually the case that some people need to take doses that are potentially toxic just to have enough for day to day physiological function-a truly nasty Catch-22.
2.  (5) Perhaps the pasta-adapted athletes found they could not digest a lot of protein and fat as I could not because of deficiencies in the elements needed to metabolize them succesfully?
3.  (3) I am guessing from some experience that the "brain effect" is really a shift to an as yet unidentified quantam level of physiological function tied to what we are learning here on the extreme survivial end of the spectrum that if one can stay with something, kicks in. Athletes are more willing to hold on until that point and I have seen aging housewives do it...#4 would be an example of training this...
4.  (6) Based on the condition of the "woods"; EMF's, and what we are learning about water batteries and the proteins and transition metals needed to keep them working and the "trees" alive; I would say these are more like "soil" issues.

Finally, I have not worked with many elite athletes, but I have lived and worked with people who all exhibit many of these charactersitics in the hills of India. Their motivation to be cold-adapted, live on restricted calories and perform at high levels day in and day out is sheer necessity, in a word survivial. Western Nepal is so poor that the people of Northern India who are by no means wealthy, can actually afford to hire the Nepalis to do most of the hard work that they are not able to get the women to do. This has resulted in 2 things:

1.  women who at about 50 kg (110lbs) can carry loads up to 3/4 of bodyweight on their heads while walking up and down steep hills in flat rubber bath slippers. The older ones still walk barefoot at times. They not only carry the grass, firewood or dung on their head, but gather it as well. They eat two meals per day and a biscuit and tea sometime as a snack. The hills are steep and the paths narrow. I humbly admit I could not even lift what these woman carried easily. They were often budles almost as large as the women themselves. Some were as old as 60. If anyone is really interested in why the hills of India are as they are, read "The Nanda Devi Affair" by Bill Aitken. Nanda Devi remains the most beautiful mountain I have ever seen....It says a lot about the hills if you look at how the mountains around her are named....
2.  What the woman would or could not do, often went to the Nepalis, men and women though mostly men. I have seen men carry 2 or more times their own weight uphill all day in cement sacks. At other times while trekking, hill men would carry my stuff and they would run 2-3 miles at altitude with 60 lbs on their shoulder and then sit and smoke while waiting for us. They ate one large meal per day and 1-2 snacks and some tea. They slept under a blanket while I used a down bag.
3.  No one in the hills of India lives in climate control so everyone gets cold in the winter. My room which was relatively well sealed never went above 48 F for 3 months. People living in high villages experienced frost and much colder temps. If one looks at the history of mountaineering, you can see where they would bargain over how many proters at what altitude would actually have shoes. People have routinely carried barefoot in the snow.
4.  One friend in his late 70's and early 80's walked from the town of Munsyiari (pretty place in North India at 7000 feet) to Mt. Kailash and back 5 times with advanced T2D before it finally killed him because that was what he liked to do. I stayed in his house and he never spent a day of his life in a heated room. He ate 1.5 meals per day. Unfortunately he got wealthy enough to afford white flour and sugar.

I see athletes as finding sports or "conditions of existence" for which they are best "adapted" and then maintaining those adpatations as long as possible just as the hill people do to survive. I would guess that something in most of their early lives and or genetics predisposed them partially to what they end up best "adapted" for. I know Tm Ferris made his experiment and showed that based on the state of the art about 10 years ago, he had conditioned himself to overcome his genetic predisposition. I am guessing that the more we understand the quatum bilogocal function of the organism, the clearer it will be how much we can do this without breaking the rules so to speak....

### Barry — 2014-02-27T13:15:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-112435>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=111824)
>
> Hi Barry thank you for your podcast I have drawn from it some very useful tips about trying to keto adapt as a cyclist by adjusting the training with small increases at a time and being sensible.
>
> I did hear that you believe that - if I got that right - power fluctuations require the kick in of the glycolitic system where I'd need carbs. I have heard - unless wrong - Dr. Kruse say on another podcast with Ben that according to him you don't need carbs period if properly keto adapted. I think it was the podcast about changing your home re. non native EMF.
>
> I started like you, low GI etc... then low carb, by low carb I am saying under 50g a day which I try to monitor.
> I TT in the UK and need to get to a point where I am able to output constant overall 20mph at least over long distances for BBAR mandatory speeds, in 50 and 100, especially.
>
> So in principle, am I in the right frame of mind when I think that in time by following what you say as well - regular small increments, I should be able to lift my power threshold to what I need?
>
> In TTs, mainly flat courses, I don't see huge power fluctuations like in a road race for ex but in say a 10 TT the threshold is that much higher (although not a BBAR event distance).
>
> I have been low carb 6 weeks now and as I am intolerant to gluten and my family history tells me I'm best without carbs, I value that you have made it and have plenty of success from your own racing. This is encouraging.
>
> As far as the point of 'there is no coconut oil on the breakfast table' I am familiar with what the likely set up is within the cycling world/s not just in Italy. But, as this science obviously offers valid points, perhaps there maybe space in the future for an approach towards a different view point?
>
> That would be in my opinion a significant positive change.

It seems like you're on the right track but its early days.. 6 weeks is nothing in relative terms... you need months and to optimise performance you need years. For steady state endurance sports , like long distance TT's, its ideal and you can upregulate everything to improve your RQ so that you are in more of a fat burning zone at threshold.

You main question is the current can of worms. Personally, through what I've done myself and with other elite athletes... I don't think its possible. I think its theoretically possible but in practice isn't. Why ? because of what is required in order to be fully ketotic, energy coherent, chemically reduced etc. Not only do the dots need to be connected but they need to be harmonized. If you're an athlete that can't always control what they eat, can't always live like a polar bear, has to fly and travel and stay in hotels, can't avoid EMF, etc etc... then harmony is impossible.

Also, it comes down to the level of performance. If you want to take 15min off your 4hr marathon time.... then maybe the fully ketotic model is possible. But thats a different can of fish compared to taking 15min off your 2.45min marathon time. Thats the bit the people in the trenches don't get.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-27T16:13:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-112465>

You maximize and harmonize what you can control in your environment. That is the goal of all. Athletes, patients and doctors. We have to get people to that ledge because they control that aspect of their lives. We are all embers of the same fire.........we seem to forget that.

Now if Jack or anyone else wants to come run a 100mile race with me or win the Tour de France or do a sub 9hr Ironman.... in a full keto/PPP state... then I would welcome the company

If you can do it while running a 4hr marathon or doing some movnat sessions..... then that's a completely different context... just sayin

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-20T16:20:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-116677>

Cool, things are getting really interesting... that article summarizes pretty well the frustration that many underwent in their pursue for that keto adaptation that would supposedly get them to a higher degree of performance... I'd personally be even more surprised to see people up their game on a 10K, a 5K or even the mile, than an ultra endurance event. Long bouts of aerobic activity still seem somehow consistent with what our biology has in plan for us regarding fat metabolism, and with the proper adaptations I can undestand why that can be done and is feasible, and Barry is one of the living proof that it can be done without feeling drained from it. I myself experienced some of these benefits and I consider far from fully fat adapted. But, take something that is very intensive in the glycolithic pathway and show me that you can adapt to do that task solely based on fat... yep, that's what I'd love to see. I can see how the PPP can aid in the redox aspect to support us mitigate the bad stuff associated with strenuous endurance events but to say that it can bypass at all the current biochemistry on the use of glucose as the primary source for fast ATP recycling... uhmm right now I still have a hard time accepting that.

I'm not as much knowledgable as many of the people here and I'm really aware that I'm blind to just too many things in order to get a clear picture of reality (and yes, I've read EMF4 many times), but right now my perception of reallity is this: In the same fashion that people that live in summer mode 24/7/365 (that's on the SAD, high carb, high fat, high protein, high everything, with few activity, lots of comfort, etc) cannot expect to succeed and have the kind of body leanness and health that has someone who lives on a more metabollicaly reduced state , athletes who try to undertake a 'winter mode of living' (cold, keto, high fat, low everything else) cannot expect to perform the same on activities that expect you to be in spring or summer mode. That's inconsistent with how nature and evolution planed us to interact with the environtment and I think that epigenetics and our gut microbiome puts us in our place irremediably, even if we don't understand why.

To me, this oversimplification would explain pretty well why fat people do so well on keto while athletes usually fail. One needs winter desperately to show up in order to reverse the big rise in entropy that arised by taking decisions that are not meant to be taken in that context, but the others (the athletes) are needing to enter spring again after being drained for a long period of time (exercising and dieting), and what do they do? they force down eating like winter time (low carb) but perform like they're willing to go out in the sun, hunt and reproduce when their environtment and expectations are clearly not the right ones. I suspect that brings a high state of entropy that brings a similar path to failure than the other spectrum of the balance, but with different outcome. In this case: fatigue, cold hands, bad hormonal status, all what people report... That's my understanding on what 'time is off in your brain' and not only ties to light cycles but also on food availability and how you interact with the environtment. I'm not saying that fat adaptation is bad at all, I really think is a great tool, specially in the world we live in today, and that it can take us to baseline so we can then unleash of full potential, but once we want to take down a personal best on a fast race, a NOS kit on our car will take us faster to the finish line that just diesel.

That's also why (right now) I depart a little bit from the idea that cold is good for everything and reverses everything and can be applied to everything. I think we are really meant to thrieve on warm, not cold. Think about it: if it's cold, you naturally seek warm places. Primal sense at work. People on the ecuator is usually happier than people living on cold places. We also have tied this a lot of times to vitamin D status, which is totally depending on light and the Vitamin A cycle. Even Jack explained that we have mithocondria exactly because we evolved to move and get photons and electrons through the food interacting and discovering our environtment and depending on seasons because we are able to unnatach ourselves from the land thanks to that kind of adaptations, which makes sense. The problem might be that we abuse that ability even when the environtment is a mismatch and we get what we see in most people: obesity and neolithic diseases. That's when keto and cold can chime in in order to put things into place (totally agree with that line, and has proven to work so many times with obese people), but once that is achieved, at least from a performance standpoint (probably not longevity) I cannot clearly see that it will be an advantatge to stay keto but the contrary, that's catabolic or mantainance at best (I'd say you're slowing down the pass of time within yourself from a quantum perspective?), and you want to stay anabolic in order to perform your best when it comes to racing speed (even if that also brings you to a higher state of entropy but that is up tou you to decide)... and at this time, anecdotical observation seems to support this idea in most cases. Trying to tie things with quantum physics that Jack always talks about, I know little about these things but intuitively I'd say that a certain line of future where you can stay keto and perform like a spring beast when it comes to speed is very unlikely probabilistically and that's why we don't see a good outcome with that strategy often at all in our observed present and past timeline, even when it might exist in some of the infinite possible futures... a strategy that brings up the best of both worlds like carb cycling seems to bring better outcomes based on what people is reporting, which BTW seems quite in line with the idea that we are meant to embrace both types of experiences when timing is correct, based on the changes and expectations from our environtment.

### Josh — 2014-03-20T17:02:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-116693>

Keto-adaption is way more than just not eating carbs as we are all learning. Unfortunately, even where it is detailed how things can go wrong and what then needs to be evaluated, most people including doctors do not understand what it means and do not know how to d/dx. I am still learning after 2 years on a non-high performance body. The "Leap Before You Look" approach does not serve most people well unless they know how to interpret the setbacks and use them creatively to tailor things. The most obvious thing in the article was that there was no review of Leptin Resistance and IR status. As we all know, with higher inflammation levels from any stress (s) can shut down the PPP and create cravings and discomfort to the say the least. Keto-adaption is very much a "devil in the detail" process for most people and in the world of people running their bodies and economies on high carb diets, it is not encouraged. I am curious as to why thee Masai never show up in competitions? I am guessing that they are keto-adapted if they stick to their traditional diets. My Oriental Medicine mentor's twin brother spent his life studying and living with them. I will look around a bit.

I can only guess how the gut flora respond to the shift. Tim Ferrris saved me with the admonition that when one makes a big shift, one needs to accept some digestive discomfort as the gut flora shifts. He was just referring to the overt digestive discomfort. The wormtherapy people say that with the worms, they see continued shifts for 1-3 years. However, their other recommended diet and lifestyle changes are limited and they only track reduction of symptoms and some basic bloodwork. Ultimately, keto-adaption may be for endurance and occasional high output-think hunting and war. I am not sure if high output endurance sports are in any way pro-survival. Not a criticism, just an observation. They are certainly a good lab to test the extremes...

Very good and interesting points......

I would love to hear **Dr. Jacks and Barry's** take on this.......

Not just pertaining to an endurance athlete but also to a strength/ explosive one's as well..... Say for a CF athlete once fat adapted (2-3 years) and 100% keto epi-paleo diet (PPP along with doing the many things in the Quilt) out perform both a carb based diet, a CKD diet or even given the above approach...?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-24T12:23:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-117563>

Comparing one keto adapted individual to another one that is not fat adapted makes no sense IMHO... I keep hearing a lot of these kind of comparisons and I think that this is what is confusing people a lot. For example, I could run a sub 3h marathon when I was on a vegetarian diet and run faster and longer than most of my mates on 10K and the half-marathon... yet that does not necessarily mean that it was because I did it that way and I was right and them not. In fact, last time I did better on a standard paleo template... so I proved myself wrong when I thought in the past that I was not...

Another one, a friend of mine who cares nothing about his nutrition and health and drinks a lot until he gets drunk can still beat me in the 10K with sub 35'.... that, again, does not necessarily mean that his approach is better than mine... how would suit him an epi-paleo templeate and being more diligent? but based on these results people might jump to conclusions too fast and say things like 'hey drinking might be not so bad look at him he pulls out great results'... but the only way to tell is to try for yourself and see how affects you and only you. I bet most things are not what they look like.

I guess only way to know is to put as many athletes as possible to switch many times between both approaches and analyzing how well did they with respect to themselves... and then it might even be counfounding variables, as the best approach in the Nth iteration with carbs might have something to do to prior adaptations when being in a keto template... really complicated to assess! maybe we should create the best keto/carb endurance trial ever and publish our own research paper? hehe

EDITED: I just realized that there's a huge difference between 'assess' and 'asses' LOL

### Barry — 2014-03-24T23:05:31+0200

>

>
> EDITED: I just realized that there's a huge difference between 'assess' and 'asses' LOL

Finally someone is getting it...

I'm reposting a post I wrote on here before, it explains in detail what I'm trying to say

and just to repeat myself - I'm not talking here about Masai warriors, Sherpa's and people who climb tree's in the morning

I'm talking about athletes that are trying to win races.

_I think this whole carbs/performance thing needs to be pulled apart piece by piece in order for us to truly get to the bottom of it. There are many gaps here that need to be filled in. Its multi-factorial and nothing in life is unequivocal. To set the tone, I will say this:

To achieve optimal performance/health, you first need all the correct dots. The next step is to connect these dots. That's what Jack is doing here. Once the dots are connected though, they need to harmonise and form a network. From here, the network needs to proliferate and constantly breed.

To put this in less philosophical terms; the majority of paleo/fitness guru's think all you need to do is eat some grass-fed beef, some macadamia nuts, do some HIIT and wear vibrams. These are just the dots, and only a mere handful of them. The connection of all these dots is a completely different task and is linked to hormonal balance, metabolic efficiency ,neurotransmitters and inflammation. The harmonisation and proliferation of the network is another ball game that is linked to CCG's, EMF, Timing, Light, Schumann Resonance, Grounding, Cold, Epigenetics and Spirituality.

I don't have all the answers, before I go on. I'm just attempting to put some structure around this. The dots, connection, harmonisation, proliferation and breeding is not even in strict sequential order. In fact, some of the important parts of the network need to be in place before you even start connecting the dots, like Sleep/Wake cycle , Electron Flow and Adrenal Grounding
I know its not hopeless. I'm simply questioning your assumption that it works as unequivocally as you think it does, especially in this context of performance.

For now, this is also just a hypothesis. I know of no athlete doing what we/you are outlining here. I'm the closest I know, yet I am not winning olympic golds. The athletes winning gold, are warm and carb adapted. The world records are not set by Epi-Paleo athletes living EMF free, using CT and Grounding etc. Yet they are the ones performing at optimal, and most are living long and healthy lives.

CT6 - you offered Armstrong, Sherpa's and Phelps as examples, also Wil Hof. Well Armstrong wasn't winning Tour de Frances because of cold therapy training, I think that's clear now. Phelps eats more glucose and fructose to shut down the PPP system of the whole US swim team. And sherpa's and Hof don't run sub 2.05hr marathons. So there is simply no real life evidence, not even anecdotal as far as I'm concerned. What I do is about as close as you can get, and still, I'm not setting world records or winning olmypic gold medals. The guys eating cereal, pasta and 24-7 iPhone addicts are the one's doing that.

You see, exercise causes many of the adaptations that your optimal pathway does. From AMPK to mTOR to Perilipins to UCPs to PPARs. It ramps up mitochondrial efficiency, promotes fat/keto adaptation and builds a massive antioxidant capacity. Throw in some of the stuff I'm doing, and this all gets upregulated. There is even stuff going on that creates positive feedback loops in terms of the acute and even chronic training stress/poor diets of athletes. Some of them are almost in a constant state of hormesis with adaptations on overdrive (e.g. SOD production caused by an athlete that eats carbs and trains too much). So full time athletes, in the context of what you are suggesting, are inherently adapted without them even knowing. I can see this and witness it first hand. I'm in the field, you're in the trenches and what happens in the field is different, I;ve got the scars to prove it. If you want to come with me to try and win the Tour de France or set the world marathon record using keto adaptation, PPP, grounding and CT... then please feel free.

The principles related to CT, Epi-Paleo, EMFs, Circadium Biology etc, I'm in agreement with you for the large part. Exercise though, in the serious athlete context, expresses many of the adaptations embodied in these principles. It might seem unfair, but they actually have many of the dots connected without even knowing it and trying to do it. Then again, that's perhaps why it works so well for them._

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-24T23:34:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-117717>

Barry, it is ridiculously funny that just this morning I got to hear your podcast with Ben Greenfield while I was hitting the weights in the gym. In fact, I was listening to the latest podcast from the Optimal Reset and when it ended then yours popped up. I didn't remember I loaded it into the music player, but I'm glad I did, I loved it, every single bit of it made a lot of sense to me!

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-25T00:19:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-117724>

I'd like to see what happens when you take an extremely keto-adapted athlete who downs a whole bunch of amino acids, as well as a slow release carb source, and then tops that off with ketone salts. You'd theoretically have a person whose bloodstream is loaded with nutrients, leading to a scenario where the person can simultaneously burn all of Carb + Protein + Fat. Peter Attia is about the only guy who has publicly talked about doing something like this, but I'd love to see the results of top-caliber athletes (not to diminish Peter's insane work capacity).

There is obviously a very good case for exercise, which definitely does things like upregulate NAD+ synthesis in a cell (CPC8 alert for the importance of this compound). But a hormetic stimulus is by definition harmful in excess, and I personally feel that to trigger the appropriate adaptations via the exercise route can **(a)** only get you so far, and **(b)** produces a bunch of harmful by-products in the process, especially if fuelled through carbohydrates.

At some point, exercise becomes harmful, and that point is going to vary depending on the individual (use Redox Rx metrics to determine this). At that point, what if your body hasn't gotten all the needed adaptations through exercise to perform at your optimal level? (athleticism or longevity)

In that case, it's probably a good idea to simultaneously add the protocols that Jack describes, which even if they didn't directly impact top-end performance, would definitely impact peripheral factors like time to recovery (eg: Russian Olympic team and pulsed EMF). Then the question is: would adding these Epi-paleo protocols improve the top-end performance of the best athletes in the world even moreso than today?

Then there's the question of how much the drug assistance that these athletes are getting is helping with the cell's ability to use energy, and how much those "nullify" the further effectiveness of adding the Epi-Paleo protocols; I'm sure PEDs improve lots of the adaptations that going keto and embracing the cold would.

And finally, there's the question of what goals you are trying to chase. Athleticism and Longevity intuitively sound like 2 conflicting extremes of the spectrum, but I don't know enough yet to comment further. Maybe after another year of Jack's blogs and more of my own research will I have clearer answers.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T00:55:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-117731>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=117724)
>
> Then the question is: would adding these Epi-paleo protocols improve the top-end performance of the best athletes in the world even moreso than today?

I don't see any reason why they should not. The epi-paleo template doesn't lack any essential nutrient, in fact is loaded with them. I'm more skeptic on what might happen with people that'd avoid some kind of food in order to remain strictly ketotic. I bet they would attempt an undercaloric, ketotic approach to the epi-paleo because, well, it's the most touted here in this forum, but mainly because it's almost always given in a context of a very low redox potential for people that carries many health issues. In this case, I think it's pretty consistent with what they need.

But we are talking about athletes here. You can adopt a slightly less strict epi-paleo template with good success I think. Heck, even Jack says that when the light cycles are the longest and you show most signs of leptin sensivity and the season allows for it it's fine to have some seasonal fruit and carbs as long as yo focus on the best choices (see garlics, onions, Brussels sprouts, beets...), nuts are also pretty high in carbs with useful resistant starch... that's the approach I currently follow and I'm usually between 90 to 200 gr of these kind of carbs a day, it's lower than the common sports science would recommend but I think it can be just fine in the context of a good diet with plenty of good fats and aminoacids (as you said) and a decent engine.

Yet, as you also pointed out, that fact that you can do it doesn't necessarily mean that it might be optimal from a longevity standpoint. More than if you can perform magic on the PPP, actually what I'd like to get a definitive answer is regarding if it can be done safely at all in some way or another. My N=1, I tried many of the biohacks that Barry and Ben and others do regularly, as well as some CT and many of the epi-paleo biohacks... I think it can be doable somehow in that context, everything helps, but... heck, you need to nail every single detail in order to maximize your recovery... To be honest, I recently switched my focus a lot more into strength training and walking, as I came from here from the start, and reduced my running a lot... and I must admit things are way easier this way :/, which makes me question a lot of things about what is what I do and what is what I should do. I suppose in the end, it's up to you to make what makes you happier and to know the caveats.

My focus should then be on optimising what I already have, and treat interventions like cellular nutrient flooding as mere "coping mechanisms" to tie me over the hump.

\---
**Random musings**: what Jack wrote above sounds pretty similar to the principles that a person like Tim Noakes talks about when he mentions the Central Governor Theorem ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central\_governor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_governor)). If it really is the fact that energetic inefficiency is the limiting factor to athletic performance, then it makes sense that CMRO2 is the true bottleneck, and not muscle-local nutrient load.

Note that Noakes' views on the amount of water that you need to consume (a lot less than conventional wisdom) also seems to gel with the notion that our mitochondria can "recycle" water -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mitochondria-produce-water-hydration-by-exercise.9543/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mitochondria-produce-water-hydration-by-exercise.9543/)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-26T03:54:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-117994>

When you go from water to protons.......you wind up ironically at proteins next. That is the next scale of learning. Proteins >>> DNA.

Ribosomes are where proteins are made and huge amounts of "protons go to die". The nucleolus is crucial to cell proliferation, with a primary role in processing and assembling ribosomes. Beyond this role, other studies have revealed a more dynamic character with a diversity of proteins passing in and out of the nucleolus. Many feel it is currently unclear what controls or drives chromosomal and protein movement in and out of the nucleolus during the cell cycle and/or in response to stress. According to recent ubiquination data it is the redox state of proteins life cycle is a key issue. So when you are looking to squeeze performance.........you best have a peak at this linkage at some point.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-26T20:21:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-118099>

Proteins are the key: Performance and muscles are linked. When muscles go bad the person is said to suffer from sarcopenia. It turns out older people get it too. Old and broken people also have poor redox potential. Sarcopenia seems to primarily occur through a loss of muscle cell size rather than number. this means the number of proteins within the muscle cell shrinks. The cells seem to adjust in size through loss of organelles, cytoplasm, and proteins in such a way as to preserve the size of the nuclear domain, the cell domain surrounding the muscle nuclei. Thus, loss of muscle size occurs through loss of nuclei within the cell. And, we know that nuclei are lost when motorneurons fail to signal the nuclei. Disuse, oxidative damage to the neurons or neural plate junction at the muscle, inflammatory damage, or atrogene expression are factors in the loss of nuclei of muscle cells.

### Barry — 2014-05-04T20:07:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-125066>

Race Update

100km, Tuscany Crossing, 3rd, 11.5hrs.

Day Before: LCHF

Before Race: Fasted, just coffee

My Key to Peak Performance/Health

I've looked at everything in terms of nutrition, physiology, training etc in terms of whats needed to optimize efficiency and endurance for events such as ultra marathons. However, I now have a sense when I am going to perform at my best and how I am going to do in a race. Its not how many grams of MCT, EPA, DHA or D-Ribose I've consumed and its not how many miles, reps or drills I've done. I now put it down to simply how "connected" I feel. This is something I can now sense when set off from the starting line and its something I can dip in and out of as I go along. The races I;ve run or performed well in , are all races in which I felt this connection. And vice versa, when I haven't performed well, this connection was not felt.

So what is this "connected" feeling ? Very difficult to describe but I do think it relates to many of the topics that have been discussed here and in Jacks blogs. If you want your redox potential, circadium rythm, magnetism, PPP system, zero entropy and electrons to flow... then you need to be connected with nature, your environment and yourself. In order to do that... you need to follow the laws of nature and you need to harmonize everything together.

Apart from running crazy distances and trying to win... I think this concept applies to general health too. The more connected you become, the better you become.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-06T02:32:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-125375>

How funny, I was just watching at the 1st week lesson from [this course about the brain](https://class.coursera.org/neurobio-001/) and did a quick look at the Wikipedia for myelin and found this:

"**Myelin repair**
Research to repair damaged myelin sheaths is ongoing. Techniques include surgically implanting [oligodendrocyte precursor cells](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oligodendrocyte_precursor_cell) in the central nervous system and inducing myelin repair with certain [antibodies](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antibodies). While results in mice have been encouraging (via [stem cell](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stem_cell) transplantation), whether this technique can be effective in replacing myelin loss in humans is still unknown.[\[8\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myelin#cite_note-pmid14702638-8) [Cholinergic treatments](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parasympathomimetic_drug), such as [acetylcholinesterase inhibitors](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acetylcholinesterase_inhibitor) (AChEIs), may have beneficial effects on myelination, myelin repair, and myelin integrity. Increasing cholinergic stimulation also may act through subtle trophic effects on brain developmental processes and particularly on oligodendrocytes and the lifelong myelination process they support. By increasing [oligodendrocyte](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oligodendrocyte) cholinergic stimulation, [AChEIs](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acetylcholinesterase_inhibitor), and other cholinergic treatments, _such as [nicotine](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotine), possibly could promote myelination during development and myelin repair in older age_.[\[9\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myelin#cite_note-pmid17070782-9) [Glycogen synthase kinase 3β](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GSK3B) inhibitors such as [lithium chloride](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lithium_chloride) have been found to promote myelination in mice with damaged facial nerves.[\[10\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myelin#cite_note-pmid22355115-10)"

### Dali Dula — 2014-05-06T16:21:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-125482>

Due diligence......
Liu took red blood cells from people and treated them in the laboratory with glucose and nicotine at various concentrations.

To measure the effects of the nicotine on the levels of blood sugar, he used the hemoglobin A1c blood test. This test measures the average blood sugar control for the previous three months or so.

The higher the test results, the more uncontrolled the blood sugar is.

Liu used doses of nicotine comparable to what would be found in the blood of smokers. The levels of nicotine he used in the lab would correspond roughly to the exposure a smoker would get by [smoking](http://www.webmd.com/smoking-cessation/default.htm) one or two packs a day, he says.

He found that the nicotine raised the HbA1c level by nearly 9% to up to 34.5%, depending on nicotine exposure.

The study was funded internally, Liu says.

**Nicotine and Blood Sugar: Second Opinion**
The study results about nicotine and blood sugar make sense, says Peter Galier, MD, attending physician and former chief of staff at Santa Monica--UCLA Medical Center & Orthopaedic Hospital. "I was always under the impression that nicotine was the culprit," Galier says. He reviewed the study findings for WebMD.

''What the study is telling us is that nicotine is most likely the reason smokers have elevated HbA1c levels," says Galier, an associate professor of medicine at the University of California Los Angeles David Geffen School of Medicine.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-07T02:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-125564>

Apparently nicotine is a potent liberator of fatty acids from fat cells (both brown and white apparently), and can even activate UCP1. Kiefer in particular cautions that high carb together with nicotine is a bad combination, because nicotine can cause a temporarily elevation in triglycerides, which in the presence of high blood glucose from carb consumption, can be a very bad thing for diabetes in particular.

Kiefer also notes that the therapeutic range for nicotine is around 7-8ng/mL in the blood, which is achieved by chewing a 4mg piece of nicotine gum. Even nicotine patches only go as high as 20mg. This is a ways off the 60-80mg of nicotine that a smoker would get. The researchers did note that high blood glucose risk scaled with nicotine dose, so one should probably stick to the 4mg dosage recommendations. Finally, I don't think we should treat this as a life-long drug, and just use it for 2-4 weeks at a time.

To me, it sounds like nicotine can be used as a great aid when trying to restore your redox potential. It doesn't add electrons to your system, but allows the body to tap into its own pathways to obtain more energy. I could definitely see nicotine + CT being a great combo.

### ssj3 — 2014-06-03T01:48:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-129392>

This reads very similar to my experience.

I had survived for years on a keto diet (eventually a story for my optimal journal to put in context) but not when supporting high volume/intensity/density weight training. But when I switched to a keto diet again, I didn’t adjust for many factors (loss of calories specifically one - I actually wanted to test the calories don’t matter spin) and training really suffered. Also, my labs tanked, couldn’t sustain let alone add muscle, body comp worsened and I was starting to have to take days off due to poor recovery.

Now about 8 months in, consuming a lot more fat, a few more carbs (about 50-70 grams a day) and about 800-1000 calories per day more, things are starting to turn good. Leaner with weight up about 4kg. Now I don’t know if this is just the calories as I did start doing other things like eating oysters or muscles nearly daily, infrared therapy, 8-10 hours of sleep, etc.

Still some bio-hacking to do as recovery and ability to maintain intensity still needs to improve, but I like where this is going.

### Barry — 2014-06-04T19:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-129654>

Low Carb and even duration are just small parts of the jigsaw here. I've mentioned this before but its not about what you eat, its about how you live. Then the inner workings of the cell are found in the quantum physics as opposed to the biology or physiology. I don't know all the theory but I do know some and I know more about the practice.

So if a ketotic state is optimal for performance - neocortical neurons, electron flow, photons etc...... what I'm saying is that the practice is far more difficult to apply than the theory

here's a post from another thread where I discuss this:
_

Context 4: elite athlete, competing not just for fun but to win, at a high standard, national/world class level

Do they need PPP and can they access it ?

The first part of this question , I'm still on the fence. We simply have nothing to go on other than theory. Am I in PPP and winning races ? I can't say.... I'm winning some races but so is the dude on a SAD diet. If Jack starts winning world championship events, at any sport, in PPP and epi-paleo/CT etc, even if its N=1, then I'll buy into it more. But for now, and I;m not a reductionist, all we have is a hypothesis.
Are olympic gold medallists, marathon world record holders, Tour de France champions etc.... are these type of athletes in PPP ? nowhere near.... and I work in this game so I personally know a lot about these type of athletes. Many of them wouldn't even know what a ketone is. However, what I will say, is that this level of athlete, with their type of living and training, genetics and physiology..... are upregulating and activating many of the pathways that are being discussed here without them even knowing it.

Finally, the second part, possibly the main subject of the debate, can these type of athletes access it ? .... .... as I mentioned, this PPP system still needs more study both theoretically and clinically. Lets assume it does work and can be accessed.

Well, it comes down to what was mentioned in sjoshua's post. Everything needs to be in smooth working order. The right dots need to be in place, the dots need to be connected, and then everything has to work in harmony.

So lets say you're an elite athlete, going to the next world championships or olympic games. First up, you have to travel to get to the destination. Flights, EMF's, maybe poor food. You then are put up in a hotel. More EMFs, maybe poor sleep, no perfect keto/epi-paleo foods available, no where near grass, mountains or sea. Outcome - semiconductors, inner mitchondrial membranne, electron flow, cell signalling, etc etc... downregulated........... what happens ? you simply don't feel as well and don't perform as well.

It all comes down to a very simple thing. The real world is not set up for optimal living. Perfection is not achievable. Therefore embrace the imperfection. Don't try to be the best, just try to be better. Apply that to everything in life.

People here are worrying themselves about something that possibly doesn't work in the muscle cell from a energy production point of view, and if it does, they don't actually need it, but more importantly, it is practically unattainable in the modern world.

I'm off for a beer............_

I like the last paragraph:
_Our 7-million-year evolutionary path was dominated by
two seasonal challenges—calorie scarcity and mild cold stress.
In the last 0.9 inches of our evolutionary mile, we solved them
both. Refrigeration and transportation have fundamentally
changed the food to which we have access and the environ-
ments in which we live. We also sleep less and are exposed to
considerably more artificial light, particularly in the winter
months. Obesity and chronic disease are seen most often in
people and the animals (pets) they keep warm and over-
nourished. Similar to the circadian cycle and like most other
living organisms, it is reasonable to believe we also respond to
the seasons and carry with us the survival genes for winter.
Maybe our problem is that winter never comes._

### sjoshua — 2014-06-24T20:48:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-132057>

> [colt said:](/goto/post?id=131412)
>
> Wait a minute…….Doc explain this quick. Whats the mechanism? Whats the protocol? How often?
>
> _**I have an idea for you Barry to increase performance.......try a nicotine patch right before the race. It is a trick I used with one of the NFL players I did some work with an injury.**_

The mechanism is bending cellular time slightly. You're tricking your cells into working a little faster than normal by changing their reality. (similar to overclocking a computer - it was still designed to work at the native speed, but if you can keep the entropy to a minimum and control the environment the semiconduction is occurring in, then you can achieve faster processing from the same device)

This will only work if your cells can handle the bend though.... i.e. you need a healthy redox

The protocol is simply attaching the patch to the skin to deliver the nicotine slowly. The only time you'd want to do this is when in a race-type environment where you are on the clock... cause, you are 'overclocking' your cells.

### Barry — 2014-07-21T13:59:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-135063>

I'm fasting from technology as much as I am from food these days... I think the adaptations are actually greater...

But I'll try to also keep this thread going :

Race Update: A mountain ultramarathon, 73Km, 5000m climb. Race start: Midnight

So a couple of things to share here. This was a relatively "short" race for me but with the midnight start, and big mountain climbs with a finish at a ski resort, its a hard race.

Race Day:

First thing, early morning, fasted, I ran 30mins barefoot, then sat down in a river, not CT, its hot summer now, but more for grounding. I ate 3 meals before this race, no snacks, Breakfast was eggs. lunch was suasage/bacon and dinner was fatty pork chops/veggies. Took a 45min nap in the evening , in the garden on the grass. Brewed a fresh coffee at 11pm... race start then at 12pm.

It rained. Not cold. Its Italy. Just a proper summer storm, thunder/lightening, heavy downpours. I wore just shorts and a t-shirt. I ran the first few hours easy, no hunger or need to eat. Despite the time , darkness and running during what are "sleeping" hours, I felt great. Rain continued but the more I got soaked, the better I felt. As we ran through the forests and mountain trail, I even made sure to put my hands through as much of the greenery as I could to wet myself even more.

Reached the 40km mark and the first signs of daylight appeared. However, due to the weather and visability, the high peaks were deemed too dangerous and the course was re-routed. This reduced the distance to 55km and also missed out on the big mountain sections. I ran then full gas for the remaining 15km. Arrived at the finish in 10th, in just over 7hrs, but only minutes behind the next 4-5 athletes. Given another 15-20km with more difficult terrain and I felt like I would have been very close to a podium spot. During this 7hrs of running, I ate very little, some pieces of fruit, some cheese/cold meat, some coffee and just water... no gels, sports drinks or bars.

Always difficult to pin-point what makes your performance better.... but my take-outs from this are - fasting, earthing/grounding, fats and rain/electrons

### Barry — 2014-08-13T16:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-137755>

Truth Bomb Alert !

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25089149](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25089149)

and here something I've posted on this thread before which puts in the context of athletic performance:

_"Exercise is simple, and the body is clever. Our physiology is not intelligent, it is purely responsive.
I have been learning and experiencing the true meaning of these statements the more and more I practice what I do. You see, we now live in a world where modern technology, science, medicine etc has developed ways to essentially inhibit the human bodies natural responses. This lies true for both general health and fitness. I won't get into the health aspect but in terms of the fitness aspect, just think about the following.

We now have sports drinks and sports foods which are supposed to improve our performance and delay fatigue. However, if you've been following this thread, you'll know that food, in particular carbohydrates, eaten before or even during exercise downregulates fat oxidation, switches off PPP and inhibits ketosis. Yet, the world has been brainwashed into thinking that you cannot exercise unless you are fully carb loaded, constantly consume food during and constantly consume fluid during. In terms of hydration, I applying almost the same strategy I do with food. In other words, let the adaptation occur by allowing your physiology to respond. It doesn't respond if you are constantly drinking and constantly taking in electrolytes. You'll find a good explanation of this if you look up Prof Tim Noakes. He has shown that we can completely self regulate our own hydration, balance our sodium levels and self control our own hydration by drinking to thirst.

The above point holds true for many other things in sport and life. We have heart rate monitors and GPS watches to track our distance,splits, HR zones etc. We have compression garments to "prevent" muscle fatigue and recovery powders to help us "recover". We then have "ice baths" , sleep machines, stress monitors and apps that tell you when you can train. The list goes on. In my opinion, and this is an important point, its all a load of BS.

My reasoning is simple. Our bodies are clever. Let them be clever. This is the key point. It ties in with practically everything Jack is writing about. In order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate. If you want fat adaptation, keto adaptation, PPP activation, semiconductor functioning, electron flow, cell signalling, timing and quantum biology all singing and all dancing... then this is what you need to do, remove the inhibitors i.e. modern foods, modern devices and modern living. EMF's, carbs, blue light, heat, sports foods, sports monitoring devices and machines. Instead, let the body do the work."_

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-04T03:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-139864>

If you believe Tim Noakes and his Central Governor theorem, then you will also see that optimal performance and optimal health are one and the same. Here's a link to a recent short paper in 2012 where Noakes lays out his thoughts -- [http://bjsm.bmj.com/content/46/1/1.full](http://bjsm.bmj.com/content/46/1/1.full)

And if you believe the previous sentence, then every single sport will be improved by improving brain energetics, and can be done while eating a ketogenic diet.

With explosive type activities, you will never be able to exhaust the muscles, so the outcome is determined by nervous system health. Example: Olympic lifters rarely miss a lift because of strength limits. A guy who can snatch 140kg can probably overhead squat at least 160kg, and front squat way in excess of 200kg. They miss because they "mis-grooved" the lift -- in other words, nervous system not working optimally => improved by ensuring brain energetics are optimal.

As for the building of the strength base (max voluntary contraction with ability to control both concentric and eccentric), I think there is ample evidence to show that this can be done on any sort of diet (which would include the ketogenic diet).

With higher intensity "strength-endurance" type activities, say an 800m sprint, or one of the shorter track cycling events, or a frame carry in strongman, the common argument is that lactic acidosis stops further force production. Noakes addresses this in the article linked above, but I think it's safe to say that it's all about rate of energy production, and that we have vastly underestimated the amount of energy production possible using fat oxidation (see my note below)

With technique sports that require frequent bouts of high intensity like Basketball, I'd say athletes are limited by their ability to transfer skills practiced during training to the playing field, and by their ability to recover quickly from a bout of high intensity bursts. Again, I think it's the "mental game" that is the most critical here -- attaining the needed physical abilities is not limited by something like a ketogenic diet, and . In many ways, this is a scaled-down version of the sorts of training requirements that elite military personnel need, and these people are the ones finding great benefit from interventions like HBOT, Ketone salts, HRV training, etc ... which do more for the brain than skeletal tissue.

Longer duration Endurance activity is the domain of Noakes, and he contends that the research shows that athletes are not limited by VO2 max, and that controlling "subjective measures" like pacing and Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) seem to be the most critical factors in racing success.

And then of course, there's the research coming out of Volek and Phinney's lab now showing fatty acid oxidation rates of 1.4-1.9g among typical fat adapted athletes. (Ben Greenfield did a post about this awhile ago -- [http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2014/05/how-much-fat-can-you-burn-2/](http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2014/05/how-much-fat-can-you-burn-2/)) By pure calorie figures alone, that's 1.4 \* 9 \* 60 = 756kcal per hour, and remember that this actually produces twice as much ATP for the same equi-caloric value in carbs. In other words, it is very possible to meet almost all of your energetic needs via the beta-oxidation of fats.

The commonality in all the cases is that performance limits are not dictated by strength limits, rates of waste clearance, etc ..., and instead are subject to the ability of the brain and nervous system to recruit themselves to maximal capacity on a consistent basis. Neural limits are best surpassed by having a constant flow of electrons into a brain and nervous system that is capable of storing and using those electrons (ie: DHA + water + lots of oxygen). In this way, I can see how ketones become a superior fuel.

\---

As for myself, I remain open-minded. What I wrote above is just my own exploration on any plausible mechanism behind achieving optimal athletic performance.

The key phrase is always "if you believe A, then B will be true", and then your job will become to test if this is true in your own personal context. Most athletes are unable or unwilling to apply such tests due to everything from lack of aware, to conflicting incentives, to a destructive environmental pressures.

In information theory we always have a saying, that "if you want to double the rate at which you gain new knowledge, fail twice as fast". So for me personally, my job is to run as many of these experiments as possible. The thing is, you can't run an experiment without first having a hypothesis. A hypothesis is just a belief in something unproven, but in which you have plausible mechanism to explain. But let's always remember that it's the belief that comes first, then you discover plausible mechanism, and then you test that mechanism, and then you formalise the conclusions.

\---

I also remind myself that this journey will take awhile. Personally, I was a pretty decent High School athlete -- easily dunk a basketball on a 10' rim, deadlift double my bodyweight, sub 18min 5km run, etc ... And then I proceeded to lose a lot of those abilities when I moved out to Melbourne Australia from Singapore (at the equator). That's almost 6 years since I made the move now, and I still haven't regained most of my physical abilities from High School. Nutrition has improved dramatically since then, training is better, circadian mismatches are less than before, but the environment here in Melbourne is sapping my health and physical performance.

Only now am I about to make the move to Cairns up in far north Australia (in a few days). I made a trip to Cairns 3 months prior, and stayed for 2 weeks. This proved to have dramatic improvements to my health parameters, as well as physical performance parameters (within a week of being there, a 50km bike ride went from requiring lots of effort, to being EASY).

But it took 6 years from feeling the effects of the problem (and not knowing it), to having both the knowledge and resources to make a major pro-survival decision. That's for a young single guy with all incentives aligned to trying to fix himself. I'd imagine the road to optimal living to be much more difficult for someone like a professional athlete, who is already invested heavily into a particular lifestyle. The relevance for us is that we can't often look to existing examples for guidance on how to reach optimal performance, but instead have to rely on known mechanism (E=mc2) along with logical self experiments to reach where we want to get to. Dr Kruse provides the mechanistic explanation of various topics, and I'll help myself to my own experiments

It maintains your redox potential and improves EEG, EMG, and fMRI data when you look for it. The key for humans it improves our flow of electrons over cell membranes and in ETC. This generates large voltages everywhere in a cell. When you do this to electrons, sleep improves and the DC current in interfascial water below myelin layers and outside the axons improve. As this improves we see massive changes in EEG's. Magneto-encephalograms also show increase magnetic fluxes in white mater. This is tied to better energy transduction of water confined in microtubules of the brain leading to an eventual increase in performance across all organ systems. It takes 24-36 months to be measured in a non athlete. Dr. Robert O. Becker established that the DC current in interfascial water is the source of the regenerative current in all life forms. The DC current (electron flow) decreases as life becomes more complex. In salamander's they can regenerate totally to re-establish their performance. Becker found in humans the only true ability to regenerate that remains in us is found in bone regeneration and finger tips. Most people believe bone heals, but it actually does not, it regenerates completely. Becker proved this. He did the same in finger tips in humans. The reason behind this was the DC current was centered and collected in the brain's neocortex where DHA is found in abundance to drive complex neocortical function. Dr. Fritz Popp took his work deeper and found some rather shocking results tied to performance and to illness. When electrons in CSF interacts with DHA, a photon is given off to the brain tissue. Popp found the number of photons emitted seemed to also be linked to the organism's position on the evolutionary tree of life. This finding was similar to Becker's findings in electrons in the tree of life at the neuroepithelial junction where injury occurs. Popp found that the more complex the organism was, the "fewer photons" were emitted. Rudimentary animals, like salamander, and even plants, tended to emit 100 photons/cm2/sec at a wavelength of 200-800 nm, corresponding to a very-high-frequency electromagnetic wave well within the visible range of light. It turns out Popp found humans emit only 10 photons/cm2/sec at the same frequency of the wavelength of light. The sheer numbers are different but the frequency of the ground state of the photon emitted was not. Remember that water absorbs best in the 270-80 nm range according to Gerald Pollack's work. What increases water inside cells and microtubules in nerves and the brain? What controls muscles to improve or decrease performance? Nerves. Intracellular water is best structured when we are in ketosis and ketosis increases myelination of nerves to tranmit data quicker. A ketogenic diet provides mitochondria more electrons than any other template. The more electrons provided, the more photons are liberated to the brain to be distributed all over the body to increase performance via nerves and the myo-neural junctions. This is how it all happens.

I've been doing heavy lifting in the fasting state for about 2 years now. I originally started IF'ing following the LeanGains protocol, but earlier this year figured out that I got drastically stronger, smaller (muscles more dense, taking up less space - of course I now know this means a decrease in cellular swelling and improved redox potential!) and felt absolutely limitless energy when I dropped out carbs entirely and increased my fat intake drastically. It was a "ding ding ding" moment. What I experienced went entirely against the grain, but intuitively I knew I was on the right track. When I discovered Dr. Kruse a month ago, I started eating Epi-Paleo, and I can't begin to express how amazing I feel and how my body has changed. I have a nearly non-existent lactic acid threshold, and never experience any muscle soreness whatsoever. I look and feel twice as good at 40 as I did in my late teens and early 20's. That says it all. I literally cannot imagine feeling more energetic or sleeping any better. This is LIFE at its fullest....which means everything to me as 4 years ago I was battling breast cancer for the second time.

### Danco3636 — 2014-09-06T06:21:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-140091>

Jacks reply.....

> **Administrator**
> 1\. Glad to see your paying attention.......most of the people in paleo need to read that truth bomb you posted in question one.
> 2\. wholly depends upon your redox potential.......which you will learn about in next blog.
> 3\. If you have liver, kidney, or gut issues protein re-feeds can hurt you. Why? Ammonia is produced from dietary amino acids and by catabolism of amino acids, amines, nucleic acids, glutamine and glutamate (nitrogenous wastes) in peripheral tissues (especially skeletal muscle). Gastrointestinal micro-organisms (primarily coliforms and anaerobes in the colon and cecum) convert dietary amino acids and urea into ammonia in the gastrointestinal system. The ammonia is absorbed into the portal circulation, taken up by the liver and converted in the liver, via the urea cycle, into urea. Urea is then excreted into the gastrointestinal system (producing a futile cycle) and into the urine.
> Of the total ammonia produced, 80-90% is shunted into the urea cycle, with the remaining 10-20% metabolised by peripheral tissues, including the kidney, heart, and brain. Everything is connected. The lower your redox potential the less effective your ammonia and urea cycle become to handle protein.
> 4\. Re feeds in the 4-6 PM window are optimal. re read this blog to see why:[http://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/](http://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/)

### Barry — 2014-09-12T15:57:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-140734>

Dan and others:

All the answers are in EMF 4. If you look more closely, you will find things explained more simply

"_When timing is off molecular crowding begins to accumulate within a mitochondria and with enough time, chaos ensues in the cell to destroy cellular signaling.
Chaos in space is called entropy, and in chaos in molecular biology is called inflammation. Inflammation is positively charged, and inflammation leads to more mitochondrial inefficiency, and loss of electron chain transport efficiency. When we lose energy we lose the ability to run processes in cells_"

What causes molecular timing to be off ?? All the lifestyle and environmental factors that athletes (and others) are drowned in.... EMFs, Artificial Light, Stress, Non-Grounded etc...

When they lose electrons because of these factors, they lose energy. Now, serious/elite/full-time athletes need a lot of energy. Where's the easiest and quickest place to get this energy ? ATP-PC and Krebs Cycle..... whats the source ? Carbs

So, back to your original question... "is Epi-Paleo Ketogenic Diet still the best" regardless of environment/lifestyle ?

If you are an elite athlete, competitive, high level athlete.... that trains a lot and does big volume and intensity.... I would say NO.

Because there molecular timing will be off, so they will lose electrons and hence energy production capacity. Sure, there are all the adaptation benefits that I have written about extensively here that LCHF, Epi-Paleo, Cyclical Keto etc can bring. But a full ketotic state will not be possible to sustain.

If you work out in your garden a few times a week, go for walk, do some Movnat...... maybe a full ketotic state is possible even with molecular timing off.

But this is the key point that many guru's just don't get...... the energy requirement of a full time cyclist, triathlete, runner, crossfitter..... without getting into the argument whether these forms of exercise are good or bad..... is just so much far greater.... that the trade off between full ketotic state and molecular timing puts them in an energy deficit

I can't make it any more simpler than that.....
