### Jack Kruse — 2015-04-13T01:08:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-161034>

ketosis is a tool that basically can be used to help you for short burst of times. Its maximal window is a seasonal in length........but athletes can use it for performance windows. The key point is that ketosis is useful while you are disconnected from your main power sources but the key is turning back to those sources by altering your environment. Ketosis should be used as a tool to control carbon and nitrogen cycling in you when you are disconnected. Doing it as a lifestyle makes little sense unless you have a death wish living disconnected 100% of the time. Many endurance athletes do just that. Most don't. LCHF folks like Jimmy Moore do the same.......the difference is he is not an athlete......he is an abuser of technology that allow for his disconnection therefore he has the false belief that ketosis is 100% needed thing for him and for everyone........not true. You need the superoxide bursts to entangle electrons to make new mitochondria to function thermodynamically better for health or performance. The reconnection is via solar radiations and the Earth's magnetic field when solar radiations ar enot directly present, but indirectly present in the form or IR light stored in the Earth as heat. That heat is radiative back to things on the surface and the atmosphere nightly. An IR camera on desert rocks at night is a simple experiment. That IR heat is also what charge separates ground water we drink and plants drink.............Both plants and animals need this water to grow........photosynthetically or using ox/phos.

Solar radiation cause atoms to move apart and lack of light causes them to recondense. certain light frequencies have the innate ability to control the atomic lattice of atoms in a liquid crystal. Physicists from MIT and the University of Belgrade have developed a new technique that can entangle 2,910 atoms using only a single photon — the largest number of particles that have ever been mutually entangled experimentally in a lab. The previous lab record was 100 atoms. Life is not created or lived in a lab.......the lab of life is the ionosphere and surface of the Earth. Here light can organize, control, and uncondense atoms at different times using different laws of physics to control the process. Entanglement is usually created by direct interactions between subatomic particles. These interactions can take numerous forms. Living systems use mitochondria to bring light in the form of a stream of electrons to ECT and target electrons in transition metals to act like pincer like devices to hold oxygen in a tight electrostatic vice grip to alter its electron spin by create free radicals like superoxide and H2O2. These are only created in pulses. The electron in the free radical is entangled to the other electron that moves down ECT to ultimately join to and reduce oxygen. That O2 goes to other cells to feed its mitochondria. In this way the electron created at the mitochondria of a living thing are entangling electrons to increase connectivity or communication of cells and tissues in your body. The more entanglement we create the more health we have. The less entanglement the worse our health is. Physicists create entanglement in a lab most common by using methods called spontaneous parametric down-conversion to generate a pair of photons entangled in polarization. Entanglement is broken when the entangled particles decohere through interaction with the environment; Life requires a recycling of atoms for this reason otherwise atoms would keep their entanglement for ever. Atomic recycling is what the April webinar was about and it is a function of our environment. Our environment is a quantized ecosystem. When an entangled electron is delivered to another chemical compound the act of joining this new collection of atoms is a form of measurement in a quantum sense. In a quantize ecosystem when a measurement is made, decoherence or de-entanglement occurs. This recycling aspect or decomposition aspect to the quantized ecosystem is something elite athletes have yet to tap in my humble opinion and I just spoke to Phil Maffatone about this issue and he agrees with my take on it. Since he is the most well known coach to ultra endurance athletes on this planet..........I thought his discussions with me on this were rather interesting and he and I will continue to collaborate further where his published works left off.........since he gave up training people and took up music.

### Barry — 2015-04-13T22:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-161096>

> [Ian said:](/goto/post?id=161029)
>
> Elite athletes must be a very tiny percentage of the population. Most sports at this level is extremely dependent on natural selection - so with practically nobody involved being properly long-term keto adapted the odds of having such a person at the top of the pile is almost zero. I watched your paleotreats interview Barry but have to ask whether or not you are giving in too easily - or do you have statistics from top level 2 to 3 year adapted individuals who have lost performance due to ketosis? The other thing is that perhaps those getting high in the natural selection ladder are not those who would perform best in a keto adapted state. Amateur sports - like pro sports is also rife with doping - just today the elite Paris Roubaix race was the fastest in history - faster than in the golden age of EPO! Odd to say the least.
>
> I myself work as a coach in a sport that is extremely reliant on natural selection at an elite level - brutally so in fact. My avatar is self explanatory here.
>
> I'm now aged 56 and use endurance sport myself to keep in shape - and also because I really enjoy it. Like you I feel the Paleo attitude to endurance sport is strangely narrow minded - especially as humans are obviously spectacularly well designed for it - being able to outrun nearly every other animal on the planet. We can't out muscle a gorilla and won't get far with lion or bear in terms of power or teeth - but while they are sleeping or overheating we can keep on running. My intuition (not lab testing) tells me that all the massive adaptations endurance sport brings must add up to something pretty good overall.
>
> Personally I participate in road cycling races of up to around 200km in the mountains (French Alps) from Spring until Autumn and a typical difficult race would have between 4000m to 5000m climbing. I'm not great but starting in a line up of about 150 well trained participants of all ages I'll come somewhere in the middle. A couple of years ago I started increasing carb supplements (maltodextrine and fructose) in an effort to overcome many training and performance issues - and for the most part this worked - but despite extensive training I ended up fat - and then found the fat impossible to reduce.
>
> Reluctantly, last year I began fasting - for three months - managing 72 hour fasts every week and continuing training. I could train during the first and second days of each fast - but eventually would almost pass out, fall off the bike or have post exercise asthma attacks. Each time it took two days to recover strength and then be ready to participate in another race. Despite successfully losing weight my performance remained identical - even on the big climbs - so I obviously lost too much muscle too.
>
> The next stage (after discovering Peter Attia's website - was to go ketogenic - which I eased myself into over a couple of races. The first one properly keto was a 175km race with 4500m of climbing. All I ate for breakfast was bacon and eggs and then nothing during the race. I expected to be found lying dead at the side of the road somewhere. It tuned out to be my best performance ever on such a hard course - with the greatest improvement during the last 2 hours. Now the interesting bit is here: At this stage my resting heart rate was 37 bpm and maximum heart rate was 172 bpm - though strangely even when in ketosis I could average 168 bpm for an hour in a hill climb race - which was very confusing! When I was in my early 30s my max was 191 bpm. 6 years ago it was 178 bpm. The ketosis was started in August of last year. Since then I have kept up cycling and running here in the Alps with CT - cycling and running at altitude in a T-shirt sub zero and in falling snow (sometimes for up to 2 hours). Rather amazingly during the winter I watched my max heart rate slowly climbing - now all the way back to 191 bpm - resting rate still at 37 bpm! I've been in ketosis all this time with a stable body weight. (64 kg or 141 lb at 171cm).
>
> For me it feels like I'm in a different body and don't really understand how to use it. There's no carb buzz to give motivation - but a small amount of competitive adrenaline sorts that out. I expect to surpass later this year anything I managed when on carbs ( I could list about 20 other physical improvements due to the ketosis). My point is that I'm not an elite endurance athlete - but in every way I'm better in ketosis than out of it - after only about 8 months.
>
> I follow the Leptin Reset protocol for eating - a good breakfast which gets me though a long day on the mountain and leaves enough juice in my body for a good training session if there is time before an evening meal. I can't see why there should be an issue with this successful protocol once the Leptin sensitivity is re-established - if it works in such a negative situation then surely all the better when things are going well. My own (limited - and probably badly executed) fasting experience shows me that stressing the body with such things is not always constructive - it was three months of the worst physical performance of my life! I also have to ask if just skipping breakfast is really training in a "fasted" state. I'd probably consider "fasting" to be after about 3 days for a man - when basic ketosis kicks in automatically.

Ian

I'm working with pro's doing > 6W/Kg and 400TSS for 1hr TT's

competing at Giro, TdF and Vuelta

so thats the context

your context is very different, as I'm sure you understand...... mid pack in a amatuer 1 Day race is not the same

I'm not giving in either.... I just explained how far i have taken it.... and I alluded to what the limitations are

I'm probably just finding the specific jargon confusing here but I thought that fat was the main power source and ketones were a result of being connected to it - allowing the heart to work up to 28% more efficiently. Carbs - as we know them hardly existed before the current interglacial period and the rise of agriculture. From what I see Dr Stephen Phinney has been in ketosis for 8 years and doesn't look at all like he has a death sentence hanging over him (unlike much of the global population today) - and Dr Peter Attia looks in pretty good shape too - with no measurable visceral fat - being more of an endurance swimmer than cyclist. Has anybody actually ever died from ketosis other than diabetics? So far I've not encountered any evidence of anything other than health improvements being related to ketosis.

I assume the "performance window" referred to for athletes then covers the 24 to 36 months needed to raise the VO2 max - which still seems like quite a long time to me. I doubt whether Barry has ever had any elite athlete go through that level of adaptation - but it would be very interesting to hear if he has. (As I mentioned already any currently successful athlete

> Ian
>
> I'm working with pro's doing > 6W/Kg and 400TSS for 1hr TT's
> competing at Giro, TdF and Vuelta so thats the context your context is very different, as I'm sure you understand...... mid pack in a amatuer 1 Day race is not the same
> I'm not giving in either.... I just explained how far i have taken it.... and I alluded to what the limitations are

Actually I do a 1hr hill climb and sustain average 98% of heart rate max (Impossible? No. I can do that when ketogenic too) - that's all I can go on because I can't afford a power meter. That's the only difference between elites and amateurs (and of course more often than not the $35,000 dollars per year spent on drugs - (Jan Ulrich)) I hate to guess how far genetic doping may also have advanced too.

Over 30 years of full time professional sports coaching (Alpine Skiing) has taught me that it's a major error to pretend that honest elites are any different from anyone else or that they do anything different. If you were built like my recent Turkish ski client you wouldn't be able to finish an ultra marathon let alone win it. Factor out the "natural selection" (which is incredibly powerful and infinitely variable) and then you are not left with a whole lot of science to go on.

I'm very glad that you haven't given up trying to explore the boundaries of low carb performance - because I believe that we don't know the half of it yet.

### NeilBB — 2015-04-15T18:49:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-161301>

The major glaring common mistakes I see are these:
(1) assuming that what is athletically "normal" for person A in a particular environment should translate to person B in another environment,
(2) assuming that optimal athletic performance and long-term health are always positively correlated. They are often not, especially in a toxic environment, and
(3) failing to realize the potential tradeoffs in the zero-sum game of balance between neural optimization and muscular optimization in humans

### NeilBB — 2015-04-15T19:27:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-161304>

The original Olympic ideal in ancient Greece was for an athlete to not only be "balanced" athletically, but also to be an integrated whole of mind/body/spirit. Contrast that with today's athletic ideal where everyone is a "specialist" even to the point where whether you run/swim/whatever 500 meters or 1000 meters distance, the "experts" are completely different people. Everything in the world today is that specialized. Specialization enhances humanity as a whole--mankind is more efficient--there is a larger variety of different sized "cogs" to fit into societal machinery. But by pursuing such extremes, the individual "cogs" are less healthy, less durable, and more easily replaced. That is what I see everywhere, in athletics and in society in general. That is the price we have decided to pay for societal (and athletic) advancement. As always, there is an associated cost...

### Danco3636 — 2015-04-26T23:27:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-162494>

What does a Keto adapted Epi-Paleo athlete do for fun on their Birthday? Well here is what yours truly has done so far:
First a 30 min cold session/ swim and breath holding in the pool - 62°F.
Then some yoga and ring work in the sun for warmup.
Then coffee with the wife and open up fun gifts.
Then 1.5 hour nature, mud, fun run, climbing trees with my dog.
100 chin-ups.
Mobility, Movement/ Movnat and trampoline work.
Then another 10 minute cold pool soak.
More sun soaking.
All fasted state.
All done before noon.
Then down a Keto/ kale smoothie and oysters.
Now later meeting friends and family for a crab and seafood feast.
I look forward to the other 1/2 of the day. BOOM!

Making it a "Superhuman Life".

\- Dan (͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)
Yep 51 years young!

[87 people reached](https://www.facebook.com/pages/Warrior-in-Shape/346466831573#)

### Barry — 2015-05-02T13:20:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-163083>

There's a lot of shit talk these days....

Before... we couldn't talk as much shit. Now everyone not only thinks they're an expert but they can reach many.

People today can use internet to put themselves on pedestals.

They come out with some clever talk, put some nice pictures up, write some good stuff.... then count their "likes" and "friends"

They they claim they have "tribes" and "followers".... that they have never actually met

Then you've got advice and information not contextualized given out by people not in the trenches and/or not in the field

I question my value here now on this forum.

My context is completely different to the majority here.

Jack is trying to cure and prevent disease. I am trying to make already healthy people fitter and perform better sometimes at the expense of health.

Plus, when it comes to sports performance, Jack is essentially an armchair critic. Yeah I know , CT, redox, semiconductors, ubiquination, UCPs etc etc... I've gone through all that..... means sweet FA when you put my context around it

Then I see Jack calling others out..... the irony is amazing..... I could go through practically everything that Jack has come out with from CT to EMF... and show you other researchers and books and papers that have been saying all this shit way before Jack read his Leptin paper and saw the statue of David

If nnEMF is the biggest destroyer of our health... then why is Jack always in my news feed every time I open my facebook ? And why is there a "tribe" that understands electron flow, magnetism and light...... all sitting in on their wifi writing blogs and comments every day ? I write here once every few weeks and even that is too much for me

So there is a lot of irony and a lot of hypocrisy going on and personally I'm not into it

Yeah... I love the info and science and even debate.. but to be honest... there is only so much you really need to know. We are largely better at doing than we are at thinking. Doers succeed eventually. Thinkers keep thinking.

I'd rather be dumb and antifragile than clever and fragile.... Nassim Taleb

And writing and arguing on a forum is for cowards. The tone also is lost in translation. I'm not going to get into any personal debates here.

If you do meet me in person... things are easier to discuss... over a beer

I'm out..... for a good while maybe too

"My teaching , if that is a word you want to use, has no copyright. You are free to reproduce, distribute, interpret, misinterpret, distort, garble, do what you like, even claim authorship, without my consent of the persmission of anybody" UG Krisnamurti

### Josh — 2015-05-05T18:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-163378>

I am guessing that at the highest levels of physical performance, athletes uncouple and it is the relative efficiency of this that determines their peak performance. I would imagine that under current COE's on the planet, no top athlete lives a life that would optimize their coupled state to a level high enough to compete with someone who is willing to push themselves into an uncoupled state. A friend of mine once said to me "there are great climbers, and there are great climbers who are willing to die climbing". The risk profile that they accept is what separates them.

### Danco3636 — 2015-05-05T20:55:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-163389>

Performance over health and longevity.
But I won't give in to not having both..... Just my personal believe that one can complement the other. I can die with that.
Quality of life given our short lifespan is improtant.
But I will settle only for both in my quest for optimization.
I am a doer.

### sfast — 2015-06-04T22:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-166588>

Hey just my 2c.

I did combine the following things:

\- fasted training
\- Crossfit, Sprints, HIIT (with a gas mask)
\- cyclical keto

I did it for 2-3 years straight. I could accomplish a world record time on 100m C2 (not official because not in competition):

This was done during a regular interval session. With some proper tapering I could have rowed a bit faster.

I did row a 1:15.0 on 500m not all out. Was able to row 500m for 1:20 without even open my mouth.

Every time I mentioned was AFTER a couple sets of heavy trap bar deadlift and as the second session of the day. First session was some strength training.

Did tear my lat a couple of days before I wanted to try the official C2-Rekord for 500m. Then moved and had no C2-Rower at my new gym.

In conclusion, world record times are quite possible. I only trained rowing for half a year.

To maximize adaption for athletic purposes I believe one should do multiple short but very intense session during the fasting window. The sessions should be highly specific to your event. I saw my training as a practice and as my movement improved I could overload my body to back it up with a better physiology.

Greets from Germany
Sascha

### sfast — 2015-06-05T11:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-166676>

I did CT without knowing it. I did wear minimal clothes during the winter (most of the time T-Shirt and Shorts), but it was nowhere near what Doc K suggests.

I did an experiment with cold. I trained in a fasted state in freezing temperatures with just Shorts and Shoes (HIIT). After a couple of weeks my energy levels went through the roof. So, I really think that CT is a must for every athlete. Sadly, I have torn my calf because I overtrained and did not take my increased strength into account.

I do a weekly refeed. The extend varies. Sometimes, I have some junkfood. Quite often I just have a bowl of fruit and thats it. I refeed mostly to give me a break from all the sympathetic activation (sitting behind the desk for 8-12h/day, training multiple sessions, fasting ect.). I give myself the freedom to have ice cream with my girl.

In a couple of months, I hope to have more time and energy to conduct a n=1 with some double adaption: Heat (Sauna) and CT. I have no idea what could happen on the micro scale. I discovered quantum biology just recently but have not time and energy left to really dig deep. So, I save it for later.

### Ian — 2015-09-19T13:49:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177366>

I wasn't going to respond to the answer "Context changes everything" - but I've decided to. It's simple: Context is irrelevant.

Perhaps the best way to bring that home is that two years ago I happened to race against Greg Lemond - the most successful (non doping) American racing cyclist ever - and I beat him. He's younger than me, but now is obese, apparently has an alcohol problem and is not fit. So the point is that "endurance" is not about getting first to the end of a race or an ultramarathon which practically nobody is interested in or even wants to try (extremely small talent pool!) - it's about getting to the end of a long life and still being in good shape - something that everybody is interested in (extremely large talent pool!). That's real endurance. We can all sacrifice long term benefits for short term gain in many ways - but that's part of the game of life - not a change of context. At age 57 I could certainly outrun Barry in a 100m sprint - because I've been a professional in an anaerobic sport nearly all of my adult life - but it would only prove what we already know - that you can't be both a sprinter and a good distance runner. Even the average couch potato would probably beat Barry in a short sprint. Probably both will end up on the scrap heap at the same age. Context?

As far as "5 W/Kg" power output goes - Lance Armstrong managed 6.6 W/Kg over 38 minutes - only to be surpassed by Bjarn Riis at 6.8 W/Kg - and YES there is a real change of context here - it's called DOPING! The reality is that I've seen guys in local amateur competitions in the Alps who would potentially leave Armstrong standing on a climb - with phenomenal power to weight ratios - but they as so small and skinny that they could never survive a Grand Tour! Context?

I have to laugh to myself when Barry snaps that "It's not a dietary system" - then goes on about his HFLC diet on his own blog. You are only going to be in ketosis if you either fast or eat HFLC and as this changes your metabolic pathway there's no reason why not to refer to this as a system - or systems - dietary and/or metabolic.

Personally I happen to play the violin, coach Alpine skiing and participate in endurance sport - each being a different category. All of those activities have their "elite" but there is no context change within each category. The greatest violin teacher of all time was Leopold Mozart - father of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. Nobody thinks of Mozart playing violin - but it's what made him great - because the scales he learned to hear were not the mathematical logarithmic "equal temperament" scales that destroy our ability to really hear music today. The irony is that you don't have to be "elite" to learn this - it's how the human ear works. One man who understood this nonsense regarding "elite" status was Sinichi Suzuki - of Suzuki violin fame. His schools had over 300,000 students at one point. He called it "Talent Education" because we all have the talent - just not the right teachers! His examples of taking apparently "retarded" individuals and training them to be top echelon concert violinists is stunning!

In Alpine Skiing I found a similar scenario to Suzuki. The elite racing level is formed through pure natural selection and natural adaptation and the coaching is extremely dumb. Ironically it can all be correctly explained through basic Newtonian mechanics - which unfortunately very few people really understand. For most of my life I have seen the industry turn good strong athletic people into wrecks - or confused mediocrity at best - leaving only a few "naturally selected" standing. Just think about poor old Michael Schumacher! I was skiing less than 100 yards from his location the day of his accident. When I trained Damon Hill for a week (with his son Josh) he was so astonished that he asked if he could recover all the money he'd wasted on being taught rubbish for 40 years. For the fist time in his life he could relate the sport to his F1 driving and motorbike racing experience. Suddenly - there was no artificial "context" barrier. Even completely different "categories" have enormous crossover.

Yes I did have to - at a later age - get my own skiing performance up to elite levels. However, like Suzuki, I could now get almost anyone to that level quite effectively. The mystique and bravado behind the "elite" image is mostly induced by ego, fear and ignorance. The very best however are noticeable in how humble they remain.

As far as the criticism levelled at Jack Kruse as being an "armchair critic" - well, sorry, I don't buy it. The fact is that due to "crossover" mastery is not specific. Mastering anything - whether is is painting or running - will train you to observe and learn and be open to much more. Mastery is about mastering yourself not anything external - so the context doesn't matter.

Anyway - what I really wanted to know (originally) was why Jack appears to make out that being permanently in ketosis is a problem - but also states that 24 to 36 months is necessary to fully adapt - and I'd love an answer to this apparent contradiction in plain English. (Look I studied quantum mechanics - but can we just leave that aside for a moment please? I'm not convinced it affects anything at a macro behavioural level. I can also prove that Einstein was wrong about relativity - but that won't get us anywhere here.) Health-wise I'm finding long term ketosis fantastic. Endurance performance-wise I'm still not sure - it's hard to separate what effects are caused by age and what are caused by other factors.

### Ian — 2015-09-20T23:22:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177461>

Thanks for that Optimalbound. It's more of an insult - but borderline... "Your views are stupid and foolish because you are clueless!" In my book "clueless and "invalid" are not quite the same.

"Abusive _ad hominem_ usually involves attacking the traits of an opponent as a means to invalidate their arguments. Equating someone's character with the soundness of their argument is a logical fallacy."

Personally I don't care because I'm just having fun. I found Jack Kruse's book really good (much better than the website!) and genuinely original. It's the "factor X" that clinches it for me. I live beside and work on glaciers and his book made a very positive difference to my life. The stunning thing is that most people don't get the fact that we are still in an ice age. They are all ranting on about "Anthropogenic Global Warming" and yet the Earth temperature is far lower than average. We are just in a temporary interglacial warm period (which was even warmer in medieval times) and we right at the end of it. Our ice age has been going on for 2.8 million years. The normal geological terminology is confusing because it was developed historically.

Here's a picture of the glacier water I use for Cold Thermogenesis....

### Ian — 2015-09-22T14:02:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177557>

It's not about "problems"!

Sometimes seeking improvement in sport yields insight simply into how to lead a healthier life. Whether chronic stress comes from how you deal with a bad boss or how you train it gives exactly the same result. Dr Phil Maffetone expresses this phenomenally well - despite his books being full of technical errors at a scientific level. He trained some of the most successful elite (Ironman) athletes in history (36 Olympic golds and World Champs in his stable) and yet he uses exactly the same program for me, you or anybody else. It enhances health. Let's face it there are countless millions of people actually damaging their health through poor sports practices (40% of runners are injured at any one time) - so the issues of health and sport cannot be untangled - especially as they are often doing the sport to maintain health in the first place. Unfortunatley you simply cannot run off that extra bagel.

### Ian — 2015-09-22T14:30:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177558>

All I'm saying is that anybody who differentiates between "elites" and anyone else is wrong - very wrong. The same nonsense in academic circles prevents progress in all directions - in this case it's usually jargon and mathematics that's used to bamboozle. It's very easily done. We use jargon to hide behind (yes, I also have a masters degree in engineering). It takes real intelligence to express clearly without jargon and mathematical bullshit.

All our friend Barry is doing here is eating a LCHF diet, running and avoiding breakfast on the day. That's it! He's obviously doing it well and more people should try it. However Maffetone has been telling people to do this since the 1970s. Perhaps he wouldn't have cut out the breakfast - but big deal.

To quote from his own article: "Today we are drowning in knowledge and starving of wisdom. The knowledge then (which we seem to now have too much of) comes in two forms. The first type of knowledge is more of a way of doing things that we cannot really express clearly but that we end of doing well. The second type is what you acquire through education, at school and university, what is explainable, rationalizable and provable. The problem is that today we rely too much on the second type of knowledge, almost obey this type of knowledge and dismiss the natural innate first type. Yet, and there are many examples of this, ironically it is often the first type that actually works in real life and ends up being “right”.

He is unwittingly confessing here that he's clueless about what is really going on - but that it's fine to define his success away as being "elite" - and happily proceed to call anyone bringing this into question "clueless, stupid and foolish" - apparently due to lack of academic knowledge. He's right about one thing - a shortage of wisdom - but he might want to put himself first in this category too.

### Ian — 2015-09-24T23:41:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177819>

You obviously don't know what at troll is Barry - but then in your case apparently it means just another insult to throw that you can add to your collection. Let me see: Stupid, Foolish, Clueless, Troll. Any more you would like to add? Might be worth while getting your hormone balance checked out though - too much running perhaps! Good luck with your racing - your own blog and nutrition business etc.

Jack - if Phil Maffetone is one of your friends then I'm impressed. He's a great man. His book is a bit dodgy - but that's totally forgiveable as he cuts straight through the nonsense and gets results regardless.

You stated on an earlier post here that staying full time in ketosis is a "death wish" and that it disconnects you from your main energy source. You say ketosis should be seasonal. However - when we slip out of the 12,000 year interglacial period and back into the full glacial mode then we won't have seasons like we do today. Your factor X concept seems to apply more logically over a far longer time scale. The glacial periods are around 100,000 years currently. People such as traditional Inuit would probably never have come out of a ketogenic state. Perhaps that has been the same for most of humanity through time.
I'm concerned about the accuracy of the statement "main energy source" implying carbohydrates as it looks like fat is the real main energy source - so ketosis is right on the button. As you know the only macronutrients we really need are fat and protein and the body makes all the carbs required. The ketones enter the metabolic process through an alternative route - not glycolysis (anaerobic) and then pyruvate - which is the carb channel. We only have a small amount of carbs available at any time - no matter how we get them - so it doesn't look like this is the main energy source in any respect - despite most medical texts showing that it apparently is. If anything the only good reason for eating carbs is to put fat on to see you through a lean winter. Even then it is probably only relevant in a specific survival scenario. Considering that stuffing yourself with carbs shuts down your fat metabolism for weeks on end - letting you pile on the fat - then it's not a rapid adaptation or one we are very good at. Certainly year round carb eating is not clever - but why should the same rule apply to year round ketosis?

From what I have seen regarding human anatomy (and I'm not a medical type) we are superbly designed running machines. It seems that we have the capacity to outrun almost all other animals over distance. This is the sort of ketogenic endurance performance that Maffetone is aiming for. In contrast the Paleo types are big on HIIT training and seem to be very negative about endurance. I've read some really silly things about HIIT - for example - that the very high lactic acid levels induced cause a fully automatic development of the aerobic system and nothing else is required for endurance. I can see that if they limit this to short infrequent sessions it would still be healthy to a point - but it wouldn't create real endurance. The interdependence of the aerobic and anaerobic systems ensures limitations. If you have a high anaerobic capacity you simply cannot have endurance - and likewise the best endurance athletes cannot sprint fast. We all know that - even if it's just because we see it at every sports meet. Maffetone prescribes the exact opposite solution for endurance and makes far more sense - he works as free from anaerobic by-products as is possible (except the pyruvate) - which is the direct link with health and stress reduction (both physical and emotional). Diet of course is king here.

What I'm saying really is that when biased arguments are removed the evidence appears to show that ketosis is our fundamental state and if anything should be "full time" then that's probably it. Sport is just useful in clarifying this issue as it shows us more clearly what works and what doesn't for the body. If like Dr Peter Attia you are an accomplished distance swimmer but eating carbs causes you to bloat like a balloon despite a phenomenal work rate - then there is a problem. In your case you got fat too and cured it just by covering yourself with ice (and probably eating LCHF) - but all part of the same picture.

I get the feeling you don't like Jimmy Moore for some reason - but his books are fine. I wouldn't let any personal feelings interfere with judgement on those issues though. The point is that real science is about disproving your best theories - not proving them. Instead of going "Paleo" or "Ketogenic" it should be about finding out what really works and trying to fault your own theories not protect them. What's sad is that there is so little real research and so little known.

I seem to have interpreted your personal views as being a bit anti "endurance" exercise - but this article here in [Runner's World](http://www.runnersworld.com/newswire/ultrarunners-have-longer-telomeres-which-is-good) links to data showing that ultrarunners have on average tellomers 11% longer than the average person - showing a 16 year younger biological age. It might be slightly biased of course coming through a running magazine. Perhaps their tellomers would be even longer if they were ketogenic runners - which it's pretty certain they weren't. This goes back to why I previously wrote that "context" here regarding health and sport is irrelevant.

For those still convinced about the nonsense of "Elite is Different" then have a read of [The Talent Code](http://thetalentcode.com/book/) which does a beautiful job of showing how talent is not innate - and not different. In France, where I live, the pro cyclists avoid amateur races now (they used to frequently use them for training) because the doping is so high even in amateur sport that the competition is now too much - I guess they can't afford to be clean and then beaten by a non-elite amateur!

Where "The Talent Code" however won't extend its reach is into epigenetics. There is no doubt that the real keys to adaptive change lie in epigenetics. It's a shame they are still only scratching the surface with this new science because it will surely change everything. It's pretty obvious that epigenetic changes early on in life can set up children to excel - as well as work in the other direction to cause disability and many terrible diseases.

### Ian — 2015-09-25T00:33:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177821>

Meet Darwin.. my Cold Thermogenesis buddy! He watches over us from his home here when we get into the glacier stream - but seems quite happy to just watch and warn us if a vulture flies overhead. Considering he's going very soon to spend at least 6 months hibernating with his brown fat to keep him warm at 6000ft altitude I guess Cold Adaptation is not an issue for him. I'm a bit worried because he's not as fat as he should be and we had a strangely long heatwave this summer which might have stopped him putting on enough fat. The mountains here are all formed by glaciers so I guess the marmottes just get pushed down the mountain when the glaciers are growing. Considering my country of origin - Scotland (further north) - was under a kilometre of ice only 15,000 years ago it might also be the opposite - only the mountain tops are free of the ice sheets! I wonder how the 7 billion population will be fed when this returns - as it will - sooner rather than later.

### Ian — 2015-09-26T18:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177932>

> [[email redacted] said:](/goto/post?id=177908)
>
> Ian wrote
>
> _"There is no doubt that the real keys to adaptive change lie in epigenetics. It's a shame they are still only scratching the surface with this new science because it will surely change everything. It's pretty obvious that epigenetic changes early on in life can set up children to excel - as well as work in the other direction to cause disability and many terri_ble diseases."
>
> I find this very interesting and I wonder if you could expand on that a bit. I am of a kind of similar opinion but I kind of wonder what exactly you mean. So if you care to address it a bit more I would be interested for sure. Also do you think anything can be done about it? Can some of these "many terrible diseases" be 'reverserd' or at least ameliorated in some way?

I don't really feel well enough informed to comment too far on this. I've just started a specific book on the subject, surprisingly called "Epigenetics" but the author has just spent a whole chapter writing about the history of Alchemy so I'm not sure where he's going!

It seems to me that Epigenetics is precisely the core of the message that Jack Kruse himself is trying to put across. Our genes appear to be influenced by their environment. Obviously that's at a cellular level - but that clearly extends to everything outside the body too.

I think it is in Dr Permutter's "Grain Brain" book where he writes about coffee - how it activates genes which cause a massive production of antioxidants. One other Dr was describing how on diagnosing an unborn fetus with Down's Syndrome he was able to change the nutrient environment of the mother's womb and so correct the gene expression of the fetus - resulting in the birth of a completely healthy baby. The point here is that I'm pretty sure that condition wouldn't be reversible at a later stage - I'm sure it can only be prevented during development.

It wouldn't surprise me if most autoimmune diseases and many chronic conditions - including autism - are reversible and are functions of genetic expression.

From what I've seen so far (and it's very little) just about everything we do is affecting gene expression. Perhaps one day we will have printed on the side of a can of beans a list of genes that get switched on and off if we eat it.

I think that a ketogenic diet supported with CT and aerobic exercise (and a few cups of coffee) is probably changing my own DNA (expression) quite considerably. It has certainly changed my phenotype considerably.

I'll be trying to read as much as I can on this subject soon so if I see anything interesting I'll report it. (Currently examining WW2 and reading a book by Sukorov - a top Soviet intelligence officer. It's called Icebreaker.) If you want any book references let me know. I also have Lipton's "Biology of Belief " to get through.

### Ian — 2015-09-27T16:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177975>

> [b612 said:](/goto/post?id=177935)
>
> Yes. It's precisely between the ski resorts of La Plagne and Les Arcs in Savoie. The glacier is on the Les Arcs side of this valley. Beyond that glacier is the more famous resort of Val d'Isère where I work in the winter.

Ah that's two hours far from where I live.
I usually ski in Chamrousse. Shame on me but I'm just a beginner.\[/quote\]
Amazing - neighbours - but writing through a forum on the other side of the planet!
If you ski in Chamrousse then you must live in or around Grenoble. I drove past Chamrousse a couple of times this year.

There's no shame in being a "beginner"! It's a great place to be. After over 30 years of teaching I'm more in a "beginner's mind" than at the start. I recently started a series of detailed explanations on skiing - [starting from complete beginner](http://www.skiinstruction.blogspot.fr/p/deep-snow.html) - but using principles that are elusive to most professionals. It's those principles that count not the "level". Work on those things (which you can do by yourself) and you will get a lot out of your skiing. There's no better environment and activity in the world when it's supported with the right principles (which is extremely rare though). I have about another 40 video clips to put up to show how this all progresses but currently find it hard to get down to doing the job. If you learned in a "snowplough" then you will see straight away why this is so different. I'm doing the actual demos here.

Have you been throwing yourself into cold streams and ponds too? You have the Vercors there as well as the Alps so lots of choice for fresh, cool water. I checked with the local Mayor's office regarding domestic water supply treatment and found out that nobody at all knew how it was sterilised!!!! They put me on the phone to the head of the private water company and he told me they use chlorine gas. We all know that this is the same sort of gas as used in warfare. The Mayor's office then denied responsibility saying it was the job of another department! I found it hard to keep my sense of humour at that point. Soaking in a bath filled with chlorine is not too appealing - and neither is watering the garden plants (soil biome) or drinking the stuff. It's such a shame that we live in the mountains and have such pure water naturally but the idiots do this to it. They could use UV or ozone to purify the water without using toxic chemicals. Chlorine gas produces something like 500 toxic chemical by-products in the water - including dioxin, formaldehyde and chloroform. That's why I take the risk of going into the glacier streams - choosing streams not too close to pastures where farm animals might be.

### Ian — 2015-09-27T22:12:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-177986>

The instruction you have found is completely standard - it's what all professionals are trained to teach. Unfortunately - beyond the first session - it's also totally wrong if you want to actually get anywhere and be safe. Just read through the link I posted and you should get the idea by the end of that alone. I explain there how all the basic mechanics in that teaching is completely incorrect - and force people into limitations. It's even worse for women (morphology) and makes it impossible to protect yourself against injury. In formal classes only around 10% ever continue after the first standard lesson - and they all mistakenly blame themselves. However - you need to be a thinker to follow the options I present as it's all 100% counter intuitive. I don't think you'd be here on the Jack Kruse forum though if that was an issue!

You aren't in the same part of Isère as I'm in so perhaps your water is chlorine free - however the statement that "water in the Isère (department)" is chlorine free is certainly not true. Might want to check with your local administration - and speak to Veolia - the water company if they are being used. Even worse it appears that over 30% of salt used in France has fluoride added - so it will be in many products (at least it isn't in the water). It's one of the most carcinogenic substances on earth and a neurotoxin - used for Sarin gas production and has replaced chlorine gas in warfare. It's also the basis of Prozac and in practically every supermarket dental product. I just shop at Satoriz and buy everything organic to avoid all of that nonsense. I drive to Albertville to find a Satoriz but I'm sure there's one near Grenoble.

>
> I'll be trying to read as much as I can on this subject soon so if I see anything interesting I'll report it. (Currently examining WW2 and reading a book by Sukorov - a top Soviet intelligence officer. It's called Icebreaker.) If you want any book references let me know. I also have Lipton's "Biology of Belief " to get through.

Ian: the idea that Down's Syndrome could be 'cured/helped' by that kind of intervention is fascinating but as you say it depends on the TIME of the intervention. I have been involved in something called Primal Therapy which attempts to 'travel back in time' so to speak and (this might sound weird but it really isn't) but with mixed results as far as I can see. This comes more from a 'psychology' perspective but I believe it can be a 'biology' perspective also. Anyway it is all very interesting, I suppose we could say the earlier the experience the more intense is the epi-genetic effect. I guess we could say for example in the womb epigenetics is firing away like mad and determining a lot about the future of the baby.

I find myself reading a lot of kind of 'odd' books also about history especially. 'Distracts' me from what is going on here and with Dr K or is it the other way around? It's all good I suppose. I just read Reza Azlan's book about Jesus "Zealot" which was very good and now am starting on his book on Islam. It's hard to ignore what is going on especially in the Middle East and I find history somehow soothing in that all this stuff we are dealing with has been a long time coming. Same in biology etc. I will check into "Biology of Belief"

This is so weird.............I just finished a book called "Pawns in the Game" by a guy called William Carr. Anyway it's about all this stuff and I found it extremely intellectually challenging (in that it contradicts a lot of what I thought I 'knew'). I guess the hardest thing for me to accept/understand is that the Bolshevik revolution was a 'capitalist' plan though the guy gives quite a bit of 'evidence' of that. It would probably be considered 'conspiracy theory' and 'anti-Semitic' but I have to say I found it extremely thought provoking and absorbing. An example of what it kind of changed my mind about was I always considered Franco the 'bad guy' in the Spanish Civil War, this guy makes out quite the opposite. Also he says Hitler never wanted to fight England but was sort of 'snookered' into it. Again he give plenty evidence of that.

As far as Lenin's theory/prediction we have already had 2 WW's and well on our way to a 3rd! And maybe if there was a WW3 there would be one world government whether 'Communist' or not. Things seem to be going that way. As for 'health' is concerned I think we would/will have more 'totalitarianism' as in compulsory vaccinations and a lot of other very bad stuff IMHO

Is Carr's book the only one of this genre that you have read so far? It's a solid reference and a good starting point. Yes, it's painful having a lifetime of brainwashing undone! The good thing is that like CT it's a pain that becomes very welcome. It seems to me that people in general go through two wake up phases in life (if any). The first is in their early thirties when they start to think a bit beyond their basic instincts and realise most progress is counter intuitive. The second is in their mid 50s or later - when they realise that they are being completely screwed by the system that brainwashed them since birth - and it's already too late to do anything about it. There's a lot I could say but it would be a little bit too hot for a public forum I suspect!

From a health perspective I don't think any of this stuff can be ignored if there is to be any hope of understanding why our world is so totally fucked up. One of the best books to give a clue is actually written by Henry Ford (His only book). It's boring to read - but essential. Written in 1920 when you go through it - it could have been written today. With books like that I use "text to speech" or audio book recordings - usually when I'm training outdoors. That way I avoid too much sedentary reading and tedious books flow far better when turned into audio.

### Ian — 2015-09-30T14:03:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178240>

I use a mobile phone. Sony Z1 compact (fully waterproof). The reader app is a paid for version of Moon Reader Pro.

I keep my books in the cloud using Google Drive (about 3000 of them) and just pull what I need into Moon Reader which has excellent organization facilities . Text to Speech is great because you also have the printed text for reference. Also it doesn't consume extra memory. The sound is very robotic but it also depends on how the author writes. Studio recorded audio books are far superior to listen to but consume memory, have no text version, use a music player etc.

I use 11 gram Bluetooth 4.1 earbuds too. They are lighter than copper cable standard earbuds and the BT4 is very low power. The tiny battery built it lasts 5 hours.

If you are worried about EMF you can go into airplane mode. I tend to go out for anything from 1 hour to 6 hours in training. If racing or working hard I wouldn't be able to listen to a book - Instead I'd have feedback from a laser HRM on my wrist - through the phone so it is audio feedback - along with pace, distance etc as necessary. When isolated I'd use rhythmical music - almost trance like music as it is one of nature's best painkillers. I use 90 bpm music sometimes for an optimal running stride.

Personally I'm not convinced about EMF having a significant impact on the body. The thing is that EMF is as intrinsic to nature as is matter itself - It's everywhere. Why say overhead electricity cables will harm you and then suggest using magnetic coils for sleeping on? High power microwaves will cook you so that's what to avoid.

Nicolai Tesla - the man who we owe our modern world to - used to use charges of thousands of volts passing through his body as a health boost! I can't see much sense there either but it never appeared to harm him. He was only harmed by the same international financiers that Carr wrote about!

### Ian — 2015-09-30T15:37:59+0300

Right. "Horrible brain" syndrome! We are getting very scientific here now.

I'm open to ideas but only in a true scientific way - and that's to pass them through a constant gauntlet of scepticism.

Tesla lost his funding when Astor died on the Titanic. Morgan then messed him around - he was championing Marconi who was given the patent for inventing radio. Eventually, posthumously it was returned to Tesla. Without funds Tesla went nowhere - horrible times perhaps but horrible brain I doubt.

Is there any medical documentation to back up the statement? To be honest I'm surprised he didn't fry himself but to this day I've seen no evidence that his experiments did any harm. He is far more likely to have been harmed by dioxins in transformer fluids and other harmful toxins.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-30T16:17:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178270>

Morgan bankrupted Westinghouse and that is when Tesla gave all his patents to Westing house free of charge to maintain the Niagra contract for AC generation. Morgan backed Edison and the DC generator and Niagra deal went to Westinghouse and Tesla. Morgan then used his influence on Wall St to get margin calls on Westinghouse and he that created the money crunch for Westinghouse. Tesla gave up his royalty rights to keep the science moving. It had little to do with Astor.

### Ian — 2015-09-30T22:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178311>

Good detail - but Astor was Tesla's financial backer. He was also passionate about science. Unfortunately he died on the Titanic. No doubt all the circumstances combined blocked Tesla. It's a terrible shame because who knows what he might have produced. Its also a shame he kept everything in his own head instead of writing it down. All his 300 or so patents were thanks to his staff.

He died of heart failure age 86 and I can find no information at all - either online or in my bookshelves - indicating he suffered from any I'll health. He avoided doctors like the plague - even after being run over by a car.

He pioneered xray development so it's remarkable that he did live so long.

Although Tesla here is being criticised my doubts about all the relevant the science are well summed up by the following (contradictory to your view) quote from Otto Warburg , Nobel Prize winner
"Cancer is 15 Millivolts of the cells, an aged cell is about 50 Millivolts and a normal cell is about 100 Millivolts The Tesla Coil is a Molecular Enhancer. Every cell in the body has a tiny battery in it. When that battery becomes low from stress, injury or disease then it will have electrical difficulty. This device charges the cells back up. This device powers the voltage back up in those cells so the chemical and enzymatic processes can work properly."

Tesla believed his exposure to EMF was beneficial to health. I doubt him there but it shows how sticking "Nobel Prize " before a name gives authority - right or wrong.

### Ian — 2015-09-30T23:54:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178319>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=178251)
>
> True.
> The only way to know for sure is to test it yourself... and the you know.
> Some can take more "poison" than others without noticing too much.... does not mean the poison is good tho........
> I know for me nnEMF does me no good and it is all I need to know to organize my life to my favor
> Maybe you want to read Going somewhere by Andrew Marino..... it really is a great book

Thanks for the book recommendation. I'll pass on it though because when I looked at the content on Amazon I found some very bad mistakes. (I'll return to that). I will look for other books however.

Certainly - if you are aware of something affecting you then you have to respond to it. We can be fooled however and the author of your book provides a classic example. To strengthen his case he cites what he calls a French doctor named Franz Mesmer who used magnets treat people. Well - for a start Mesmser was German and he never used magnets for anything. The word "mesmerise" comes from this episode in history. Mesmer was a medical doctor who discovered a way of treating patients without medicine that brought far greater success for almost everything (not a great surprise!!!). He became particularly famous in France and eventually worldwide. He would work with placing hands on or near people and what he referred to as balancing a life force - fluid in and around their body. The patient would normally go into convulsions and this would indicate that the cure was starting to work. He was a very pragmatic man and so he wanted to make his theory concrete so he named this mysterious substance "animal magnetism". He would "magnetise" an entire tree and have large groups each person holding on to a rope hanging down and he would "magnetise" all of them simultaneously and they would all go into convulsions. At the height of his fame he was confronted by an international scientific panel which included Benjamin Franklin. The panel concluded that there was no such thing as animal magnetism and that it was fraudulent to use the term. Franklin didn't want to harm Mesmer - but he was destroyed and ridiculed throughout the world. Unfortunately there was another big mistake! One of Mesmer's students continued (as they still do today in France - where they still call it magnetism) -the Marquis de Puysagor (I'm writing from memory so excuse any minor errors). The Marquis eventually realised what was going on - it was the discovery of hypnotism. Later on - from being a professional hypnotist - Freud discovered the unconscious mind - which led to "psychology". My point being - like the with the author of your book - we can fool ourselves very easily.

By the way - one day somebody brought a village idiot to one of the tree sessions and gave him a rope to hold onto. Being confused and uninterested he simply decided to have a nap and dozed off - causing everyone else to do the same. This was the origin of the idea to use "sleepiness" for hypnotic induction.

Bottom line is that your self experiments should give more questions than answers. Real science is rigorous and is neither a "consensus" nor "personal". It is relentless and open testing that makes theory into fact... _"If it could be demonstrated that any complex organ existed which could not possibly have been formed by numerous, successive, slight modifications, my theory would absolutely break down."_ \--Charles Darwin, Origin of Species

On the other hand Mesmer really had something to offer that was totally missed - and he killed many fewer patients that way too!

Epicurus (270BC) came up with the idea of "atoms". He was pretty much right but it took over 2000 years before that could be confirmed. I suspect however for each thing like this that turns out to be right there will be thousands that are wrong.

I believe you could argue that hypnotism IS magnetism since Becker proved that running a an electromagnetic current from front to back (instead of the usual back to front flow of electrons) can induce a change in brain function. Numerous experiements with magnets have shown that it can also induce anesthesia and an altered sense of perception. ### Barry — 2015-10-01T18:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178376>

Just to get this blog back to where its supposed to be:

Training Update

Ran 6hrs in the mountains with no food, just water.

Before: nothing, just black coffee.

After: not hungry. Ate about 2hrs after.

This is the ultimate measure of how fat/keto adapted you are. If you can exercise and go for long in the fasted state is one good sign. The second is if you do not feel the need to eat during. The ultimate sign is if you do not feel hungry after.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-10-01T19:05:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178377>

and the more purple light you absorb the further you can go..........without food.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-01T20:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178397>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=178376)
>
> Just to get this blog back to where its supposed to be:
>
> Training Update
>
> Ran 6hrs in the mountains with no food, just water.
>
> Before: nothing, just black coffee.
>
> After: not hungry. Ate about 2hrs after.
>
> This is the ultimate measure of how fat/keto adapted you are. If you can exercise and go for long in the fasted state is one good sign. The second is if you do not feel the need to eat during. The ultimate sign is if you do not feel hungry after.

Agreed, Barry. Although I do not do endurance exercise, I generally run sprints every other day and lift heavy six days a week (typical bodybuilding split routine.) I have been intermittent fasting (evening meal is generally my only meal) for 3 years now, and I work out entirely fasted (no BCAA's, but a shit ton of my new favorite very cold Icelanding spring water) in the early mornings, about 10 hours into my fasting period. I then continue fasting until about 6:30 p.m, so another 11 1/2 hours beyond my workout. Over the years, my evening caloric intake has gone from ENORMOUS to quite small. Last night, for instance, my meal was one pound of slightly browned grassfed ground beef (I like it raw, but my favorite is warmed enough to melt the fat) mixed with lightly sautéed shallots. This particular meal gives me a rather blissful satisfaction, as does lamb. And, of course, seafood!

This year and last, I have found that as the fall progresses toward winter, I tend to gravitate to meat-only meals. Last winter I would have occasional strong cravings for a very particular vegetable (something like green beans, asparagus, broccoli, or brussels sprouts), which I would then eat tons of at dinner until the craving was gone. I'm interested to see what happens this winter.

About a year and a half ago I experimented with a couple 48-hour fasts. My first experiment, about 12 hours into fasting I borrowed my daughter's mountain bike and biked 26 miles - after not having biked since my teenage years, and I only stopped because my nether regions were becoming numb. I had NO appetite for the rest of the day, so I decided to go another 24 hours. By the end of the second day, though, all I wanted was roasted chicken, with top priority being the skin! I ate two whole chickens, if I recall. I also lost about 5 lbs of body fat in that 48 hours, and over the course of the rest of the week, another 5 lbs. It was a pretty amazing experience, mentally and physically. Metabolically, it felt like I was running super fast (maybe too fast mentally, LOL) and super clean. Come to think of it, I might do it again here soon just for fun.

### Ian — 2015-10-01T22:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178403>

Are you sure you're not confusing hypnotism with ECT - electroconvuslive therapy? That's a highly popular medical use of electricity on the brain and works even better in places like Guantanamo. Perhaps this should be another thread as it's probably a never ending subject - and forever diverging!

Hansen Kenimer - 5lb of body fat in 48 hours - on a 26 mile bike ride? Sure it wasn't phantom weight loss - the system emptying out?
Why don't you do aerobic exercise?

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-01T22:31:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178405>

I sprint. And my "warm up" cardio is HIIT. I have some muscular imbalances I'm addressing due to two years of working a job where I carried something heavy in one hand all day. About a year and a half in, I realized I was developing that problem, but when I switched and starting carrying it right handed and working left handed, it was a little late in the game. So I'm not running anything but sprints until I'm straight again. Since I'm striking on my forefoot with a foreword balance when I sprint, I can run straight.

I'm actually wanting some rollerblades, though. I love rollerblading.

It was 10 pounds that week. I didn't put it back on. In fact, another few months later, I lost another 10 pounds in a week in a big whoosh.

### Joe Gavin — 2015-10-02T15:31:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178430>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=178376)
>
> Just to get this blog back to where its supposed to be:
>
> Training Update
>
> Ran 6hrs in the mountains with no food, just water.
>
> Before: nothing, just black coffee.
>
> After: not hungry. Ate about 2hrs after.
>
> This is the ultimate measure of how fat/keto adapted you are. If you can exercise and go for long in the fasted state is one good sign. The second is if you do not feel the need to eat during. The ultimate sign is if you do not feel hungry after.

digging this Barry. I'm no where close (or desire at this time) to the stuff your doing, but n=1 over the summer eating seasonal epi-paleo bears this out. specifically, i was rarely sore after weekly beer league softball games whereas last year I'd be crippled for 3 days (from playing, not drinking-well, sometimes).

Also, heat and humidity were absolutely no problem for me while folks were dropping like flies. Also did a cross-fit thing (row 1000 meters, 23 burpees over the rower, sprint 35 yards, drag 100 pound sled back -only did one leg) in about 6 minutes-recovered in about 90 seconds.

And I purposely haven't trained/exercised since January. It's pretty cool being 50.75 years old, just eating a ton of fat and seafood, controlling light, and being able to perform ON DEMAND better than folks younger, stronger (relative term).

Just doing some yoga shit now and find that I have no problem finishing the class and feeling good (no, I'm not good at yoga poses, but digging the breathing.)

May run a 5k with my best friend this sunday just for the fuck of it.

Cool shit all around.

### Barry — 2015-10-02T18:40:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178443>

> [Hansen Kenimer said:](/goto/post?id=178397)
>
> Agreed, Barry. Although I do not do endurance exercise, I generally run sprints every other day and lift heavy six days a week (typical bodybuilding split routine.) I have been intermittent fasting (evening meal is generally my only meal) for 3 years now, and I work out entirely fasted (no BCAA's, but a shit ton of my new favorite very cold Icelanding spring water) in the early mornings, about 10 hours into my fasting period. I then continue fasting until about 60 p.m, so another 11 1/2 hours beyond my workout. Over the years, my evening caloric intake has gone from ENORMOUS to quite small. Last night, for instance, my meal was one pound of slightly browned grassfed ground beef (I like it raw, but my favorite is warmed enough to melt the fat) mixed with lightly sautéed shallots. This particular meal gives me a rather blissful satisfaction, as does lamb. And, of course, seafood!
>
> This year and last, I have found that as the fall progresses toward winter, I tend to gravitate to meat-only meals. Last winter I would have occasional strong cravings for a very particular vegetable (something like green beans, asparagus, broccoli, or brussels sprouts), which I would then eat tons of at dinner until the craving was gone. I'm interested to see what happens this winter.
>
> About a year and a half ago I experimented with a couple 48-hour fasts. My first experiment, about 12 hours into fasting I borrowed my daughter's mountain bike and biked 26 miles - after not having biked since my teenage years, and I only stopped because my nether regions were becoming numb. I had NO appetite for the rest of the day, so I decided to go another 24 hours. By the end of the second day, though, all I wanted was roasted chicken, with top priority being the skin! I ate two whole chickens, if I recall. I also lost about 5 lbs of body fat in that 48 hours, and over the course of the rest of the week, another 5 lbs. It was a pretty amazing experience, mentally and physically. Metabolically, it felt like I was running super fast (maybe too fast mentally, LOL) and super clean. Come to think of it, I might do it again here soon just for fun.

good work Hansen..... the most important thing to do is keep connecting... I don't give a shit whether its HIIT or Endurance...... just keep connecting

### Ian — 2015-10-02T21:36:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178453>

Perhaps you don't need any advice! However a big imbalance (even a small one) can propagate into all sorts of problems - some even very hard for experienced professionals to figure out - but I'm sure you know this. Personally if I had any such issue (unless I misunderstood you) I'd avoid both endurance and HIIT until it was sorted out. I'd be more inclined (where appropriate) to do targeted strength and flexibility work and then build up other activity slowly. (Though I have been known to continue working on skis with broken bones - but only when released by the doc to do so!) In addition I wouldn't rely solely on my own judgement - especially if I'd put myself into the situation in the first place. I've certainly been in far more desperate sounding situations - leading to extreme agony and surgery - and only very much later realised it could all have been avoided with the right advice. Most people move inappropriately for most activities and the advice of a trained eye makes a massive difference.

By the way - today - as on most second days - I had a good breakfast and then fasted for 9 hours before a 12k run. I wasn't hungry before, during or after. However I believe the real measure of ketosis is found in measuring both ketones and blood sugar levels. When - like Dr Mike Stroud - you can drop to 0.2 mmol/l blood glucose and you are not only still alive but thriving and putting out a terrific work rate - then you know you are in ketosis.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-02T22:58:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178456>

Well, I certainly am careful. I have sought assistance in the form of rehab, but found it rather lacking. And prohibitively expensive. The most therapeutic and corrective thing I have discovered so far is simply walking (forward some but mostly backward) on one of the lane divider lines --which are less than 3 inches wide -- at the local high school track (both directions, of course), then progressing to running backward on the line. This seems to engage and strengthen all the right musculature in a whole-body sort of way. So far I am only running the lines backward on the straightaway....I haven't tried running backward around curves yet. ;-)

I did not (and still do not) face forward to run sprints until I can run on the lines backward at a good clip, and easily. I'm actually amazed at how much this has improved my speed. I also do this at my gym's indoor track as a warmup, and boy do people give me funny looks. But I saw someone else walking backward the other day, so maybe they're testing the method to my madness, LOL.

Oh, and last week I also started farmer's walks on the lines carrying a 45 pound plate in each hand.

### Ian — 2015-10-03T14:29:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178486>

Do you do those exercises barefoot? For about 7 years now I 've only run with minimalist shoes or even barefoot. The negative effect of built up cushioned shoes on gait and sensory feedback is horrendous. It's been a long slow process of self correction - aided by study - for me to adapt after a lifetime of unconscious bad habits. I found "Chi Running " by Danny Dreyer shares the most useful insights - though perhaps not obvious at first. I use Chi Walking when hiking now and if I have to climb back up a mountain to help a stranded ski client. The difference is phenomena. My girlfriend is a professional mountain guide and she does the same now. It's hard to believe that this is entirely missed by her profession. I've looked at many other sources of biomechanical theory and information but nothing is as insightful as Dreyer's application of "Chi" . And that's coming from someone who has a hard core science background!

By the way - running is just a side issue for me. Race cycling is my favourite sport, mountain biking second and skiing I do professionally.
