### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-05T21:59:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178671>

> [Ian said:](/goto/post?id=178459)
>
> Guess I'm lucky in France - anything serious is fully covered by the state. In fact with recovery and rehab the problem is getting out of it. They keep you as long as possible because the government pays them more the longer they have you!
>
> Maybe if you described your physical issue in more detail someone could suggest other useful ideas for you. I see that the fastest time recorded for a backward 5k race is 19 mins 31 secs! I'd probably currently struggle doing that forwards.
>
> Must be impressive seeing HIIT sprints backwards ! I sometimes ski backwards when teaching but last year managed to hit a snow canon - was filming at the time - made the kids day.

I had an "A-ha!" moment this morning as I was standing at the sink brushing my teeth. I load my feet very unevenly, certainly from carrying that heavy scoop every day for 2 years on the job. I put a shim in my shoe that dropped the outside of my left heel and voila! I was straight as can be. Wore it to the gym and made it completely effortless to squat straight instead of having to MAKE myself straight. Makes perfect sense, really. Vertical human + uneven loading of feet = all sorts of torso/hip rotation and compensatory musculature.

I'm so excited!

### Ian — 2015-10-05T22:54:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178673>

> [Hansen Kenimer said:](/goto/post?id=178671)
>
> I had an "A-ha!" moment this morning as I was standing at the sink brushing my teeth. I load my feet very unevenly, certainly from carrying that heavy scoop every day for 2 years on the job. I put a shim in my shoe that dropped the outside of my left heel and voila! I was straight as can be. Wore it to the gym and made it completely effortless to squat straight instead of having to MAKE myself straight. Makes perfect sense, really. Vertical human + uneven loading of feet = all sorts of torso/hip rotation and compensatory musculature.
>
> I'm so excited!

That's great! My job is about having those "A-ha" moments all the time - at least if it's being done right. The trouble with the feet though is they are very complicated - each foot having 26 bones, 33 joints and over 100 muscles, tendons and ligaments. I spent many years working on correcting the alignment of feet/boots/bindings with skiers - placing wedges all over the place and working out systems - and that's in addition to them having podiatrist built footbeds. In the end I realised I had to stop all of it! Sure it helps and it's all part of a learning process, but the best answer was unexpected. When I have a new race trainee I ask right at the start if they have corrective footbeds. If they do (usually costing from $100 to $300) I ask them to take them out of the boots and hand them to me. I look at them for a few seconds and then throw them away as far as possible. Usually they panic and run after them - but the point is made. The student instead has to learn to use the muscles in the feet actively - to become aware of them. Most people couldn't name any muscle in their foot let alone feel what they are doing with any of them. I was fortunate enough to attend a short training in Switzerland by a group of medical experts specifically on this subject - they called it "Spiral Dynamics" - but now it's untraceable - though that same name is seemingly used for many other unrelated things.

The simplest way to explain it is to place the outside edge of your foot on the ground. Keeping the foot on this edge stretch the ball of the foot towards the ground. You will feel a sort of tension across the foot. With practice (lots) your foot becomes more flexible and you can reach further towards the ground. When you are at your limit allow the weight to go on the foot and for the ball of the foot to take some weight - but with weight distributed between the ball of the foot, the outside edge and the heel. Maintain this active muscle tension. This will realign your legs entirely -especially with the anterior and posterior tibialis muscles - if there is an existing issue. For the massive forces involved in Alpine skiing the benefits are enormous. The difference between this conscious action and the training and awareness it brings - compared to the crutch of a medical support - is pretty easy to figure out.

However - when people aren't able to stay on top of this (due to many other things going on) then they still use wedges and footbeds! It's a very good temporary solution.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-05T23:36:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178675>

My thought was to use the wedge for about a week as the weak muscles strengthened, the overdeveloped ones rested a bit, and the necessary tendons/ligaments adapted, and then to try without and see where I am. I agree with you wholeheartedly about learning to do it without the wedge.

Alpine racing is something I wish I had done. I started skiing when I was 4...was blessed with some top skiing in my childhood as I grew up in Colorado between two ski resorts, Telluride and Crested Butte. About 13 years ago (after not having been on skis for about 10 years) I went home for a visit and spent a day at Telluride. They were having NASTAR races that day. I had never run any sort of course, but come to find out at the end of the day I had the 3rd fastest time and had earned a bronze medal, I think.

Thank you for the foot exercises! Now I have something to do when I'm sitting at my desk pining for the gym. ;-)

### Ian — 2015-10-06T14:06:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178702>

You are probably a good musician too - or could be. Sounds like you have very good body memory. Learning when very young has several advantages. First of all you don't really listen to instructors - you just get on with it! You have low weight so can't push through the air which means you don't accelerate uncontrollably. You have a far better power to weight ratio. Centre of mass is much higher (due to relative weight of the head) and that makes you much more stable (contrary to what people believe). You are a lot closer to the ground and lighter so falling (impact) isn't so dramatic (also - as for acceleration - less weight is spread over a proportionately greater surface area). You will tune into natural movements better and will be far less defensive than learner adults (for all of the other above reasons). You get colder as a child but don't feel it as much. (once again surface area to volume ratio). Once you have the right skill set though it's like riding a bicycle - you never forget. Perhaps some people get rustier than others but it's usually because they learned the wrong moves initially.

Memory is very odd! I simply can't remember how to spell many words, or forget people's names and even faces - but my physical memory (unconscious) is solid. I can pick up a violin after years of not playing and it's like I never put it down - and all the music is still in there. My girlfriend - who is a professional musician (as well as mountain guide) has the opposite sort of memory - fantastic for learning songs but she can't stop practising her instruments or she forgets how to play them and forgets the music!

I read in Dr Perlmutter's "Grain Brain" how exercise stimulates BDNF production (brain - derived neurotrophic factor) - as does a ketogenic diet. Perhaps this is how physical activity boosts its own learning? It's very clear how ketosis permits phenomenal physical endurance - so the link between the diet and exercise here appears to be relatively obvious. Just about any animal our equivalent size can overpower and out sprint us - even a chimpanzee is far more powerful - but we developed fur-less skin so we can sweat and cool down - we have incredible mechanics for efficiency in running - oddly enough using gravity for forward propulsion (as in skiing!) - but without even needing to go downhill. All of that allows us to hunt almost any animal on the planet into the ground just on our bare feet - and the improved brain lets us track them (we figuratively "shape shift" - imagining that we are the animal and predicting all its moves - probably enhanced by certain stimulants and even hallucinations). For me this is all a little bit missing from Jack's "Factor X" but should be part of it.

I can understand obese or injured people wanting to lose weight without exercise (where CT comes in) - but like "paleo" - it shouldn't become a religion.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-06T19:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178742>

My mother started us out snowplowing between her skis, with one of her poles held lengthwise in front of her for us to hold on to. I've never seen anyone else skiing with their children this way, so I don't know if she came up with the idea or not. If she did, it was brilliant.

Funny you should mention music! I am a classically trained pianist. I started playing at 6 years old. I also picked up the flute in the 6th grade, but only played through high school. I hardly have time to play anymore, but when I sit down at the bench I can play (from memory, songs I learned for competitions when I was high school age) for literally 3 hours. My favorite composer? Chopin. Although I also love (J.S.) Bach. But for me, they lie on opposite ends of the spectrum. There is so much freedom for lyrical interpretation with Chopin, and yet I appreciate the exactness and discipline of Bach. Mozart I find to fall somewhere in the middle. Funny how that seems to correlate well with their countries of origin, Chopin of course being French, Bach being German, I believe, and Mozart, Austrian.

I suppose I am unusual in that I am a balance of visual, auditory/verbal, and kinesthetic. This is wonderful for learning anything athletic, but even better for teaching it!

To get this thread back on topic at least a little bit, today I pulled trap-bar deadlifts, which I just added to my routine the week before last. I added 30 pounds to my lift since my last session on Saturday the 3rd and could have managed to add 50, I think. Pulled 155 on Sat and 185 today. I am quite pleased. The kicker? Food intake yesterday was a pound of grassfed beef and two beets at dinner. Gallon+ of spring water, with two quarts of that during my workout.

It's very clear that I am becoming water-powered. "Starvation mode," my ass!  Makes me wish I had a deadlift platform outdoors so I could pull in the sun and cold and be WATER, SUN, and COLD powered!

### Ian — 2015-10-07T12:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178796>

Probably the "heavy" music shuts down your Alpha brain waves and makes you more attentive! There is a strong biofeedback at that level. Alpha brain waves correlate to relaxation - and people making mistakes due to inattentiveness. Phonons look like a quantised vibrational behaviour at the molecular scale - corresponding to heat. I guess it's all about resonance at root. Those brain waves are circuit resonance on a macro scale. We could branch off at this point in many directions - I mean Tesla was convinced he could split Earth in two with just small timed pulses from his huge Tesla coil due to resonance. He did blow up an entire power station before they stopped him. His development of alternating current for efficient energy transmission is all due to resonance. Perhaps the convergence of quantisation and resonance in music is the "octave". On your piano the only interval that is designed to resonate is the octave - they are the only notes truly "in tune". You are aware I'm sure that a piano doesn't use a "music" scale - it uses a logarithmic scale! Guitar (and flute) are the same. The problem with music is that harmonics are generated with prime numbers - totally indivisible - so there are severe limits as to what sounds good when playing notes together. They get around it by fudging - tempering - the scales so the ear gets used to the problem within acceptable tolerance. Equal tempered scale - like the piano - is just a pure logarithmic scale with harmonic matching only on the octave. You said you don't find Bach as musical - but they only worked out the tuning used by Bach in 2005 - so his music sounded very different from how it is heard today. On my violin the solution is to use "extended 1/6th syntonic comma meantone" for tuning - with the flats played higher than the sharps. This gives a far better musical harmony without compromising melody. You can't do that on a piano because your sharps and flats are the same note. The teacher who left the information about the tuning I use to posterity was Leopold Mozart. I'm not classically trained by the way - just self taught - Irish/Scottish music. Music certainly kills pain and motivates. Probably as I suggest by disrupting certain brain circuit resonances rather than by stimulating them. Hence why non resonating scales are perhaps popular! Just Intonation (pure harmonics) sounds beautiful in melody - but you might not want to listen to it all the time. One example of Just Intonation is the Highland Bagpipes. Mongolian "throat singing" is also a natural polyphonic sound. I'll bet you don't listen to either when training. Actually I do listen to quite a lot of Just Intonation - with the Afro Celt Sound System - which has a trance like effect. It's my favourite training music perhaps in this case pumping up the Alpha waves and killing pain! Who knows? It's easiest to be mindful and focused however without music or audio books - just sweet outdoors nature and a positive voice in your head!

By the way - the reason most people (in the West) can't sing "in tune" is because the only scales they know (unless Chinese or Indian or aboriginal) are those equal division logarithmic scales - and the ear cannot work with them - the ear works with harmonics! That's why on the violin I changed the system I was using - because it's played by ear. Professional singers in reality learn to sing "out of tune". For me an outstanding voice is when the personality of the singer can overcome all the other instruments (like the bagpipes do) and impose natural resonance. You may have noticed how difficult it is to tune a piano! Even a guitar cannot be tuned by harmonics though a lot of musicians make the mistake of tuning with harmonics.

### Ian — 2015-10-07T13:21:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178801>

What's with gulping down all this water? You must be running to the loo all the time and risking hyponatremia! Are you really drinking gallons of water because you are naturally thirsty? Excessive water consumption kills.

Whereas the connection between fasting, ketosis and endurance appears to be clear - I'm not so sure about the connection with weight lifting. Perhaps if completely keto adapted (at least 12 weeks) the muscles would still work fine - or even better - but fasting combined with a non-keto adapted muscle system would seem to be inappropriate for heavy weight training. It takes at least 12 weeks in ketosis for the muscles to start using ketones properly - this is to protect the brain when food sources change and give ketone priority to the brain.

About 18 months ago I was fasting weekly - two to three days at a time - in a non-ketogenic state. The loss of muscle was a massive mistake. Even in ketosis you lose about 200 grams of muscle per day - and the muscle mass is protected by the ketosis. Intermittent fasting however is different - it's not deep fasting, more or less just letting the digestive system get through a complete cycle without loading it up again - around about 8 hours minimum. I only eat two meals per day - permanently - due to finding that IF is very natural and I'm not hungry between meals - especially when exercising. However I'm also in ketosis all the time. I also drink much less than before. When eating a carbohydrate based diet the body stores much more water (which is why people lose a lot of weight when fasting - as they lose the water - not fat initially) and salt - which seem to be tied up by the carbs. The other thing is to breathe through the nose - the breathing organ. You lose a lot less water during exercise. When cycling and drinking from a bottle you can drink and breathe at the same time when nasal breathing - that makes the issue crystal clear.

### Ian — 2015-10-07T13:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178803>

There is also the epigenetic aspect of ketosis and CT. It may be that much less training is required for useful muscle mass to develop in ketosis due to genetic expression. That seems to be the case when we look at indigenous populations who still have appropriate diets. Carbohydrate oriented diets seem to only be good for storing fat - which can be useful for survival but today is killing most people. It fits that if you are in fat storage mode then you don't want muscle burning it off.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-07T23:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178842>

I DO drink a lot of water during my workouts, yes. I don't drink as much, though, when it's smaller muscle groups I'm working. But on deadlift mornings it's at least 2 liters. (Probably still a liter and a half with other body parts, though.) Deadlifts work the entire body, and the largest muscle groups do the most work. The legs and glutes have to generate tremendous force when you pick up dead weight off the floor, especially when the weight weighs more than you do. I'm also unusual in that I do sets of 5 reps in touch-and-go fashion and never drop the weight after each rep but lower it slowly. Since I also have no lactic acid buildup, my rest periods between sets are very short, never longer than 2 minutes, even at maximal weights. All this adds up to a pretty massive energy demand.

Occasionally I will do a long lower-intensity cardio session, and I don't seem to need much water then. So I really think it's a matter of intensity.

What's interesting is that I rarely need to pee until I get home. Pretty sure I'm putting all that water to good use, and then flushing it out when it's no longer needed.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-07T23:42:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178843>

By the way, Ian, the foot exercise you recommended is excellent. You're right...better than the wedge insert in my shoe.

### Ian — 2015-10-08T14:18:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-178857>

Water consumption is interesting because there is a lot of conflicting advice. I suspect though that hydration is strongly influenced by diet. Perhaps it's a bit like vitamin C in a sense. We know that lack of vitamin C causes scurvy - but on closer examination that appears to be only when the diet is carbohydrate based.

Yesterday for example I did a 1hr 50min bike ride - 40k, 5000ft climbing - but although I was carrying 0.6L of water I didn't touch it. The sun was out and air temp about 25°C (77°F) but cooler in the shade. About 20 minutes after the workout I became thirsty and enjoyed a drink. I sweat heavily and even a head band beneath my helmet was not enough to stop sweat getting into my eyes.

I think one molecule of glucose needs 6 molecules of water for digestion - though maltodextrin doesn't need so much. I've no idea how this connects to storing water in the body and the detailed implications regarding salt. Although I live in the mountains and have a superb supply of water - with natural springs all around - the local administration treats all household water with chlorine gas - so I'm reduced to buying bottled water for drinking. I get a specific water that is naturally high in minerals - magnesium, bicarbonates etc. I know that in the US there is a lot of scamming by corporations selling just about anything as "spring water" - so I'd be vigilant there!

There used to be a lot of talk about how it was necessary to pre-empt thirst to avoid a drop off in performance - but this then led to people dying due to drinking too much and diluting minerals in the body. More recently the advice has been to listen to your body and drink when thirsty. Obviously, fasting and ketosis add another dimension and as usual there is no data. I often wonder what all those universities studying physiology, nutrition and sports science actually do with themselves.

Great that you caught on with the foot muscles! Most people don't seem to have enough body awareness to get that so easily.

[http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2015/07/08/hydration-drink-when-thirsty\_n\_7736610.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2015/07/08/hydration-drink-when-thirsty_n_7736610.html)

_According to an updated consensus statement on exercise-associated hyponatremia in the June issue of the Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, it's best to [manage water intake by simply drinking when you feel thirsty](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2015-06/wkh-wt062915.php). Their findings are relevant not just to endurance athletes but recreational exercisers -- that means you, summer cyclists and beach volleyballers -- as well.

"Using the innate thirst mechanism to guide fluid consumption is a strategy that should limit drinking in excess and developing hyponatremia," [read the new report](http://journals.lww.com/cjsportsmed/Fulltext/2015/07000/Statement_of_the_Third_International.2.aspx), which was developed at the 3rd International Exercise-Associated Hyponatremia Consensus Development Conference this year._

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-12T21:22:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-179237>

On Saturday morning for a change of pace, I hopped (fasted, as usual) on a stationary bike for my warm-up before lifting. Hadn't pedaled anything since summer of 2012. It was a warm-up, indeed...ended up being a 20-min spin on resistance setting 10 at 85-88% HR (158-160 BPM) at about 125-130 rpm. Remarkably, I had absolutely NO lactic burn whatsoever. I was not labored in my breathing. I could ALMOST breathe entirely through my nose, but I found I needed to blow off CO2 occasionally through my mouth.

Now, according to the paragraph below, it's "just not possible" to be functioning aerobically at this intensity, especially given the fact that I do NO aerobic training other than running 5-10 sec "sprintervals" a few times a week. (Although I didn't sprint at all last week.)

"It has been shown that at about 80-90% of maximum heart rate, there is no longer adequate oxygen supplied to working muscles. This figure is known as the **Anaerobic Threshold**, because, beyond this heart rate anaerobic glycolysis must be used to contribute the extra energy and lactic acid will start to accumulate in the working muscles. The actual percentage will vary slightly from person to person, but is relatively constant within the individual." [http://global-rowing-service.com/html/heart-rate\_training-zones.html](http://global-rowing-service.com/html/heart-rate_training-zones.html)

I suppose another option is that I am anaerobic at that intensity but over the years somehow (heavy lifting while fasted?) I have become one of these: "...**it is actually possible over time to increase an athlete’s anaerobic threshold. Additionally, it is certain that with training over several months and /or years, athletes can increase the tolerance of their muscles to the effects of lactic acid and continue to function at high intensities with levels of lactic acid that would stop an untrained individual in their tracks."** (same webpage) However, after my "warm-up" I went down and pulled deadlifts up to my 1 rep max, so I really wonder how that would have been possible unless I had conserved my muscle glycogen.

My hypothesis is that I have converted the vast majority of my 2B muscle fibers to 2A fibers and am now capable of sustaining aerobic capacity at high intensities. That, or my max HR is actually higher than expected, and I wasn't really performing at over 85% of it.

So what would be some good tests? Increase resistance, decrease cadence? Increase resistance, maintain cadence? I have no problems testing out the 90-100% "red zone." If you don't keep pushing it, you don't keep adapting. What does Barry say again, "The stimulus must resonate?"

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-13T20:12:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-179377>

So, this is interesting. After a second 20 min spin at 85-90% HR this morning, I added 10 pounds to my dumbbell rows and still got my 5 reps. That's definitely a significant jump. I was sorely tempted to move up to the 80's to test a 15-pound jump, but instead made the wiser decision not to reach. (Seems I'm finally learning.  )

Don't know anything else it could be. It's got to be the intense cycling improving my lifting strength. Which makes me think (as I've suspected for quite a while) that I'm not functioning anaerobically when I lift, seeing as muscular adaptation to resistance training involves a shift of the glycolytic type 2B fibers to oxidative, fatigue-resistant 2A fibers. This is seen in sprint training, too. I'm sure it's exponential when combining both in the fasted state. And ketotic.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-13T22:46:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-179407>

Also interesting....for the past two mornings, I've been having the "ghrelin growlies" fairly intensely post-workout, with occasional rumblings throughout the day. However, I've had no increase in hunger. Improved GH response to my workouts w/the spinning work?

Update: stepped on the scale this morning 5 lbs lighter since last week. Favorite, freshly washed jeans that I just wore on Saturday fit considerably looser today in the upper thigh/lower glutes, which is typically the fat area I have difficulty budging. I feel like the 20-min cycle work has shifted me into a new higher gear. Ever closer to being a zero-entropy system.

As an aside, I have read before that spinners have very high IMTG stores in their legs. So maybe my body is taking the subcutaneous fat and shifting it into IMTG stores?

### Hansen Kenimer — 2015-10-20T23:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-180010>

Leg muscles/Bainbridge Reflex? [http://www.combat-aging.com/maximum-heart-rate.html](http://www.combat-aging.com/maximum-heart-rate.html)

Makes sense if you consider increased perfusion of muscles with oxygenated blood a necessary adaptation to maximum-intensity aerobic exercise. The faster/harder your legs work, the more blood they'd require to maintain aerobic capacity, so the faster the heart rate would need to be in order to deliver it.

I'm seeing some crazy numbers in the bike's calculation of my MHR...

Edited to add: the Bainbridge Reflex appears to be the polar opposite (pun intended) of the mammalian dive reflex. Whereas the Bainbridge reflex involves increased heart rate, vasodilation, and increase blood flow/perfusion to the muscles, away from the organs, the dive reflex involves decreased heart rate, vasoconstriction, increasing blood flow/perfusion to the organs while directing away from the muscles. I can see great value in training both. Hmmmm....perhaps seasonally.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-05T15:24:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181198>

> [Danco3636 said:](/goto/post?id=180016)
>
> Yep. That is what I am finding out. The deeper we dive the more we discover.

Redox is a function of how much light we recapture from the environment once our cells empty their stores of ELF-UV from signaling. Now the type of light, meaning frequency is also key. This is the key to Van Wijk's work. Once you understand that every cell uses ELF-UV to signal then you immediately begin to realize there must be a way for the body to assimilate UV. There is. It is the eye, specifically the retina.

Consider the long range implications of the following for optimal performance:

The pupillary light reflex allows the eye to adjust the amount of light reaching the retina and protects the photoreceptors from bright lights. The iris contains two sets of smooth muscles that control the size of the pupil. These muscles are under autonomic control and they cannot be conditioned by behavior. They respond to characteristics of light. The interesting aspect of the retina surface is that it projects to the central grey matter where the PVN and parasympathetic control of the cranial nerves are housed segmentally in the brainstem.

-   The **sphincter muscle fibers** form a ring at the pupil margin so that when the sphincter contracts, it decreases (constricts) pupil size to limit light.
-   The **dilator muscle fibers** radiate from the pupil aperture so that when the dilator contracts, it increases (dilates) pupil size. to let more light in
-   To check the receptor sensitivity (retina) we should only use liminal amounts of light to check the system tonus. Bright light LED's alter tonus as laid out in Ubi 24.

Both groups of these smooth muscles act to control the amount of light entering the eye and the depth of field of the eye. This shows you that pupillary changes and accommodation changes tell us a lot about the local light environment. We clinicians need to monitor this in all out patients carefully too see truly what the retina is being exposed too. It is often different than what the patient reports.

-   The iris sphincter is controlled by the **parasympathetic system**, whereas the iris dilator is controlled by the sympathetic system.
-   The action of the dilator is antagonistic to that of the sphincter and the dilator must relax to allow the sphincter to decrease pupil size.

**_Normally the sphincter action dominates during the pupillary light reflex. Since nnEMF and blue light increase ROS this means pupillary change tells us about the output or fatigue of the PVN from our environment.

This means that our pupil and our accommodative powers are beacons to redox potential in our current environment. This is powerful information for anyone who lives around the alien sun of high color temperature blue/white light in our modern world. You might be surprised to know that Bernstein and Steinhaus showed in the 1920-45 that accommodative power is linked to longevity. My sense it is also linked to optimal performance. The tone and balance in the autonomic nervous system is key to being able to perform best. Ask any human trying to save its life when under attack. This is what the ANS is designed to do._**

This is powerful information for anyone who lives around the alien sun of high color temperature blue/white light in our modern world. You might be surprised to know that Bernstein and Steinhaus showed in the 1920-45 that accommodative power is linked to longevity. My sense it is also linked to optimal performance. The tone and balance in the autonomic nervous system is key to being able to perform best. Ask any human trying to save its life when under attack. This is what the ANS is designed to do._**

Get cold and outside often and as much as we can. Sunrises and sunsets are powerhouses.
Cold water swims in the sun, eyes open and cold . Then eat seafood after fasting.
My morning fast adapted morning routine.

### Barry — 2015-11-06T14:39:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181267>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181198)
>
> Redox is a function of how much light we recapture from the environment once our cells empty their stores of ELF-UV from signaling. Now the type of light, meaning frequency is also key. This is the key to Van Wijk's work. Once you understand that every cell uses ELF-UV to signal then you immediately begin to realize there must be a way for the body to assimilate UV. There is. It is the eye, specifically the retina.
>
> Consider the long range implications of the following for optimal performance:
>
> The pupillary light reflex allows the eye to adjust the amount of light reaching the retina and protects the photoreceptors from bright lights. The iris contains two sets of smooth muscles that control the size of the pupil. These muscles are under autonomic control and they cannot be conditioned by behavior. They respond to characteristics of light. The interesting aspect of the retina surface is that it projects to the central grey matter where the PVN and parasympathetic control of the cranial nerves are housed segmentally in the brainstem.
>
>
> -   The **sphincter muscle fibers** form a ring at the pupil margin so that when the sphincter contracts, it decreases (constricts) pupil size to limit light.
> -   The **dilator muscle fibers** radiate from the pupil aperture so that when the dilator contracts, it increases (dilates) pupil size. to let more light in
> -   To check the receptor sensitivity (retina) we should only use liminal amounts of light to check the system tonus. Bright light LED's alter tonus as laid out in Ubi 24.
>
> Both groups of these smooth muscles act to control the amount of light entering the eye and the depth of field of the eye. This shows you that pupillary changes and accommodation changes tell us a lot about the local light environment. We clinicians need to monitor this in all out patients carefully too see truly what the retina is being exposed too. It is often different than what the patient reports.
>
>
> -   The iris sphincter is controlled by the **parasympathetic system**, whereas the iris dilator is controlled by the sympathetic system.
> -   The action of the dilator is antagonistic to that of the sphincter and the dilator must relax to allow the sphincter to decrease pupil size.
>
> **_Normally the sphincter action dominates during the pupillary light reflex. Since nnEMF and blue light increase ROS this means pupillary change tells us about the output or fatigue of the PVN from our environment.
>
> This means that our pupil and our accommodative powers are beacons to redox potential in our current environment. This is powerful information for anyone who lives around the alien sun of high color temperature blue/white light in our modern world. You might be surprised to know that Bernstein and Steinhaus showed in the 1920-45 that accommodative power is linked to longevity. My sense it is also linked to optimal performance. The tone and balance in the autonomic nervous system is key to being able to perform best. Ask any human trying to save its life when under attack. This is what the ANS is designed to do._**

So in other words Jack, we are better off training outside in the open air rather than plodding along on some treadmill or weights machine in an air conditioned/brightly lit gym with blearing pop music ? likewise, we are better off resting come darkness and not being exposed to blue light/nnEMF

we know that of course

here's the thing.... I do this as do many others

while I am in total agreement that it optimises health....

how does it optimise performance ?

performance at elite/world class level is sometimes, in fact often, not healthy

I have been saying this for a long time now....

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-06T15:48:07+0200

how does it optimise performance ? Blue light makes singlet state of oxygen radicals that slowly destroy a body. Full spectrum sunlight while connect to earth make triplet state free radical that are optimal for signaling and they build tissues.

Simple.

All free radicals are not created equal.

This was shared in a coaching group I belong to. Unfortunately one of their conclusions was along other lines.

"They seems to be missing the link into the 5 stages of hunger while rejoicing in fat burning while effectively (and dangerously) promoting low carb diets with this science without realising that people will hit the wall earlier and that LC diets will put people at risk of hypoglycemia (and other health problems) - thoughts?"

The paradigmatic assumption is that keto and low-carb states always represent an allostatic (thus ultimately stressful) state - I have ceased discussing the particular point there because it seems once someone pulls out the 'allostasis' card then the discussion just stops dead in its tracks before it goes somewhere interesting.

### Barry — 2015-11-08T21:12:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181427>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181398)
>
> This one should open some eyes............[http://www.metabolismjournal.com/article/S0026-0495(15)00334-0/fulltext](http://www.metabolismjournal.com/article/S0026-0495\(15\)00334-0/fulltext)

Jack

the good:

they used long term LCHF subjects (~20 months) and high level ultra endurance athletes

they took muscle biopsies

LC athletes showed much higher rates of fat oxidation... running 3hrs @ 65% VO2 max

for some, that was a sub 3hr marathon pace

the not so good:

"nutrition" adapted but not cold adapted athletes

no difference in performance between LC and HC

so this is the key debacle - carb junkies will still claim that athletic performance is not improved

My conclusion:

its a step in the right direction

### René Borg — 2015-11-08T22:12:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181432>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181198)
>
> Redox is a function of how much light we recapture from the environment once our cells empty their stores of ELF-UV from signaling. Now the type of light, meaning frequency is also key. This is the key to Van Wijk's work. Once you understand that every cell uses ELF-UV to signal then you immediately begin to realize there must be a way for the body to assimilate UV. There is. It is the eye, specifically the retina.
>
> Consider the long range implications of the following for optimal performance:
>
> The pupillary light reflex allows the eye to adjust the amount of light reaching the retina and protects the photoreceptors from bright lights. The iris contains two sets of smooth muscles that control the size of the pupil. These muscles are under autonomic control and they cannot be conditioned by behavior. They respond to characteristics of light. The interesting aspect of the retina surface is that it projects to the central grey matter where the PVN and parasympathetic control of the cranial nerves are housed segmentally in the brainstem.
>
>
> -   The **sphincter muscle fibers** form a ring at the pupil margin so that when the sphincter contracts, it decreases (constricts) pupil size to limit light.
> -   The **dilator muscle fibers** radiate from the pupil aperture so that when the dilator contracts, it increases (dilates) pupil size. to let more light in
> -   To check the receptor sensitivity (retina) we should only use liminal amounts of light to check the system tonus. Bright light LED's alter tonus as laid out in Ubi 24.
>
> Both groups of these smooth muscles act to control the amount of light entering the eye and the depth of field of the eye. This shows you that pupillary changes and accommodation changes tell us a lot about the local light environment. We clinicians need to monitor this in all out patients carefully too see truly what the retina is being exposed too. It is often different than what the patient reports.
>
>
> -   The iris sphincter is controlled by the **parasympathetic system**, whereas the iris dilator is controlled by the sympathetic system.
> -   The action of the dilator is antagonistic to that of the sphincter and the dilator must relax to allow the sphincter to decrease pupil size.
>
> **_Normally the sphincter action dominates during the pupillary light reflex. Since nnEMF and blue light increase ROS this means pupillary change tells us about the output or fatigue of the PVN from our environment.
>
> This means that our pupil and our accommodative powers are beacons to redox potential in our current environment. This is powerful information for anyone who lives around the alien sun of high color temperature blue/white light in our modern world. You might be surprised to know that Bernstein and Steinhaus showed in the 1920-45 that accommodative power is linked to longevity. My sense it is also linked to optimal performance. The tone and balance in the autonomic nervous system is key to being able to perform best. Ask any human trying to save its life when under attack. This is what the ANS is designed to do._**

It struck me when you wrote this that the balance between the two nervous systems is almost surely one of the proximate causes of the massive decline in running performance in the West although most are blindsided to this (no pun intended).

The anaerobic system is linked to the stress response and in turn to the sympathetic nervous system and that in turn will make sympathetic muscle tone dominate to a degree beyond what I would speculate was normal.

The drop-off in athletic performance for runners in the West is so shocking that it should raise more questions than it does. We are celebrating times and improvements because people have no other measure of success than the people running directly against them - so it is a case of 'the sick racing the sick' most of the time (even our few elites barely see the Kenyans as all but a handful of 'First World' athletes will finish 5-10 minutes after the East Africans (i.e. - they are not in the same race).

Everything I read, see and hear - here and in other places - seems to conspire to suppress parasympathetic muscle tone and dominance of the aerobic system (and further down the line the efficiency of it) - even the evolution of how we train described above in 'Tragedy of the Commons' is a psychosocial maladaptation to a false perception of the world. We see today young, strong and healthy men celebrating running 16:30 for a 5 km race as a very strong time - good enough to win local races - and it is a time beyond most of the runners you will meet. Yet, go back 30 years and a fit man would be ashamed of running such a time. We only need to look at the juniors in countries less 'compromised' - 14-year old Kenyan female athletes will run this sort of time if they are anyway talented and they will have beaten that by almost 2 minutes by the time they are 17 or 18. Such a staggering difference cannot simply be explained away by 'pretty good genes' and 'pretty favourable environment' and 'decent running culture' .

The greatest show-stopper for athletic performance in the West is injury - in a leading Dublin club, after only 3 races of the cross-country season, 11 out of 17 of the senior squad are side-lined with injury. In my experience that is the rule - not the exception. I noticed early in my coaching how many athletes with great natural promise (i.e. guys who could run 32 minutes for 10 km with little training) would get injured and be out of the sport within 2-3 years. Kenya produces hundreds of these. We produce only a handfold - and most of these we lose to injury. As a historical interest compare the current injury situation at Liverpool FC in England (in short: horrific) with Bill Shankley's 1960ies Liverpool side which played 50 plus matches per season and barely had ANY injuries.

So two questions I am very interested in:

1\. What 'healing resources' does an 'optimal lifestyle' free up which could help our next generation of athletes fulfil their potential without showstopping injuries - and how do we implement it in a world that we cannot fully turn back the clock on?
2\. Is our understanding of the aerobic vs. anaerobic system debate completely misunderstood (I believe it is).

today we equate certain race intensities with certain heart rate with certain metabolic systems being dominant. But it is forgotten that heart rate, sympathetic activity and anaerobic activity is a gauge of the level of threat the body perceives from the intensity of the work it is exposed to. How accurately the body reads this threat level depends on the quality of the sensory system coming in. There is a reason injured athletes can rarely perform in a sustainable way - because the threat level of any activity is inflated. it has nothing to do with the efficiency or adaptation of the energy systems - these systems are purely ramped up because the brain perceives a greater threat than normal. This can be very misleading: you can draw conclusions that a person is 'aerobically unfit' because the particular exercise he engages in is interpreted as a massive threat by the brain (or central governor or whatever we wish to call it). Simple example: some athletes perform very well in one area (say roads) but the moment there is a hill they blow it. This is often put down to 'lack of specific adaptation' but often I have seen this is simply not the case (the athlete did plenty - if not almost too much, specific work) - the problem is that their subconscious mind has been conditioned to perceive this particular activity has more threatening than roads (for whatever reason - perhaps the first race was a disaster). You see this in boxers who get beaten up early when they go in the ring - they develop negative defensive reflexes under pressure with over-reliance of the anaerobic system and inability to be sufficiently relaxed for efficient movement.

I feel that in a world where our brain perceives less threats from the environment (whether light, stress, nnEMF etc.) we will see less reliance on the anaerobic/sympathetic axis because our bodies will simply not be as threatened by exercise - even very high intensity exercise. Low energy individuals (low redox potential) should also be expected to show 'over-reaction' to high intensity exercise - it just makes sense - why force heavy activity on a body that feels it is starving for energy. The conscious mind will say 'I want to do this' but the subconscious will be screaming 'don't kill me' and put the brakes on.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-08T22:13:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181433>

There is so much horseshit in the performance literature and forums I stay away. That is Barry's battle to deal with. I am his brother in the battle but the argument being made shows you they are worried about being wrong with carbs. Carbs in a blue light environment at deadly. Most of Barry's guys are not really indoor trainers so the effect might not be as big in them but the endurance side create massive proton flows and not electron balance.

When ambient EMF rises in any environment, allostasis is completely lost because the tone is lost in the parasympathetic side of the ANS. All endurance guys operate in the sympathetic side and this is why ketosis makes so much sense. Cold and ketosis are beta 3 agonist gameplan.

What is inflammation any way? It is the excess of protons and/or loss of electrons at your surfaces. The surfaces being the eye, gut, and skin. So inflammation sounds like the final common pathway of nrf2 and NFkappa beta..........all tied back to calcium efflux. Calcium is the concrete for your cell membrane lipid bilayer when you are a eukaryote endurance athlete or not. It keeps them welded together to act as a native EMF antenna. All paths lead to low plasma volume and loss of RBC mass decreasing the zeta of the plasma and dehydration and loss of divalent atoms in cell membranes and plasma at once.......endurance steepens the loss.

That volume and chemistry change is sensed by the 7 areas in the brain with no BBB........most important one and first one affected is PVN........but when you use carbs to fuel your engine the gut becomes bad slowly....This alters the tonus in the vagus nerve which is parasympathetic for the gut. This projects to the area postrema and the balance is really thrown off in endurance because the third surface interaction with light is now incoherent and off kilter......namely the gut. What connects that surface with area postrema? Vagus nerve. Stress breaks down all barrier or surface chemistry and this alters the interaction and direction of energy flows from the environment to the cells below........surface chemistry dictates what happens in the deep layers in our tissues. This is why this study is really bad news for these folks. Barry said recently in this thread that endurance is never good for optimal longevity and his guys are not interested in this but that idea is flawed. If it is not good long term it is not optimal short term either. You may not feel the effects in performance but you will biologically. This still shortens your window for performance.

You can't out supplement, out hack, out exercise, or out eat a bad environment.........that is the story of the ubiquitin series. It's nature's axiomatic rules of engagement.

I've told Rene here before that the endurance guys understanding of the Randle cycle is superficial. Very superficial. They have ZERO clue that light and not carbs control cortisol and carbohydrate metabolism and hence there is a huge link to adrenal fatigue which they are live with chronically. This causes muscle fibrosis eventually under chronic carbohydrate consumption.
Consumption of carbs will stimulate the ultradian cycle of cortisol production. This occurs because presence of carbohydrates are associated with the longer light cycles seasons from an evolutionary timeline. they grow in long light cycles but when you eat them your RPE in your eye must also be getting the UV signal to not cause inflammation in the brain to alter the allostasis in the ANS.

Light is the most effective way to alter your cortisol levels, because of its dramatic effects on the circadian cycle. If you don’t believe it, try eating carbs in the winter for adrenal fatigue. This is usually an epic failure for patients and athletes clinically, because the light cycles are stronger stimulus to allostatic control of cortisol levels than carbs are. Why does this occur, you ask? Because sunlight or bright artificial light are potent effectors of the cortisol pathways. The cortisol awakening response is highest at sunrise, due to release of cortisol from the hippocampus in the brain. It is the hormone that wakes us up daily. It is lowest at night when light ends. Bright light with UV on the skin diminishes cortisol if the dose of light is high, while darkness seems to lower it as time increases in it. The cortisol awakening response is larger when people wake up in light rather than darkness. Therefore, light is the most effective means of controlling our cortisol production that we can use clinically.

So until that time you the clinician or athlete must realize that the patient is trading wellness for time............and what endurance guys believe is the can condition the ANS..........NO YOU CANT. It is physiologically impossible. You can condition other parts of the CNS and PNS but not the ANS. It responds directly to light stimulus at surfaces.

There is no set point for metabolism; too many patients and clinicians however, believe otherwise and this is why diet is their first tool. Metabolism is on a sliding scale of natural selection built into ubiquitin rates. Ubiquitin is the interaction of light with nitrogen in proteins.. Its boundaries are limited only by the how we account for and assimilate and contain the electrons from our environment.

Set points are creations of concrete reductive minds. That is not the work of evolutionary biology nor is it following nature’s law’s.

If you believe the nonsense about food and metabolism ask yourself this: At what age do you hit your set point? The concept is completely vacuous. It confuses negative and positive feedback loops with an attempt to attain a steady-state outcome. I spoke about this in webinars and in the ubiquitin series at length. Loss of negative feedback is deadly in a far from equilibrium state, why? In that scenario when control is lost both prey and predator die. For example, when a patient gets Candida in their gut from any stressor.......you've lost predator and prey in your microbiome, the biome gets smaller in number and species, because your surface chemistry is destroyed in the gut.

In a non-linear system, there may be no equilibria or there may be many. Either one is possible. That makes it fundamentally quantum. Possibilities exists until the waveform is collapsed by the environment you allow. I think the 900 pound plus people we have in the endurance world have gone into a zone of the non-linear dynamics where the negative feedback loops in our cells ceases to limit inflammation/fat mass as a protective mechanism of a toxic environment to our surface chemistry.

### Joe Gavin — 2015-11-09T17:35:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181495>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181398)
>
> This one should open some eyes............[http://www.metabolismjournal.com/article/S0026-0495(15)00334-0/fulltext](http://www.metabolismjournal.com/article/S0026-0495\(15\)00334-0/fulltext)

found this section fascinating:

_"A provocative finding was that LC athletes appeared to break down substantially more glycogen (>100 g) than the total amount of carbohydrate oxidized during the 3 hr run. This was the case in all ten LC athletes. Why would athletes with high rates of acetyl CoA generation from fatty acids bother breaking down muscle glycogen if those carbons are not terminally oxidized? Although speculative, we believe the reason may be to provide a source of glucose for the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) and a source of pyruvate to form oxaloacetate. The PPP generates important 5 carbon sugars and reducing power (i.e., NADPH) for biosynthetic reactions such as ribonucleic acid synthesis and maintaining glutathione levels, respectively. Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate and fructose 1,6 bisphosphate generated from the PPP can also enter glycolysis after the energy input stage, thereby generating ATP through substrate level phosphorylation and increasing formation of pyruvate. Pyruvate may be necessary in a keto-adapted athlete for two reasons. First, pyruvate can be used as an anaplerotic substrate by pyruvate carboxylase to generate oxaloacetate. At the onset of exercise several TCA cycle intermediates increase in concentration including fumarate, citrate and malate, however oxaloacetate remains low. Thus, in the keto-adapted athlete glycogen breakdown may be necessary to ensure a constant source of oxaloacetate for optimal TCA functioning. Second, pyruvate can be converted to lactate or alanine in muscle, which may then serve as gluconeogenic substrate for the liver."_

It is truly fascinating how the body uses ALL the pathways available to it to maintain stasis.

"From 1966 to 2004 it was reported 1,101 cases of sudden death in athletes younger than 35 years, 90% were due to cardiovascular diseases, 50% presented anatomic congenital cardiac diseases and cardiomyopathies, 10% presented early coronary atherosclerotic disease, 40% occurred in athletes younger than 18 years and 33% below 16 years old, the ratio of female/male was 1/9. "In the United States from 1980 through 1995, a total of 1318 deaths were attributed to lightning, (average: 82 deaths per year {range: 53-100 deaths}). Of the 1318 persons who died, 1125 (85%) were male, and 896 (68%) were aged 15-44 years. The annual death rate from lightning was highest among persons aged 15-19 years (6 deaths per 10,000,000 population; crude rate: 3 per 10,000,000). The greatest number of deaths attributable to lightning occurred in Florida and Texas (145 and 91, respectively), but New Mexico, Arizona, Arkansas, and Mississippi had the highest rates (10.0, 9.0, 9.0, and 9.0, respectively)."

### René Borg — 2015-11-12T13:00:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181761>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181706)
>
> Metastable is a better term. Anti fragile I am not on board with.

So just to try and put that side by side:

\- Metastable: stable provided it is subjected to no more than small disturbances.
\- Antifragile: has more upside than downside from volatility

The difference seems small - metastable seems to be close to Taleb's definition of robust or resilient.

Is your issue with the term that although organic systems thrive on small shocks generally and thus 'get better' (adaptation), over the course of a life-time the end result is always the same: death (i.e. our bodies may improve in some sense from training but over the course of a lifetime it get's weaker no matter what we do - what may change is the rate of reduction of function of certain systems). if interpret that one step further: is your trouble with specialised endurance athletics that although you can gain beneficial adaptations in some systems - some general and some specific - that an overspecialised pursuit extracts a cost in other systems (shortening of telomeres) and you do not think this trade-off is worth it because it may accelerate the velocity towards terminal station?

Of course, in the end we all end up dead

His motivations are optimized for transformation of others. He does things for himself first and foremost.

As for his term "anti fragile"; it is a total misnomer to me. We have to be fragile because natural harmony with seasons requires destruction and damage with renewal. We have to come apart to so we don't fall apart. So I think my term is far more accurate for biology. His term is more about stamping financial ideas onto a biologic billboard and saying some interesting. I am not interested in being interesting just accurate.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Now lets talk about why I like metastable. A dose is not a dose across the board Rene. Let me explain. In physics or economics the defintion of dose is similar. In biology the definition of dose is quite different. In physical systems there is never any regeneration. The sun burns hydrogen until it comes to equilibrium with the atoms it has left. Once it gets to iron it burn out and change its physical form. Things in physics fall to equilibrium and gain entropy as they do so. In biology we never fall to equilibrium until death. This means in biologic systems there is never a time where entropy can win. That is because biology controls the fall to randomness by making sure there is always a balance between repair and damage. See in financial markets, like physics they act randomly. Nothing in biology is random. It is ordered at the edge of chaos. That is why I favor the definition of metastability because it imparts the idea that we are designed to change every moment based upon the redox changes we face.

Nature invented biological clocks before we invented mechanical ones. It's just that like everything else, we copied nature and did a relatively poor job. The same is true in financial markets with all the mathematics designed to time the market changes. Nature's oscillators are adaptive and pay attention to the environment--ours are rigid. In financial markets they are contrived by market makers like Taleb. Which is why I find him disingenuous and why his term is so bothersome. But synchronization (clocks) are necessary to coordinate the sequence of complex events that are separated in the distances in space. If biological clocks fail, cellular signaling becomes uncoordinated and breaks down. Basic chronobiology. If human clocks fail, civilization can't function except in very small localities. This is not true in physics or markets.

We die a fraction every day and by degrees we’re reborn into different people, older people in the same clothes, with the same scars. There is no perfection in this life, only beautiful versions of brokenness. The key is realizing we need damage to repair. The DC electric current is the key to all repair and it comes from UV/IR light. Blue light is the ultimate destruction tool of nature because of its ability to carry power and to bend easy. This makes failure or damage the pivot foot in change. That is metastability in my view.

"_The key is to make the body consume the so-called fat stores during a long cycling race lasting six or seven hours before the body starts to consume the glucose stores. These can then be saved for the final sprint. That is why Kristoff's team has worked systematically over several years to increase his fat burning._" (my translation)

### René Borg — 2015-11-13T12:33:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181891>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181867)
>
> Devin sent this to me:[https://www.t-nation.com/training/death-of-steady-state-cardio](https://www.t-nation.com/training/death-of-steady-state-cardio)

The author of the article starts off on the wrong track: no one in running, in athletics or involved in performance coaching for endurance sees endurance training as primarily a fat-loss tool - the aritcle goes nowhere from there. Many bodybuilding and fitness coaches tend to fail to grasp this as the article points out, also failing to control for multiple variables and attacking a simplified strawman - lazy lazy. Runners are well aware that it is more important how you live and eat than how much you exercise once you get beyond the people who just drift into the activity (rather than the sport) pushed there by the old fat-loss paradigm (i.e. 'Coca Cola wants us to believe that doing a workout is great and we cna keep sipping the Coke). Running tends to form a great keystone habit in driving the incentive - I am a point in case - I would still sit in the pub drifting from the two default states I had from 15 to 27 - drunk and hung-over, if I hadn't discovered running. Once I discovered that, it opened up all the other doors. It is a crucial part of our evolutionary heritage (the Running Man hypothesis) and one of the sports were we can actually outperform most other land mammals. Does that mean it cannot be done incorrectly ('chronic cardio' as it is studied is done at the wrong intensity by and large) or that it cannot be done to excess (i.e. 'the Achilles choice - do you want to live a short life and be remembered forever or a long one and be only a brief memory' - obsessive commitment to any art - whether running or otherwise has a price). Great progress - for the species - was often achieved by the price paid by individuals overspecialising, they paid the price and we gained at their expense. When someone goes and breaks the 2 hour marathon barrier should we shake our heads and wonder 'what a stupid guy' or should we say 'marvellous, a new frontier in human performance has been broken'. Should we lament that this 'sub-2 hour man' has cut a few years off his life potentially or should we applaud the risk and sacrifice he made to show us what is possible. You could construct this argument for any pursuit - not just running.

Are there more important pursuits in the world than running? Of course, you can find many that will, on the net, increase human thriving more. But you will find far more pursuits that detract from the sum of human well-being.

As an athletics coach I only need to look around at the smiling faces, the sense of community, the deep connection people feel to this primeval motor pattern, to know that running as a sport is a force for good. This doesn't mean we cannot polish off some edges. It also doesn't mean it is the solution and cure to all ills (it's not).

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-18T02:14:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-182232>

And when you consider the implications of what I just said above, how we do studies has a massive impact on what we all believe. I believe light and temperature are the major factors in biology. I said this in CT 6 blog, long ago and got made fun of by people with limited knowledge of these effects. Biochemists have issues accepting this idea because of what they are taught. When The UK book by John Joy and Al Khalili came out you'd think that would get people looking at this closer. It did not in ancestral health. Barry likes Robb, and I hate half truths, so there in lies Barry's irritation with me. I love discomfort. Not every one does.

Now this: So if Cold Thermogenesis is really hormetic as the Wolf says, why is it that this one variable can cause biochemistry to shift in an experiment. Why? Because biochemistry is thermoplastic just like photosynthesis is. See the biochemists out there, with the exception of the guys mentioned in Jim Al Khalili's book, know that surface temperature changes the biochemistry at deeper tissue levels because it is a quantum effect. It turns out mice have lower inflammation and this is capable of altering scientific data, can you imagine when they finally realized the very same mice are also kept in high color temperature light 24/7, yet they are nocturnal mammals? You think that is a problem our paleo superstars planned for? Well I did. And I said so a long time ago in CT 6. The light environment will have a far bigger impact they never account for in any experiments. This was the point I made in the CT 6 blog post years ago. Biochemistry is thermoplastic and anything that alters temperature has fundamental changes on electrons and protons in mitochondria. Now we have proof I was right and that somebody was selling half truths to lemmings. [http://www.the-scientist.com/?articles.view/articleNo/44486/title/Cold-Mice-Skewing-Data-/](http://www.the-scientist.com/?articles.view/articleNo/44486/title/Cold-Mice-Skewing-Data-/)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-19T19:53:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-182361>

When people talk diet and food they must talk about light and there is my rub with the ancestral leaders. Color = frequency of light. So what type of sun you train under will give you the idea of what you think it best. When you train outdoors you begin to see a world Barry senses. When you train indoors you are low in dopamine in the eye and they eye sends that message to the frontal lobes and you think you need carbs to perform. IT is a light story. Where you train matters deeply. When hacking color of light for any reason at all know this about the color ratio of the light source: Sun has equal and quite balanced ratio’s of blue, green, and red, while Edison bulbs have massive amounts of red, medium amount of green like the sun, and a much smaller amount of blue compared to the sun. LED’s and fluorescents have massive amounts of blue compared to the sun, the same amount of green, and a very tiny amount of red compared to the sun. This alone should make you question every study done under artificial lighting in science.
Not one nutritional study controls for color temperature.........NOT one.

### Barry — 2015-12-03T16:22:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-183169>

enough squabbling ...

Training Update:

so once you have deeply fat adapted, fasted, LC, keto, etc..... the requirement to do it reduces... yet you can still maintain the adaptations

previously, I would train fasted 5-6 times per week.... big mileage... suffering ... 2 meals a day

I now don't need to do this anymore... yet I still have what I need

now I do it on feel... and for convenience

for example... I am CTing a lot now.... sea swims... cold weather. I like doing it early, pre breakfast. Recently I had a pro cyclist with me, Svien Tuft, a legend in the sport and truly primal [http://cyclingmagazine.ca/sections/...ration-are-matters-of-the-mind-and-the-heart/](http://cyclingmagazine.ca/sections/news/for-svein-tuft-recovery-and-tour-de-france-preparation-are-matters-of-the-mind-and-the-heart/)

so we CT... do some movnat after... then drive straight to the mountains and run for 2hrs. Snow on the summit and we ran barefoot. No foods, no water. We finished up about 2pm and then just sat out on the grass and had a beer.

This isn't being studied in the lab. Nor is it being practiced by the self confessed guru's. They might claim things but very few are doing it.

Stressors are information. I do think a period of unmeasured deep stress is needed to really see the woods from the trees. After that, a punch is just a punch, a kick just a kick

"in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

### Paul Fendler — 2015-12-30T17:26:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-185118>

Barry...excellent article. Your perspective comes through crystal clear. There's nothing like firsthand experience to turn knowledge into wisdom.

Ideally...someone dispensing advice on human performance has not only been a participant...they have also worked with many, many others...either as a coach or advisor or whatever...and has closely observed the experiences of many others. You need a large data base of experiences. That combined with a knowledge of the science involved gives your advice usefulness. But even with all that...you need an open mind...a willingness to dump everything you ever knew.

I'm a big fan of both you and Jack. Phil Maffetone was mentioned a while back. He was my coach and primary health care practitioner back in the 80's. In 1989, I ran a 100 miler on 100 certified 1 mile loops on virtually nothing but water in 14:11 (7:04, 7:07) so I connect with your experiences with running long distances with very little carbohydrate.

The thing about "skin in the game"...it has to be clear what "game" is being talked about. If it's about athletic performance in long-distance endurance events, you have a lot of skin in the game. If it's about how an 85 year old with arrythmia rehabs after a hip operation, you don't. Well, at least I don't think you do. So often the problem is "experts" wander into areas where they really shouldn't be acting like experts....if that makes sense.

I feel if someone wanders into your area and starts making claims that do not jive with your experience, it might be best to simply communicate to them just that. Tell them what they are saying is not jiving...and discuss why that might be. There's no need to let it bother you.

Thank you kindly for all your wonderful contributions.

Barry, thanks very much for the encouragement.

I had a couple of decent ultras way back when but I hadn't a clue how to recover from them so I drifted away. You are a million miles ahead of me in both your accomplishments and your understanding of things. I did do thousands of miles of fasted state training for several decades which yielded consistently good results for a long time. It wasn't until fairly recently...when my mother had a serious car accident followed by a slew of health problems and emergencies...and my brother imploded with a severe case of OCD...that my stress levels went though the roof, my performance declined and I began to notice compromised health. It has lead me here...not only to help myself...but also to find ways to help my mom and brother and others close to me.

I just watched the video about Wim Hof. Mind-boggling to say the least. I know you are using CT...but are you using any of the breathing techniques he teaches?

### Barry — 2016-02-09T17:20:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-188622>

Training Update:

I see nothing but addition these days in the fitness world. I recently wrote a 30 page report for a client, competitive runner, detailing everything from fat adaptation to mitochondrial biogensis to circadium rhythm to PVN.... I got one question back... "whats the best protein shake to use"...

Its the bullshit society we live in today where everything to improve our health and fitness is about what we can add.... what we can add... that costs money.... that provides comfort.. that removes any effort... that is a one stop magic bullet

I see this everywhere... the quantlet is just another example of this.... I've already discussed this with Jack

Rather... when you look deeper... much deeper... and you aren't afraid of discomfort.. and want to put in effort... and realise that time and patience are required... you realise that substraction brings gains.. it adds to your life

so now, for example... I don't use protein shakes, or D-ribose or exogenous ketones etc etc.... I eat real food, I get cold, I train fasted, I suffer and I wait...

In fact, as each day passes... I am removing way more from my life yet I am gaining much more.

People are blinded to this today.... and I personally think its one of the main reasons for failure.

We're creating fragility.

another rant over.....

### Barry — 2016-02-23T19:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-189661>

Similar to my "rant" , what happens when we try to add more

this from Nassim Taleb....

"_When people get rich, they lose control of their preferences, substituting constructed preferences to their own, triggering their own misery. And these are the preferences of those who want to sell them something (a skin-in-the-game problem as their choices are dictated by others who have something to gain, and no side effects, from the sale).

I've mentioned here the following anecdote. I once had dinner in a Michelin-starred restaurant with a fellow who insisted on eating there instead of my selection of a casual Greek place. People in the restaurant were of the very constipated style, that kind of atmosphere. Dinner consisted in a succession of complicated small things. It felt like work. I left the place starving. Now if I had a choice I would have had some time-tested recipe (say a pizza with fresh ingredients, or a juicy hamburger) in a lively place --for a tenth of the price. But because the fellow could afford the expensive restaurant, we ended up the victims of some complicated experiments by a chef judged by some Michelin bureaucrat (one that would fail the Lindy effect, instead of eating some minute local variation around a progress through Sicilian grandmothers).

(Footnote: Hamburgers are tastier than filet mignon, because of the higher fat content, but people have been convinced that the latter is better because it is more expensive to produce).

The same with real estate. Most people, I am convinced, are happier in close quarters, in a real barrio-style neighborhood, where they can feel human warmth, but when they have big bucks they end up pressured to move into a humongous impersonal and silent mansions, far away from the neighbors. Not counting the fact that their house will be professionally managed like a corporation.

I am saying this because, with the passage of time, I am more and more convinced that very few people understand their own choices, and end up being manipulated by those who want to sell them something. Looking at Saudi Arabia which should progressively revert to the pre-oil level of poverty, I wonder if taking away some things from them will make them better off."_
